Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: A travel agent displays a holiday package in its window priced at £599 per person. Sarah enters and says 'I would like to book that holiday for two people.' The assistant checks the system and states the price is now £699 per person. Sarah insists she is entitled to the displayed price. Which of the following best describes the legal position?

options.A: A contract has been formed at £599 because Sarah accepted the displayed price.
options.B: Sarah is entitled to the £599 price under the Consumer Rights Act 2015, which requires traders to honour displayed prices.
options.C: No contract has been formed because the window display is an invitation to treat, not an offer.
options.D: No contract has been formed because Sarah's statement was a counter-offer, which the travel agent rejected.

Contract Law.explanation: A shop or window display is an invitation to treat, not an offer: Pharmaceutical Society of GB v Boots Cash Chemists [1953]. Sarah's statement constitutes the offer, which the travel agent has not yet accepted. Option A is wrong because there was no offer capable of acceptance — the display merely invited customers to make offers to buy. Option B is incorrect: the CRA 2015 does not require traders to honour displayed prices; an incorrect price display may raise a Trading Standards issue but does not create a binding contract. Option D mischaracterises Sarah's statement — she was making an offer, not a counter-offer. A counter-offer responds to a prior offer; no prior offer existed here.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: On Monday, Alan posts an offer to sell his car to Beth for £4,000, requesting a reply by post. Beth posts her acceptance on Wednesday. On Thursday, Alan phones Beth to withdraw the offer. Beth's letter arrives on Friday. Has a contract been formed?

options.A: No contract was formed because Alan withdrew the offer before Beth's acceptance was received.
options.B: No contract was formed because revocation by telephone overrides a postal acceptance.
options.C: A contract was formed on Wednesday when Beth posted her acceptance.
options.D: A contract was formed on Friday when Alan received Beth's acceptance.

Contract Law.explanation: Under the postal rule established in Adams v Lindsell (1818), acceptance is complete and binding on the offeror at the moment of posting. Beth's acceptance was therefore complete on Wednesday. Alan's Thursday phone call came after the contract had already been formed and cannot revoke an already-accepted offer. Option A is wrong because the revocation came after formation on Wednesday. Option B is incorrect: revocation is effective when communicated, but is irrelevant here because the contract existed before Alan's call. Option D is wrong because receipt is not the operative moment under the postal rule.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: Marcus offers to sell his motorbike to Natasha for £3,500. Natasha replies: 'I reject your price — I will pay £3,000.' Marcus says: 'No.' Natasha then says: 'Fine — I accept your original offer of £3,500.' Has a contract been formed?

options.A: Yes, because Natasha's final acceptance matched the terms of Marcus's original offer.
options.B: Yes, because Marcus's rejection of £3,000 implicitly reaffirmed the original offer.
options.C: No, because Natasha's offer of £3,000 was a counter-offer that extinguished Marcus's original offer.
options.D: No, because Natasha's change of mind showed she lacked the necessary intention to contract.

Contract Law.explanation: A counter-offer operates as a rejection of the original offer and substitutes a new one: Hyde v Wrench (1840). Natasha's offer of £3,000 was unambiguously a counter-offer, extinguishing Marcus's offer of £3,500. There was no original offer capable of acceptance when Natasha said 'I accept.' Option A is wrong because the original offer no longer existed. Option B is a common trap: Marcus's rejection of £3,000 was a rejection of Natasha's counter-offer, not a reinstatement of his original offer. To revive it, Marcus would need to make a fresh offer. Option D misidentifies the reason: the issue is destruction of the original offer, not intention to contract.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: Last year, Hannah spent three hours searching for David's lost dog and returned it safely. Moved by her kindness, David later promises to pay Hannah £200 for what she did. David never pays. Can Hannah enforce David's promise?

options.A: Yes, because Hannah's act of finding the dog was good consideration for David's promise.
options.B: Yes, because David's promise was made in recognition of a benefit he received.
options.C: No, because Hannah acted gratuitously and cannot claim payment for voluntary acts.
options.D: No, because Hannah's act was past consideration, which is not valid consideration.

Contract Law.explanation: Consideration must be provided in exchange for the promise and cannot be past: Re McArdle [1951]. Hannah found the dog before David made his promise; her act therefore cannot support his subsequent promise. Option A is wrong: a past act cannot constitute consideration for a later promise, however beneficial it was. Option B reflects a moral argument but not the law — past consideration does not become binding merely because the promisor acknowledges a benefit. This option conflates past consideration with the narrow exception in Lampleigh v Braithwait (1615), which applies only where the prior act was performed at the promisor's request and payment was always contemplated; neither condition is met here. Option D is a distractor: the legal objection is timing (past consideration), not the voluntary character of the act.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: Married couple Anita and Ben agree that Ben will pay Anita £50 per week for household expenses while she manages the family home. Ben stops paying. Anita seeks to enforce the agreement in court. Which of the following best describes the legal position?

options.A: The agreement is binding because it is supported by good consideration on both sides.
options.B: The agreement is not binding because agreements between spouses about household arrangements are presumed not to be legally binding.
options.C: The agreement is binding because it was made with clear agreement between both parties.
options.D: The agreement is not binding because household management is not recognised as good consideration.

Contract Law.explanation: There is a rebuttable presumption that domestic and family agreements are not intended to create legal relations: Balfour v Balfour [1919]. Anita and Ben's arrangement is a classic domestic agreement that the courts consistently hold to be outside legal enforcement. The presumption is not rebutted on these facts — there is no indication of a separated couple formalising arrangements or a commercial context. Option A is a distractor: even if consideration exists, a binding contract requires both consideration and intention to create legal relations; these are separate requirements. Option C is wrong: agreement alone does not create legal relations in a domestic context. Option D is incorrect: the reason for non-enforceability is lack of ICLR, not absence of consideration; household management may in principle constitute consideration.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: Jamie, who is 17, buys a professional racing bicycle from a sports shop for £800 and uses it for competitive cycling training. He attempts to return it the following week, claiming he lacks capacity to contract. Which of the following best describes the legal position?

options.A: The contract is void because minors lack capacity to enter contracts.
options.B: The contract is void because the purchase price exceeds an amount a minor can legally spend.
options.C: The contract is voidable at Jamie's option because he is a minor.
options.D: The contract is binding on Jamie because it is a contract for necessaries.

Contract Law.explanation: A minor is bound by contracts for necessaries: Sale of Goods Act 1979 s.3. Necessaries are goods suitable to the minor's condition in life and to his actual requirements at the time of sale. A professional racing bicycle for a minor engaged in competitive cycling training qualifies as a necessary. The contract is therefore binding and Jamie cannot repudiate it. Option A is wrong: minor's contracts are generally voidable, not void. For necessaries, they are fully binding. Option B is incorrect: English law imposes no financial threshold on minor's contracts; the test is the category of goods relative to the minor's lifestyle, not price. Option C is wrong on these facts: while non-necessary contracts are voidable at the minor's option, the necessaries doctrine defeats that right here.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: Rachel books a car parking space by telephone. On arrival at the car park, she sees a prominent sign at the entrance stating: 'Parking at owner's risk. All liability excluded.' Her car is damaged due to the negligence of car park staff. Is the exclusion clause incorporated into the contract?

options.A: No, because the contract was formed by telephone before Rachel saw the sign, so the clause was brought to her attention too late.
options.B: No, because exclusion clauses must be contained in a signed document to be incorporated.
options.C: Yes, because the sign is prominently displayed and Rachel had constructive notice of its contents.
options.D: Yes, because exclusion clauses in commercial car parks are always incorporated as standard industry terms.

Contract Law.explanation: An exclusion clause must be incorporated before or at the time the contract is made: Olley v Marlborough Court [1949]. The contract was formed when Rachel booked by telephone. The sign was not brought to her attention until she arrived — after the contract was concluded. The clause therefore cannot be incorporated. Option A overstates the requirement: exclusion clauses can be incorporated by notice without a signature, provided notice is given in time: Parker v South Eastern Railway (1877). Option C is wrong in this context: even prominent notice cannot incorporate a term into a contract already formed — the prominence of the sign is irrelevant to the timing point. Option D is incorrect: there is no industry convention that automatically incorporates exclusion clauses; incorporation always requires reasonable notice given to the specific party before or at contracting.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: A band is booked to perform at an outdoor festival on a specific date for £15,000. Before the event, the government declares a national emergency and bans all public gatherings, making the festival impossible. Which of the following best describes the legal effect?

options.A: The contract is voidable at the festival organiser's option because performance has become impractical.
options.B: The contract is discharged by frustration because a supervening event has made performance impossible without the fault of either party.
options.C: The contract is void because the parties failed to provide for government intervention in their agreement.
options.D: The band can claim £15,000 because the organiser must bear the risk of cancellation.

Contract Law.explanation: A contract is frustrated where a supervening event, beyond the parties' control, makes performance impossible, illegal, or radically different from what was contemplated: Davis Contractors Ltd v Fareham UDC [1956]; Taylor v Caldwell (1863). A government ban makes performance physically impossible without fault on either side. Option A is wrong: frustration terminates the contract automatically by operation of law; it does not create a voidable contract at one party's option. Option C is wrong: frustration does not require the contract to be silent on the supervening event — it applies unless the parties have specifically allocated the risk of that event; no such allocation appears here. Option D is wrong: upon frustration, the contract is discharged and the contractual fee cannot be claimed; financial adjustment is governed by the Law Reform (Frustrated Contracts) Act 1943 instead.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: A publisher contracts to pay a first-time author a £20,000 advance on publication. The publisher breaches by refusing to publish. The author spent £3,000 preparing the manuscript and expected royalties of at least £30,000 over the life of the book. The primary measure of damages in contract law aims to put the claimant in the position they would have been in had the contract been performed. Which measure best applies?

options.A: The author should recover the £3,000 in wasted preparation costs under the reliance measure.
options.B: The author should recover the £20,000 advance only, because that is the benefit promised by the publisher.
options.C: The author should recover damages based on the expectation interest, including the £20,000 advance and any lost profits, subject to remoteness and mitigation.
options.D: The author should recover nothing because the royalty projections were speculative.

Contract Law.explanation: The primary rule in contract damages is the expectation measure: the court aims to put the claimant in the same financial position as if the contract had been performed: Robinson v Harman (1848). This covers the promised advance and lost profits, subject to remoteness and mitigation. Option A is wrong as a primary answer: the reliance measure (recovering wasted expenditure) is an alternative the claimant may elect, typically where expectation loss is difficult to quantify. It is not the primary measure. Option B is incomplete: the expectation interest is not limited to the advance; it includes loss of profit subject to remoteness. Option D is wrong: courts will assess damages for uncertain future profits where there was a real chance of earning them; incomplete speculative projections do not defeat the claim where a reasonable expectation of profit existed.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: Alpha Ltd contracts with Beta Ltd for Beta to install an IT system at the offices of Gamma Ltd, Alpha's subsidiary. The contract states: 'This contract is for the benefit of Gamma Ltd.' The installation is negligently performed and Gamma suffers loss. Can Gamma enforce the contract against Beta under the Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Act 1999?

options.A: No, because Gamma was not a party to the contract and privity prevents third-party enforcement.
options.B: Yes, because the contract expressly identifies Gamma as a beneficiary, satisfying s.1(1)(b) of the Act.
options.C: Yes, but only if Gamma can show it provided consideration to Beta.
options.D: No, because the Act only applies to contracts for the direct supply of goods or services to the third party.

Contract Law.explanation: Under s.1(1)(b) of the Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Act 1999, a third party may enforce a contractual term if the contract purports to confer a benefit on them and they are expressly identified by name, class, or description. The contract here expressly states it is 'for the benefit of Gamma Ltd' — Gamma is identified by name and a benefit is conferred. Unless s.1(2) applies (i.e. it appears the parties did not intend the term to be enforceable by the third party), Gamma may enforce. Option A is wrong: the 1999 Act is the statutory exception to the common law privity rule. Option C is wrong: the Act specifically removes the requirement for the third party to provide consideration — this was the fundamental change the Act made. Option D is wrong: the Act contains no limitation restricting it to direct supply contracts; it applies to any contract satisfying s.1(1)(a) or s.1(1)(b).

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: Omar, a builder, contracts to renovate Priya's café for £40,000. Halfway through, he tells her he has underestimated costs and will not finish unless she pays an extra £5,000. Priya agrees because she has a corporate booking in three weeks. Omar completes the work. Can Omar enforce the additional £5,000?

options.A: Yes, because completing on time gave Priya a practical benefit, which constitutes good consideration.
options.B: No, because Omar was only doing what he was already contractually bound to do, which is not good consideration.
options.C: No, because the promise to pay more was made under duress and is therefore unenforceable.
options.D: Yes, because both parties agreed to the variation, making it contractually binding.

Contract Law.explanation: Where a party performs an existing contractual duty but doing so confers a practical benefit on the promisor, that benefit constitutes good consideration for the variation: Williams v Roffey Bros [1991]. Priya received the practical benefit of timely completion, avoiding loss of her corporate booking. Option A states the traditional rule in Stilk v Myrick (1809) but Williams v Roffey qualifies this rule where a genuine practical benefit is obtained. Option C is a distractor: while economic duress can vitiate contracts where pressure is illegitimate, the question is about enforceability of consideration, and on these facts Omar's threat to stop is not necessarily illegitimate pressure. Option D is incorrect: mutual agreement alone does not make a modification binding without consideration; agreement and consideration are separate requirements.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: A buyer sends an order on its standard form, which limits the buyer's liability. The seller responds with an acknowledgment on its own standard form, which excludes the buyer's liability limit and imposes the seller's terms. The goods are delivered and accepted without further correspondence. On whose terms is the contract formed?

options.A: The buyer's terms, because the buyer made the first offer.
options.B: The seller's terms, because the seller's acknowledgment was a counter-offer accepted by the buyer taking delivery.
options.C: Both parties' terms apply in equal measure, with conflicting terms cancelling each other out.
options.D: No contract is formed because the parties never agreed on terms.

Contract Law.explanation: In a battle of the forms, English law applies the 'last shot' doctrine: the final set of terms put forward before performance is treated as a counter-offer, and a party who performs without objection accepts those terms: Butler Machine Tool Co v Ex-Cell-O Corp [1979]. The seller's acknowledgment on its own terms was a counter-offer, and the buyer accepted it by taking delivery without objection. Option A is wrong because the first shot does not win in a battle of forms — it is displaced by subsequent counter-offers. Option C is incorrect: English law does not apply a 'knock-out' rule blending conflicting terms; conflicting terms are resolved by the last shot. Option D is wrong because performance resolves the battle — delivery and acceptance confirm the parties proceeded on the seller's last-stated terms.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: Two businesses enter a supply agreement for widgets where the price is 'to be agreed between the parties at the start of each month.' Six months of supply proceed smoothly. In month seven, the parties cannot agree a price. The supplier claims a binding contract exists and seeks payment at the previous month's price. Which of the following best states the legal position?

options.A: A binding contract exists and the court will imply a reasonable price under the Sale of Goods Act 1979 s.8.
options.B: The contract is void for uncertainty because the price was never definitively fixed.
options.C: The contract is binding for months one to six but there are no obligations for month seven because agreement has broken down.
options.D: The supplier can enforce payment at the previous month's price because the parties' course of dealing implies that term.

Contract Law.explanation: Where a price is not fixed but goods are supplied, the Sale of Goods Act 1979 s.8 implies a reasonable price unless the contract shows a contrary intention. Six months of consistent performance is strong evidence that a binding framework contract exists, and the statutory mechanism provides the fall-back for pricing disputes: Foley v Classique Coaches [1934] (distinguishing May & Butcher v R [1934] where there had been no performance). Option A overstates the effect of 'to be agreed': this formulation does not automatically void a contract where consistent performance and a statutory fall-back exist. Option C imposes an artificial split between months 1–6 and month 7 that does not reflect how courts treat framework contracts with a statutory pricing mechanism. Option D goes further than the law allows: the SGA 1979 s.8 supplies a reasonable price — not necessarily the previous month's price — and cannot be displaced by merely invoking a course of dealing without more.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: Marco, a sole trader, buys a commercial coffee machine from a business supplier under a contract excluding all liability for defects. The machine fails within a month due to a manufacturing defect. Marco challenges the exclusion clause. Which legal regime governs his challenge?

options.A: The Consumer Rights Act 2015, because Marco is an individual purchasing goods.
options.B: The Unfair Contract Terms Act 1977, because Marco is contracting in the course of a business.
options.C: The common law reasonableness test only, because statutory controls apply solely to consumers.
options.D: Neither statute: exclusion clauses between businesses are entirely a matter of freedom of contract.

Contract Law.explanation: The Unfair Contract Terms Act 1977 (UCTA) applies to business-to-business contracts. Marco, as a sole trader buying equipment for his business, is contracting in the course of a business and is not a consumer. UCTA s.6(3) subjects clauses excluding liability for breach of the SGA 1979 implied terms in non-consumer sales to the reasonableness test. Option A is wrong: the Consumer Rights Act 2015 applies to contracts between a 'trader' and a 'consumer' (an individual acting outside their trade, business, craft or profession). Marco is acting within his business. Option C is incorrect: UCTA applies to B2B contracts and the reasonableness test is the statutory test under UCTA s.11, not a purely common law device. Option D is wrong: UCTA specifically restricts freedom of contract in B2B contexts involving exclusion of the SGA implied terms.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: A shipping contract guarantees delivery of perishable cargo 'by Friday.' The ship arrives Saturday. The cargo is slightly delayed but unspoiled. The buyer refuses to pay, claiming the delivery term is a condition and any breach entitles termination. Which of the following best describes how a court will approach the classification?

options.A: As a condition, because 'guaranteed' language signals strict compliance and any breach entitles the innocent party to terminate.
options.B: As a warranty, because the breach caused no serious loss to the buyer.
options.C: As an innominate term, because the court should assess the seriousness of the consequences of the actual breach.
options.D: As a condition, because time of delivery is always a condition in commercial contracts under the Sale of Goods Act 1979.

Contract Law.explanation: Where a term does not fall clearly within a recognised category, the court may treat it as an innominate (intermediate) term and assess the consequences of the particular breach: Hong Kong Fir Shipping Co v Kawasaki Kisen Kaisha [1962]. Whether the innocent party can terminate depends on whether the breach deprived them of substantially the whole benefit of the contract. A one-day delay causing no spoilage is unlikely to meet that threshold. Option A is wrong: 'guaranteed' language is persuasive but not determinative; courts look to consequences of breach, not the label used. Option B reverses the analysis: classification is not determined by the loss caused. For an innominate term, the loss caused determines the available remedy — but the reasoning must flow from classification first. Option D is incorrect: the SGA 1979 makes certain implied terms conditions (ss.12–15) but does not make every delivery time term a condition in all commercial contracts; time is of the essence only by agreement or in specific commercial contexts.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: A software company contracts to develop bespoke invoicing software for a law firm. The contract is silent on quality. The delivered software contains numerous bugs rendering it unfit for the firm's purposes. Under which basis is a quality term most likely to be implied?

options.A: A term implied in law under the Supply of Goods and Services Act 1982 s.13, requiring reasonable skill and care.
options.B: A term implied in fact under the business efficacy test, because the contract cannot work without quality software.
options.C: A term implied by custom, because it is standard in the software industry to warrant fitness for purpose.
options.D: A term implied in law under the Sale of Goods Act 1979 s.14, requiring satisfactory quality.

Contract Law.explanation: A contract to develop bespoke software is primarily a contract for services, not the sale of goods. The Supply of Goods and Services Act 1982 s.13 implies a term that the supplier will carry out the service with reasonable skill and care. Delivering bug-ridden software unfit for purpose is likely to breach this. Option B is incorrect: a term implied in fact under business efficacy (The Moorcock (1889)) fills specific gaps necessary to give effect to the parties' obvious intention. The statutory implied term is the more direct and appropriate mechanism. Option C is wrong: custom requires proof of an established and notorious trade usage — general expectations in the software industry do not automatically constitute a legally recognised custom. Option D is wrong: the SGA 1979 s.14 applies to contracts for the sale of goods. A bespoke software development contract is primarily a service contract governed by SGSA 1982, not the SGA 1979.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: Priya sells her car to Tom and tells him it has never been in an accident. She genuinely believes this, but in fact the car had a minor collision before she bought it. Tom purchases the car and later discovers the accident history. Which type of misrepresentation has Priya made?

options.A: Fraudulent misrepresentation, because Priya should have checked the car's history before making the statement.
options.B: Negligent misrepresentation under s.2(1) MA 1967, because she had no reasonable basis for her belief.
options.C: Innocent misrepresentation, because Priya genuinely believed the statement and had no reason to know otherwise.
options.D: No misrepresentation, because the statement was an opinion rather than a statement of fact.

Contract Law.explanation: An innocent misrepresentation is one made without fault — the representor had reasonable grounds to believe the statement was true and made it honestly. On these facts, Priya genuinely believed the car had never been in an accident and had no reason to know otherwise; she therefore made the statement innocently. Option A is wrong: fraudulent misrepresentation requires knowledge of falsity, absence of belief in truth, or recklessness as to truth: Derry v Peek (1889). Mere failure to check does not amount to fraud. Option B is wrong: s.2(1) MA 1967 requires that the defendant lacked reasonable grounds for their belief. The facts state Priya genuinely believed and had no reason to doubt — on these facts she may have had reasonable grounds, so s.2(1) is not clearly established. Option D is wrong: the factual history of a car ('never been in an accident') is a statement of fact, not an opinion.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: A seller tells a buyer the company's annual turnover is £500,000. Relying on this, the buyer pays £1.2m. After completion, true turnover is found to be £310,000. The seller made the statement believing it was correct but had not verified the figures. The buyer claims damages under s.2(1) of the Misrepresentation Act 1967. Which of the following best states the seller's position?

options.A: The seller is liable for damages assessed on a fraudulent basis unless he can prove he had reasonable grounds to believe the statement was true.
options.B: The buyer must prove the seller was negligent in making the statement for s.2(1) to apply.
options.C: The seller is not liable because he made the statement honestly and without intent to deceive.
options.D: The seller is liable only if the buyer can show the misrepresentation induced the contract.

Contract Law.explanation: Section 2(1) MA 1967 reverses the burden of proof: once the claimant proves a misrepresentation that induced the contract, the defendant is liable unless they can prove they had reasonable grounds for their belief up to the time the contract was made. Moreover, damages under s.2(1) are assessed on the tortious fraud measure: Royscot Trust Ltd v Rogerson [1991]. Failing to verify figures may mean the seller cannot discharge the burden of proving reasonable grounds. Option B is wrong: the burden is reversed under s.2(1) — the seller must disprove negligence, not the buyer prove it. Option C is wrong: honesty without reasonable grounds is not a defence under s.2(1); the test is objective reasonable grounds, not subjective belief. Option D identifies a genuine requirement — inducement is necessary for all misrepresentation claims — but it does not address the seller's specific position under s.2(1) and is therefore incomplete as an answer.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: Amy and Ben contract for Ben to sell his vintage motorbike to Amy for £8,000. Unknown to both parties, the motorbike had been destroyed in a fire the previous week. What is the legal effect on the contract?

options.A: The contract is void under the doctrine of common mistake because the subject matter did not exist at the time of contracting.
options.B: The contract is voidable at Amy's option because she was the party misled.
options.C: The contract is valid and Ben must pay damages to Amy for failure to deliver.
options.D: The contract is frustrated because the motorbike was destroyed after the agreement was made.

Contract Law.explanation: Where both parties share a fundamental mistake about the existence of the subject matter at the time of contracting (res extincta), the contract is void: Sale of Goods Act 1979 s.6. Neither party knew of the destruction; the mistake is shared and goes to the root of the contract. Option A is wrong: voidable contracts arise in cases of unilateral mistake or misrepresentation. Common mistake as to res extincta renders the contract void ab initio, not merely voidable at one party's option. Option C is wrong: Ben cannot be in breach for failing to deliver goods that did not exist at formation; there is no valid contract to breach. Option D is wrong: frustration applies to supervening events occurring after the contract is formed. The motorbike was destroyed before contracting, making this a case of res extincta at formation, not frustration.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: Builder Atlas threatens to walk off a major project unless the client, Brixton Hotels, agrees to pay an additional £100,000. Brixton Hotels has a non-negotiable opening date two weeks away and no realistic prospect of finding a replacement contractor. Brixton agrees under protest and, after the opening, seeks to set aside the additional payment. What elements must Brixton establish?

options.A: That Atlas's threat was unlawful, and that the threat caused Brixton to agree.
options.B: That Atlas made a fraudulent misrepresentation about its ability to complete the contract in order to extract the extra payment.
options.C: That Atlas exploited a relationship of confidence and trust to induce the payment.
options.D: That Atlas's pressure was illegitimate, that it left Brixton with no practical alternative, and that it caused the agreement.

Contract Law.explanation: Economic duress requires: (i) illegitimate pressure (which need not be unlawful in a criminal sense — threatening to breach a contract can suffice: DSND Subsea Ltd v Petroleum Geo Services ASA [2000]); (ii) which left the claimant no practical alternative; and (iii) which caused the agreement: Atlas Express Ltd v Kafco [1989]. Option A is too narrow: 'unlawful' in the criminal sense is not required; illegitimate pressure includes a threat to breach contract. Option C describes undue influence (exploitation of a relationship), which is a distinct doctrine not engaged here — there is no relationship of trust and confidence between a contractor and a commercial client. Option D introduces fraudulent misrepresentation, which is not on the facts: Atlas's threat was about future conduct, not a false statement of existing fact.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: Elderly Margaret signs a deed of gift transferring her house to her adult son Robert, who has managed her finances for three years and to whom she 'always deferred.' She now seeks to set aside the gift claiming undue influence. Which of the following best describes how her claim would be approached?

options.A: Margaret must prove actual undue influence by showing Robert applied specific pressure to obtain the gift.
options.B: The presumption of undue influence cannot apply because Margaret is an adult with full legal capacity.
options.C: Robert must prove there was no undue influence, because parent-child relationships always raise a presumption of undue influence.
options.D: The relationship raises a presumption of undue influence; Margaret need only show the transaction calls for explanation.

Contract Law.explanation: Undue influence can be presumed (Class 2B) where a claimant proves a relationship of trust and confidence and shows the transaction calls for explanation: Royal Bank of Scotland v Etridge (No 2) [2001]. Robert managed Margaret's finances for three years and she 'always deferred' to him — capable of establishing a Class 2B relationship. Transferring the family home is a transaction that plainly calls for explanation, shifting the burden to Robert to rebut. Option A is too onerous: once a Class 2 presumption arises, actual pressure need not be proved. Option C is wrong: a parent-child relationship is not a Class 2A (automatically recognised) category; it must be established as Class 2B on the specific facts. Option D is wrong: capacity and undue influence are separate doctrines; having legal capacity does not prevent a finding of undue influence.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: In January, a sports manufacturer contracts to deliver 500 football kits to a club by 1 August. In March, the manufacturer calls the club and says: 'We have taken on another client and will not be fulfilling your order.' The club immediately sources replacement kits at higher cost in April. Which of the following best describes the club's position?

options.A: The club must wait until 1 August to determine if the manufacturer actually breaches, then claim damages.
options.B: The club has no claim because the manufacturer may change its mind before the delivery date.
options.C: The club can treat the contract as discharged but must wait until 1 August to assess its loss for damages.
options.D: The club can treat the contract as discharged immediately, accept the repudiation, and claim damages.

Contract Law.explanation: Where a party clearly and unequivocally renounces a contract before performance is due, this constitutes anticipatory breach: Hochster v De la Tour (1853). The innocent party may accept the repudiation immediately, treat the contract as discharged, and sue at once without waiting for the performance date. The club was entitled to source replacement kits in April. Option A is wrong: under anticipatory breach, the innocent party need not wait until the performance date. Option C is wrong: damages are assessed from the date of accepted repudiation, not the original performance date; there is no need to wait until August. Option D is wrong: a clear, unequivocal renunciation in March is anticipatory breach; the manufacturer's theoretical right to change its mind does not prevent the innocent party from accepting the breach and mitigating.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: A courier is contracted to deliver a part for a food company's production machine. The courier is told the delivery is 'urgent' but not told the machine is broken and production has stopped. The courier delays delivery by three days. During those three days the company loses £50,000 in production profit. Under the remoteness rule in Hadley v Baxendale, can the company recover the £50,000?

options.A: No, because loss of production profit from a broken machine was not within the reasonable contemplation of the courier at the time of contracting.
options.B: Yes, because the courier was told the delivery was urgent.
options.C: Yes, because lost profits are always recoverable in contract as consequential losses.
options.D: No, because the courier was merely negligent rather than in deliberate breach.

Contract Law.explanation: Under the second limb of Hadley v Baxendale (1854), special losses are only recoverable if the party in breach was aware of the special circumstances at contracting, such that the loss was within their reasonable contemplation. The courier was told delivery was 'urgent' but not that the machine was broken and production had stopped. The £50,000 production loss is a special loss not arising naturally from a delivery delay. Without knowledge of the specific machine-breakdown context, this loss was not in the courier's reasonable contemplation. Option A is wrong: 'urgent' does not communicate the specific circumstances causing the lost profit and does not bring the £50,000 within the second limb. Option C is wrong: consequential losses are only recoverable if within the reasonable contemplation of the parties at contracting — there is no general rule that lost profits are always recoverable. Option D is wrong on the facts: a courier who delays delivery is in breach of contract; the issue of damages arises only once breach is established.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: A property developer emails an investor: 'I offer you the development site at 14 Birch Lane for £2.5m, subject to contract.' The investor immediately replies: 'I accept and consider myself legally bound.' The developer later sells to a third party. The investor claims a binding contract was formed. Which of the following best analyses the position?

options.A: A contract was formed because the investor's immediate acceptance was communicated to the developer.
options.B: A contract was formed because the postal rule applies to email communications.
options.C: No contract was formed because 'subject to contract' negatives the intention to be legally bound at this stage.
options.D: No contract was formed because contracts for the sale of land must be in signed writing under s.2 LP(MP)A 1989.

Contract Law.explanation: 'Subject to contract' is a well-recognised phrase in English property dealings indicating that the parties do not intend to be legally bound until formal contracts are exchanged: Eccles v Bryant [1948]. This qualification negatives the intention to create legal relations at the offer stage, regardless of the investor's purported acceptance. Option A is wrong: even if acceptance was effectively communicated, there was no binding offer to accept because the developer's communication was not intended to create legal relations. Option B is a trap: the postal rule applies to acceptance by post, and its application to email is uncertain; in any event the issue is the 'subject to contract' qualification, not the mechanics of communication. Option D is a distractor: s.2 LP(MP)A 1989 does require contracts for sale of land to be in signed writing, but the correct primary reason here is the 'subject to contract' qualification operating at the ICLR level, before formality requirements are even reached.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: TechSolve provides business software to a medium-sized accountancy firm under a contract limiting TechSolve's liability for all losses to £5,000. A serious software bug causes the firm to lose £250,000 in client data. TechSolve relies on the limitation clause. Under UCTA 1977, which factor is most relevant to the reasonableness assessment?

options.A: Whether the accountancy firm read and understood the clause before signing.
options.B: Whether the parties had equal bargaining power and whether TechSolve was insured against the risk capped by the clause.
options.C: Whether the limitation clause was prominently displayed in the contract.
options.D: Whether £5,000 was a reasonable estimate of likely loss at the time the software failed.

Contract Law.explanation: The UCTA 1977 Schedule 2 guidelines direct courts to consider, among other things, the relative bargaining strength of the parties and whether the supplier was insured for the risk: George Mitchell (Chesterhall) Ltd v Finney Lock Seeds [1983]. Whether TechSolve was insured and whether the firm had equal bargaining power to negotiate the cap are central to whether the clause is reasonable. Option A is wrong: incorporation and reasonableness are separate tests under UCTA. Reasonableness is assessed objectively against the Schedule 2 guidelines, not by the customer's subjective knowledge. Option C conflates incorporation (where prominence assists notice: Parker v South Eastern Railway) with reasonableness — prominence is relevant to notice, not to the fairness of the substantive term. Option D misapplies the test: reasonableness is assessed at the time of contracting, not at the time of loss, and focuses on the overall allocation of risk, not merely whether the cap matched eventual loss.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: In 2021, James bought shares after Carla falsely told him the company had no outstanding debts. He was aware 'something was not right' but purchased anyway, continued holding the shares and claiming dividends until 2024, when he discovered the true position and sought rescission. Which factor is most likely to bar rescission?

options.A: Lapse of time only, because James took three years to bring his claim.
options.B: Affirmation only, because James continued to hold shares and claim dividends after becoming suspicious.
options.C: Both affirmation and lapse of time, because James's conduct evidenced acceptance and the delay is also relevant.
options.D: Neither: rescission for fraudulent misrepresentation cannot be barred by affirmation or lapse of time.

Contract Law.explanation: Two bars to rescission are engaged. First, affirmation: a representee who continues to perform the contract with knowledge of the misrepresentation (or facts putting them on notice) affirms it and loses the right to rescind: Long v Lloyd [1958]. James continued holding shares and claiming dividends while aware 'something was not right' — strong evidence of affirmation. Second, lapse of time: while for fraud the limitation period runs from discovery, the equitable remedy of rescission can be lost by laches (unreasonable delay making rescission inequitable) even in fraud cases, particularly where third-party interests or unjust enrichment concerns arise. Option A is incomplete: lapse alone is uncertain without more on equitable laches; affirmation is the stronger and clearer bar on these facts. Option B is also incomplete: affirmation is clearly made out, but lapse is also a relevant equitable consideration. Option D is wrong: rescission can be barred even for fraud by affirmation, laches, impossibility of restitution, and third-party rights.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: A fraudster visits a car dealership claiming to be a well-known local businessman with excellent credit. The dealership makes reasonable checks (which are faked) and sells the car on credit. The fraudster immediately sells the car to an innocent third party. When the fraud is discovered, the dealership seeks to recover the car from the third party. Which principle determines whether the dealership can recover?

options.A: The dealership can recover because unilateral mistake as to identity renders the contract void, and a void contract passes no title.
options.B: The dealership cannot recover because the third party gave value and is protected by a general bona fide purchaser defence.
options.C: The dealership can recover under the Misrepresentation Act 1967 by rescinding the contract against the third party.
options.D: The dealership cannot recover because it dealt face-to-face with the fraudster, and the law presumes the seller intended to contract with the person physically present, making the contract voidable not void.

Contract Law.explanation: The critical distinction is between contracts made at a distance — where mistake as to identity can void the contract (Cundy v Lindsay (1878)) — and face-to-face dealings — where the presumption is that the seller intends to contract with the person physically present, making the contract voidable not void: Lewis v Averay [1972]; Phillips v Brooks [1919]. A voidable contract can pass valid title before rescission, and once an innocent third party acquires for value, the right to rescind is lost. The dealership therefore has only a voidable contract; the fraudster passed title to the third party before rescission, extinguishing the dealership's right to recover. Option A is wrong: in face-to-face dealings the contract is voidable, not void — the void/voidable distinction turns on the face-to-face rule, not merely on the existence of a mistake. Option C is wrong: even analysed as misrepresentation, the MA 1967 cannot assist because the right to rescind is barred by the third party's acquisition for value. Option D partially captures the outcome but identifies the wrong mechanism — it is not a general bona fide purchaser doctrine but the specific operation of the voidable contract rule in face-to-face dealings.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: A caterer prepays £3,000 to a venue for a banquet subsequently frustrated by a fire destroying the venue. The caterer had also incurred £1,500 in preparatory costs before the frustrating event. The venue had spent £500 acting on the agreement. Under the Law Reform (Frustrated Contracts) Act 1943, what is the most likely outcome?

options.A: The caterer recovers £3,000 minus an allowance for the venue's expenses up to the cap of the sum paid; the preparatory costs are not recoverable.
options.B: The caterer recovers the full £3,000; the venue cannot retain any sum because the contract is frustrated.
options.C: The caterer recovers £3,000 and can also claim the £1,500 preparatory costs from the venue as unjust enrichment.
options.D: Losses lie where they fall: neither party can recover anything after frustration.

Contract Law.explanation: Under the Law Reform (Frustrated Contracts) Act 1943 s.1(2), prepayments are recoverable but the court may allow the payee to retain or recover expenses incurred before the frustrating event, up to a cap equal to the sum paid. The venue may therefore retain up to £500 of the £3,000, and the caterer will recover the remainder. As for the £1,500 preparatory costs: s.1(3) allows a party to claim a just sum for a valuable benefit conferred on the other party — but the caterer's preparatory costs were for its own performance and did not benefit the venue, so s.1(3) does not assist. Option A is wrong: s.1(2) permits the payee to retain an expense allowance; a blanket recovery ignores this. Option C is wrong: s.1(3) requires a valuable benefit to have been conferred on the other party; the caterer's own preparation did not benefit the venue. Option D states the common law position before the 1943 Act, which the Act was enacted to replace.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: Omega contracts to deliver specialist machinery to Gamma by 1 June. On 15 May, Omega says it will be unable to deliver by 1 June. Gamma responds: 'We'll give you more time — deliver by 30 June.' Omega delivers on 30 June, but the machinery is defective. Gamma wishes to terminate for both the late delivery and the defective machinery. Which of the following best analyses Gamma's position?

options.A: Gamma can terminate because the original failure to meet the 1 June deadline was itself a repudiation.
options.B: Gamma cannot terminate for late delivery because it affirmed the contract by granting an extension, but may be able to terminate for defective machinery if the defect goes to the root of the contract.
options.C: Gamma can terminate for both the late delivery and the defective machinery, because both arose from the same breach.
options.D: Gamma has no right to terminate because it is estopped from relying on the delivery terms having varied them.

Contract Law.explanation: By agreeing to an extension on 15 May, Gamma elected to affirm the contract rather than accept the repudiation. Affirmation waives the right to terminate for that particular breach. Gamma therefore cannot retrospectively terminate for late delivery. The defective machinery is an independent, subsequent breach. Whether Gamma can terminate for it depends on the classification of the term: if delivery of conforming goods is a condition, any breach entitles termination; if an innominate term, termination requires a breach depriving Gamma of substantially the whole benefit. Option A is wrong: Gamma affirmed the contract on 15 May, waiving the right to terminate for late delivery. Option C is wrong: affirmation cuts off the right to terminate for the 1 June breach; subsequent breaches are assessed independently. Option D misstates the position: estoppel prevents enforcement of strict delivery by 1 June during the extension, but does not extinguish all rights — Gamma retains the right to enforce the varied date and to rely on subsequent defects.

Contract Law.subject: Contract Law
Contract Law.stem: A logistics contract states: 'In the event of late delivery, the carrier shall pay £10,000 per day of delay as liquidated damages.' The carrier delivers two days late, causing the client actual losses of £3,000. The client claims £20,000. Under which principle is the clause most likely to be challenged, and what is the correct statement of current law?

options.A: The clause is void as a penalty under the rule that liquidated damages clauses must be a genuine pre-estimate of loss.
options.B: The clause is enforceable because the parties freely agreed to it and freedom of contract prevails.
options.C: The clause is void as a penalty because £10,000 per day is extravagant compared to £3,000 actual loss.
options.D: The clause will be assessed under Cavendish Square Holding BV v Makdessi: it is void only if it imposes a detriment out of all proportion to any legitimate interest the innocent party has in enforcing the obligation.

Contract Law.explanation: The Supreme Court in Cavendish Square Holding BV v Makdessi [2015] reformulated the test for penalty clauses. The old 'genuine pre-estimate of loss' test has been superseded: a clause is a penalty only where it imposes a detriment on the contract-breaker that is out of all proportion to any legitimate interest of the innocent party. Commercial parties with equal bargaining power have considerable latitude to agree deterrent clauses with legitimate interests in securing performance. Option A misstates current law: the 'genuine pre-estimate of loss' test from Dunlop Pneumatic Tyre Co v New Garage [1915] is no longer the primary test after Makdessi. Option B overstates freedom of contract: the courts retain equitable jurisdiction to strike down genuine penalties even in commercial contracts, albeit on a narrower basis post-Makdessi. Option C applies the pre-Makdessi Dunlop test, which focused on disproportion between the stipulated sum and actual loss; post-Makdessi, the question is whether the clause is out of all proportion to a legitimate interest — a wider and different analysis.

Tort Law.subject: Tort Law
Tort Law.stem: Which of the following best describes the 'neighbour principle' established in Donoghue v Stevenson [1932]?

options.A: A duty of care is owed to those so closely and directly affected by your acts that you ought reasonably to have them in contemplation.
options.B: A duty of care is owed only to those with whom the defendant has a contractual relationship.
options.C: A duty of care is owed to all members of the general public regardless of foreseeability.
options.D: A duty of care is owed only in cases where there has been physical contact between parties.

Tort Law.explanation: Option A is correct. The neighbour principle (Lord Atkin, Donoghue v Stevenson) established that a duty of care is owed to those so closely and directly affected by acts that they ought to be in contemplation. Option B is incorrect because the neighbour principle explicitly moved beyond contractual relationships. Option C overstates the duty (foreseeability is required). Option D is too narrow and ignores the principle's breadth.

Tort Law.subject: Tort Law
Tort Law.stem: In Robinson v Chief Constable of West Yorkshire Police [2018], the Supreme Court clarified the role of the Caparo test. When does Caparo apply?

options.A: Caparo applies only to novel situations where the facts do not fall within an established duty category.
options.B: Caparo applies in every negligence case without exception.
options.C: Caparo applies only when the claimant is a professional.
options.D: Caparo applies only where the defendant has acted intentionally.

Tort Law.explanation: Option B is correct. Robinson clarified that Caparo's three-stage test applies only to novel situations; where facts fall within established categories (road users, employers, manufacturers, occupiers), the duty exists without applying Caparo. Option A is incorrect (Caparo is not universal). Option C wrongly limits Caparo to professionals. Option D confuses Caparo with intentional acts.

Tort Law.subject: Tort Law
Tort Law.stem: Which case established the conditions under which a duty of care is owed for negligent misstatement?

options.A: Hedley Byrne & Co v Heller & Partners [1964].
options.B: Donoghue v Stevenson [1932].
options.C: Caparo Industries plc v Dickman [1990].
options.D: Bolam v Friern Hospital Management Committee [1957].

Tort Law.explanation: Option B is correct. Hedley Byrne established that a duty of care can be owed for negligent misstatement if there is a special relationship (assumption of responsibility) and reasonable reliance by the claimant. Option A established the neighbour principle for products. Option C applied Caparo to auditors but was based on Hedley Byrne principles. Option D concerns the professional standard of care, not misstatement liability.

Tort Law.subject: Tort Law
Tort Law.stem: In negligence, how is the standard of care generally measured?

options.A: By the standard of care of the least careful person in similar circumstances.
options.B: By reference only to what the defendant actually knew.
options.C: By the standard of the reasonable person in the defendant's position.
options.D: By comparison with the standard of the claimant's own conduct.

Tort Law.explanation: Option C is correct. The standard of care is objective: how would a reasonable person in the defendant's position have acted? Option A sets too low a standard (the least careful person). Option B ignores the objective standard (what a reasonable person should have known matters). Option D improperly focuses on the claimant's conduct rather than the defendant's.

Tort Law.subject: Tort Law
Tort Law.stem: What is the 'but-for' test in causation?

options.A: Causation requires proof that the claimant would not have suffered harm but-for the defendant's breach.
options.B: Causation is established if the defendant's negligence was the sole cause of harm.
options.C: Causation requires that the claimant can prove the harm would have occurred anyway.
options.D: Causation is satisfied if the defendant's act was one of many possible causes.

Tort Law.explanation: Option A is correct. The but-for test asks whether the claimant would have suffered the same loss but for the defendant's breach. If the answer is no, factual causation is established (Barnett v Chelsea & Kensington [1969]). Option B wrongly requires sole causation. Option D is too weak. Option C contradicts the test (if harm would have occurred anyway, the but-for test fails).

Tort Law.subject: Tort Law
Tort Law.stem: The defendant negligently injures the claimant, who is taken to hospital. A junior doctor at the hospital makes a serious error in treatment, which significantly worsens the claimant's condition. Which statement best describes the legal consequence?

options.A: The doctor's error is an intervening act that breaks the chain, so the defendant is not liable for the worsened condition.
options.B: If the medical error was foreseeable (as ordinary professional negligence can occur), the chain of causation is not broken and the defendant remains liable.
options.C: The defendant remains liable for all consequences unless the doctor's error was so gross or reckless as to be extraordinary.
options.D: The defendant loses liability unless they can prove they did not contribute to the need for hospital treatment.

Tort Law.explanation: Option C is correct. An intervening act (novus actus interveniens) breaks the chain of causation if it is independent and unforeseeable or extraordinary. Ordinary (but still negligent) acts by medical professionals are generally foreseeable and do not break the chain. Only extraordinary or reckless acts by the doctor break the chain. Option A is too absolute. Option B confuses the test. Option D wrongly shifts the burden.

Tort Law.subject: Tort Law
Tort Law.stem: What common duty does an occupier owe to lawful visitors under the Occupiers' Liability Act 1957?

options.A: A duty to ensure the premises are absolutely safe.
options.B: A duty to indemnify visitors against all injury or damage.
options.C: A duty to take reasonable care to ensure visitors are reasonably safe in using the premises for the purposes for which they are invited or permitted.
options.D: A duty to warn of all hidden defects, regardless of whether they are foreseeable.

Tort Law.explanation: Option C is correct. The OLA 1957 Section 2(2) provides that occupiers must take such care as is reasonable in all the circumstances to ensure visitors are reasonably safe. This is not absolute safety but reasonable care. Option A overstates the duty. Option B imposes unlimited liability. Option D is too broad (all hidden defects).

Tort Law.subject: Tort Law
Tort Law.stem: What are the key elements of private nuisance?

options.A: Unauthorized entry onto the claimant's land with intent to cause harm.
options.B: Any noise or smell that disturbs the claimant, regardless of severity.
options.C: A physical collision between the defendant's property and the claimant's land.
options.D: Interference with the claimant's use and enjoyment of land that is unreasonable in the circumstances.

Tort Law.explanation: Option D is correct. Private nuisance requires a substantial and unreasonable interference with the claimant's use and enjoyment of land. Option A describes trespass to land. Option B is too broad (nuisance requires unreasonableness). Option C describes encroachment rather than nuisance.

Tort Law.subject: Tort Law
Tort Law.stem: What are the essential elements a claimant must prove in an action for defamation?

options.A: Unauthorized publication, distress caused, and intent to harm.
options.B: Negligence, causation, and quantifiable financial loss.
options.C: A false statement, publication to a third party, and damage to reputation (or presumed damage in libel).
options.D: Malice, provocation, and failure to retract.

Tort Law.explanation: Option C is correct. Defamation requires: (1) a defamatory (false) statement; (2) publication to a third party; (3) a real or presumed reference to the claimant; and (4) damage to reputation (presumed in libel, proved in slander). Option A confuses defamation with other torts. Option B applies to negligence. Option D relates to malice and retractions, which are relevant but not essential elements.

Tort Law.subject: Tort Law
Tort Law.stem: A nightclub bouncer pushes a customer out of the door without consent or lawful justification. Has the bouncer committed battery?

options.A: No, because pushing someone out is a reasonable security measure.
options.B: Yes, but only if the customer suffered physical injury.
options.C: No, unless the bouncer used force beyond what was minimally necessary.
options.D: Yes, battery is committed when the defendant makes intentional physical contact without the claimant's consent and without lawful justification.

Tort Law.explanation: Option D is correct. Battery requires intentional application of unlawful force. Pushing without consent or lawful justification is battery. Option A wrongly treats this as a reasonable security measure (which would require lawful justification). Option B wrongly requires injury (battery does not require damage). Option C misses that any intentional contact without consent is battery.

Tort Law.subject: Tort Law
Tort Law.subtopic: Duty of Care — Omissions & Liability for Acts of Third Parties
Tort Law.stem: A local council owns and maintains a public park. A visitor is attacked by a third party in a poorly-lit area. The council had not installed additional lighting despite previous complaints about the location. Is the council liable in negligence for failing to prevent the third-party attack?

options.A: Yes, because the council should have foreseen the risk of attack and must prevent all third-party crimes.
options.B: No, because the council has no general duty to prevent crimes committed by third parties.
options.C: Yes, if the council owed a duty to the visitor (as occupier) and the failure to light was a breach that contributed to the foreseeable risk.
options.D: No, because the third party's act was an intervening act that breaks the chain of causation.

Tort Law.explanation: Option C is correct. While there is generally no duty to prevent third-party acts, a council as occupier owes a duty to visitors and must take reasonable precautions against foreseeable risks. If poor lighting contributed to a foreseeable risk of attack, and the council failed to remedy it, breach may be established. Option A overstates the duty (councils need not prevent all crime). Option B is too broad (occupiers do owe duties). Option D is inaccurate; a third-party act may not break causation if the risk was foreseeable.

Tort Law.subject: Tort Law
Tort Law.stem: A train passenger is involved in a minor collision but is not physically injured. He claims psychiatric injury (PTSD) suffered as a result of fear during the incident. Which case principle would determine whether he can recover?

options.A: He cannot recover because he was not physically injured.
options.B: He can recover only if he can show a pre-existing psychiatric condition.
options.C: As a primary victim in the zone of danger who feared physical harm, he can recover under Page v Smith [1996] if the psychiatric harm is foreseeable.
options.D: He must show that he was closer than two metres to the impact point.

Tort Law.explanation: Option C is correct. Page v Smith [1996] establishes that a primary victim (someone in the zone of danger and fearing physical harm) can recover for psychiatric injury even if only psychiatric harm materializes, provided it is foreseeable. Option A wrongly denies recovery for psychiatric harm alone. Option B is not a requirement. Option D misapplies a formula; there is no rigid two-metre test.

Tort Law.subject: Tort Law
Tort Law.stem: A surgeon performs a well-established surgical procedure on a patient. The operation is conducted in accordance with a practice accepted as proper by a responsible body of medical opinion, but a different body of equally responsible opinion would have adopted a different approach. The patient suffers harm. Is the surgeon liable in breach of the duty of care?

options.A: Yes, because there is an alternative practice that might have avoided the harm.
options.B: No, as long as the surgeon acted in accordance with a responsible body of medical opinion (Bolam v Friern test).
options.C: Yes, because the surgeon should have adopted the more defensive approach.
options.D: No, unless the surgeon's approach was reckless or grossly negligent.

Tort Law.explanation: Option B is correct. The Bolam test (Bolam v Friern Hospital Management Committee [1957]) provides that a professional is not negligent if they act in accordance with a practice accepted as proper by a responsible body of professional opinion. The mere existence of an alternative approach is not breach. Option A wrongly penalizes choosing one accepted practice over another. Option C overstates the duty. Option D confuses the standard with gross negligence.

Tort Law.subject: Tort Law
Tort Law.stem: A claimant contracted mesothelioma after exposure to asbestos at two different workplaces over many years. Medical evidence cannot identify which workplace exposure caused the disease, but both exposures materially contributed to the risk. Apply Fairchild v Glenhaven Funeral Services [2002].

options.A: The claimant cannot recover because the but-for test cannot be satisfied.
options.B: The claimant can recover if they can show that the defendant's negligence made a material contribution to the risk of harm, even if causation of the actual disease cannot be proved.
options.C: The claimant can recover only from the employer with the longest exposure period.
options.D: The claimant cannot recover because mesothelioma is a pre-existing condition.

Tort Law.explanation: Option B is correct. Fairchild v Glenhaven established an exception to the strict but-for test: where multiple tortfeasors have each negligently exposed the claimant to the risk of a disease, and the disease has materialized, the claimant can recover from any tortfeasor who made a material contribution to the risk, even though the but-for test cannot identify which exposure caused the actual disease. Option A ignores Fairchild's exception. Option C unfairly singles out one tortfeasor. Option D mischaracterizes mesothelioma.

Tort Law.subject: Tort Law
Tort Law.stem: A plumber visits commercial premises to repair a heating system. She is injured by a defect in the staircase. Must the occupier take the same precautions for this visitor as for a member of the public on a residential premises?

options.A: Yes, the standard of care is identical regardless of visitor type or premises.
options.B: No, skilled visitors have no protection under the OLA 1957.
options.C: Yes, but only for risks related to the plumbing work, not the staircase.
options.D: No, the OLA 1957 recognizes that an occupier may reasonably expect a skilled visitor to appreciate and guard against risks inherent in their trade.

Tort Law.explanation: Option D is correct. Section 2(3)(b) of the OLA 1957 provides that an occupier may reasonably expect a skilled visitor to appreciate and guard against risks inherent in their trade. The standard of care is not identical; it is adjusted for the visitor's skill and knowledge. Option A wrongly states the standard is identical. Option B wrongly denies protection. Option C is too narrow.

Tort Law.subject: Tort Law
Tort Law.stem: A trespasser is injured on the defendant's land when they fall into a hidden pit. The occupier was aware of the pit and of the likelihood of trespassers in the area. Under the Occupiers' Liability Act 1984, is the occupier liable?

options.A: Yes, if the occupier knew or had reasonable grounds to know of the danger, knew or should have known of trespassers, and failed to protect them or warn them reasonably.
options.B: No, unless the trespasser can prove the occupier deliberately set a trap.
options.C: Yes, the occupier must take the same precautions for trespassers as for lawful visitors.
options.D: No, because trespassers have no legal status and no protection under the OLA 1984.

Tort Law.explanation: Option D is correct. The OLA 1984 does protect trespassers and non-visitors. Section 1(1) creates a duty if the occupier: (a) is aware of a danger; (b) knows or has reasonable grounds to believe a non-visitor is in or may come near the danger; and (c) fails to take reasonable care or to warn adequately. Option A wrongly denies protection. Option C wrongly equates the standard. Option B wrongly requires deliberate action.

Tort Law.subject: Tort Law
Tort Law.stem: A factory has been in operation in the same location for 60 years, generating noise and vibration. A new residential development is built nearby five years ago. The new residents claim private nuisance. What is the correct legal position?

options.A: Yes, the factory can rely on statutory authority if authorized by statute or planning permission and operated with reasonable care.
options.B: No, private nuisance never allows a statutory authority defence.
options.C: Yes, because the factory operated before the development existed.
options.D: Yes, only if the factory obtained explicit written consent from the local authority.

Tort Law.explanation: Option A is correct. Statutory authority is a defence if the defendant acts within the scope of express statutory authorization or planning permission and exercises reasonable care. The mere fact that the activity predates the claimant's arrival is not itself a defence (though it may be relevant to establishing unreasonableness). Option B wrongly denies statutory authority as a defence. Option C wrongly suggests mere temporal priority is a defence. Option D overly restricts the defence.

Tort Law.subject: Tort Law
Tort Law.stem: The defendant owns land and constructs a large artificial pond for recreational purposes. Heavy rain causes the pond to overflow, flooding the claimant's property. Can the claimant bring an action under Rylands v Fletcher [1868]?

options.A: Yes, because any accumulation of water is a non-natural use under Rylands v Fletcher.
options.B: No, because heavy rain is a natural event beyond the defendant's control.
options.C: No, because recreational ponds are considered a natural use of land under modern law.
options.D: Yes, only if the defendant failed to maintain the pond properly, and the overflow constitutes an escape of something likely to cause mischief.

Tort Law.explanation: Option D is correct. Rylands v Fletcher liability requires an accumulation of something likely to cause mischief, non-natural use of land, and escape. A recreational pond may or may not be non-natural use (depending on context and reasonableness), but if it is, and something escapes causing damage, the defendant is liable regardless of fault. Option A is too broad. Option B ignores that the defendant's choice to create the pond is the relevant consideration. Option C misapplies Cambridge Water (which narrowed but did not abolish Rylands).

Tort Law.subject: Tort Law
Tort Law.stem: A journalist publishes an article reporting statements made in a court judgment handed down that day. The statements in the judgment are factually false and harm the claimant's reputation. The journalist made no independent investigation. Can the journalist rely on privilege as a defence?

options.A: No, because the journalist should have verified the statements independently.
options.B: No, because privilege applies only to direct court statements, not to journalistic reports.
options.C: Yes, but only if the journalist exercised reasonable care and acted in the public interest.
options.D: Yes, under absolute privilege for fair and accurate reports of court proceedings (Reynolds v Times Newspapers [1999]).

Tort Law.explanation: Option D is correct. Fair and accurate reports of court proceedings are protected by absolute privilege; journalists need not verify facts or fear liability for reporting what was said in court, even if false. The Defamation Act 2013 Section 14 codifies this. Option A wrongly requires independent verification. Option B wrongly denies privilege to reports. Option C confuses absolute with qualified privilege.

Tort Law.subject: Tort Law
Tort Law.stem: An employer is vicariously liable for an employee's negligent act if the negligence occurred in the course of employment. Which of the following best illustrates the 'close connection' test from Lister v Hesley Hall [2001]?

options.A: The negligence was directly authorized by the employer.
options.B: There was a sufficiently close connection between the employment and the negligence, such that it is fair and just to hold the employer liable.
options.C: The negligence was committed during working hours on the employer's premises.
options.D: The employee had acted maliciously or with intent to harm.

Tort Law.explanation: Option B is correct. Lister v Hesley Hall [2001] established the 'close connection' test: vicarious liability applies if there is a sufficiently close connection between the employment and the tort that it is fair, just and reasonable to hold the employer liable. This is a flexible test that goes beyond strict 'course of employment.' Option A wrongly requires authorization. Option C is too restrictive (negligence can occur outside premises). Option D introduces irrelevant intent.

Tort Law.subject: Tort Law
Tort Law.stem: A pedestrian is injured in a collision with a car. The driver was exceeding the speed limit, but the pedestrian was not using a designated crossing and stepped into traffic without checking. Under the Law Reform (Contributory Negligence) Act 1945, how is recovery affected?

options.A: The pedestrian cannot recover because they were partially at fault.
options.B: The pedestrian can recover full damages because the driver was primarily at fault.
options.C: The pedestrian can recover, but damages are reduced by a percentage reflecting their share of responsibility for the accident.
options.D: The pedestrian can recover only if they can prove the driver's negligence outweighs their own by at least 75%.

Tort Law.explanation: Option C is correct. The Law Reform (Contributory Negligence) Act 1945 allows recovery even if the claimant was partly at fault; damages are reduced by a percentage reflecting the claimant's share of responsibility. Option A wrongly bars recovery. Option B ignores contributory negligence. Option D sets an arbitrary threshold.

Tort Law.subject: Tort Law
Tort Law.stem: A rock climber participates in an organized climbing session at a professional climbing gym. The gym staff negligently fail to secure a belay anchor properly. The climber falls and is injured. Can the gym rely on the defence of volenti non fit injuria (consent to injury)?

options.A: Yes, because the climber voluntarily participated in an inherently risky activity.
options.B: Yes, if the climber signed a waiver form agreeing not to hold the gym liable.
options.C: No, because no one can consent to negligence, and waivers are always void.
options.D: No, because modern courts require genuine informed consent to the specific risk, and consent to inherent risks does not extend to negligence by the defendant.

Tort Law.explanation: Option D is correct. Volenti non fit injuria requires genuine, informed consent to the specific risk. Consent to inherent risks of an activity (e.g., climbing) does not extend to negligence by the defendant (improper securing). Modern courts construe volenti strictly. Option A overstates the defence. Option B overstates the effect of waivers (though they may be evidence of consent). Option C wrongly suggests waivers are always void.

Tort Law.subject: Tort Law
Tort Law.stem: A defective product manufactured in Germany is imported and sold in England. The purchaser is injured. Under the Consumer Protection Act 1987, who is strictly liable for the defect?

options.A: Only the manufacturer.
options.B: Only the retailer who sold the product.
options.C: The manufacturer, and if the manufacturer cannot be identified, the importer into the UK becomes the producer for liability purposes.
options.D: Only the importer, because they brought the product into the UK market.

Tort Law.explanation: Option C is correct. The CPA 1987 Section 2 imposes strict liability on the producer (manufacturer). Section 2(3) provides that if the producer cannot be identified, the importer into the UK is treated as the producer. This is a strict liability regime; fault is not required. Option A is incomplete (it ignores the importer rule). Option B wrongly limits liability to the retailer. Option D wrongly excludes the manufacturer.

Tort Law.subject: Tort Law
Tort Law.stem: What are the three stages of the Caparo test from Caparo Industries plc v Dickman [1990]?

options.A: Foreseeability, proximity, and fair, just and reasonable.
options.B: Intention, causation, and remoteness.
options.C: Breach, damage, and proof of loss.
options.D: Assumption of responsibility, reliance, and knowledge.

Tort Law.explanation: Option B is correct. The Caparo test requires: (1) reasonable foreseeability of risk to the claimant; (2) sufficient proximity between claimant and defendant; (3) fairness, justice and reasonableness in imposing a duty. Option A confuses elements of other torts. Option C lists elements of proving negligence, not the duty test. Option D relates to negligent misstatement, not the general Caparo framework.

Tort Law.subject: Tort Law
Tort Law.stem: A bystander witnessing a serious accident involving a family member suffers psychiatric injury. She was not in the zone of danger and did not fear for her own safety. Under Alcock v CC South Yorkshire Police [1991], what must she prove to establish a duty of care owed to her?

options.A: That the defendant owed a duty to all persons who might witness negligence, regardless of proximity.
options.B: That she was a secondary victim with sufficient proximity to the incident, a close relationship to the primary victim, and reasonable foreseeability of psychiatric harm.
options.C: That she was a primary victim who was in reasonable fear of physical harm.
options.D: That the accident was caused by the defendant's gross negligence.

Tort Law.explanation: Option B is correct. Alcock v CC South Yorkshire Police established that secondary victims (those outside the zone of danger) may recover for psychiatric harm if they satisfy three requirements: proximity to the incident (or its aftermath), a close relationship to the primary victim, and reasonable foreseeability of psychiatric harm to them. Option A overstates the duty. Option C describes primary victims, not secondary victims. Option D confuses the test with gross negligence standards.

Tort Law.subject: Tort Law
Tort Law.stem: Does Bolitho v City & Hackney Health Authority [1998] modify the Bolam test, and if so, how?
