Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: Under the Human Rights Act 1998, which of the following accurately describes the duty under section 3?

options.A: Section 3 requires courts to read and give effect to legislation in a way that is compatible with Convention rights, so far as it is possible to do so
options.B: Section 3 allows courts to strike down legislation that is incompatible with Convention rights
options.C: Section 3 permits courts to set aside legislation only by making a declaration of incompatibility, which binds no one
options.D: Section 3 applies only to common law and does not apply to statutory interpretation

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: Option B is correct. Section 3(1) of the HRA 1998 requires courts to read and give effect to legislation in a way compatible with Convention rights, 'so far as it is possible to do so' (FLK1-CAL-028, Ghaidan v Godin-Mendoza [2004]). This is a strong interpretive duty, but it has limits — if it would require departing from fundamental features of the legislation, it may not be possible. Option A is incorrect — s.3 does not allow courts to strike down legislation; that power is limited by parliamentary sovereignty. Option C confuses s.3 with s.4; s.3 is interpretation, s.4 is declaration of incompatibility. Option D is incorrect — s.3 applies to all legislation, not just common law.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.subtopic: Sections 3 and 4: interpretation and declaration of incompatibility
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: A statute defines 'marriage' as the union of a man and a woman. Under the HRA 1998, a transgender woman challenges this definition. The courts determine that a s.3 interpretation to read the statute as including same-sex marriages would require reading words into the statute that are not there. Which statement best describes the court's next step?

options.C: The courts have exhausted their duty under s.3 and may make a declaration of incompatibility under s.4, which binds the courts but does not invalidate the statute
options.A: The courts must strike down the statute because it violates the right to marry under Art 12 ECHR
options.B: The courts must rewrite the statute to conform to Convention rights, even if this requires going beyond s.3 limits
options.D: The courts must declare the statute void under the HRA and the matter is conclusively resolved

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: Option B is correct. When s.3 interpretation is not possible (because it would depart from fundamental features), courts may make a declaration of incompatibility under s.4 (FLK1-CAL-029, Bellinger v Bellinger [2003]). This declaration does not invalidate the statute but flags the incompatibility and prompts executive/legislative review. Option A is incorrect — courts cannot strike down primary legislation; the remedy is s.4 declaration. Option C is incorrect — courts cannot simply rewrite statutes; s.3 and s.4 set the limits. Option D is incorrect — a s.4 declaration does not void the statute; it is declaratory only.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: A prison governor restricts a prisoner's access to legal correspondence on grounds of security. The prisoner argues this breaches the right to a fair trial (Art 6 ECHR). Under the HRA 1998, how would a court assess this restriction?

options.C: The court would apply a proportionality assessment: whether the restriction pursues a legitimate aim, is rationally connected to it, and is not excessive (de Freitas test)
options.A: The court would uphold the restriction automatically because national security is mentioned in the statute
options.B: The court would strike down the restriction because Art 6 is an absolute right that cannot be interfered with
options.D: The court would defer entirely to the prison governor's decision because prison management is a matter of executive discretion

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: Option C is correct. Art 6 is engaged here (right to a fair trial, including access to legal advice), and while security concerns may be legitimate, the court applies proportionality review under the de Freitas test: is there a legitimate aim, rational connection, and proportionality? (FLK1-CAL-033). This reflects how qualified rights are assessed under HRA s.6 and JR principles. Option A is incorrect — national security does not automatically justify breaches. Option B is incorrect — Art 6 is not absolute; it can be subject to restrictions if proportionate. Option D is incorrect — courts will review the decision for proportionality; deference is not abdication of review.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: A local authority grants planning permission for a leisure facility on public land. Evidence at judicial review reveals that the real reason for approval was to improve the reputational standing of the council leader (who owns a nearby property). The stated reason was 'to promote local employment and community benefit.' The applicant seeks quashing. Which principle of illegality applies most directly?

options.C: Acting for an improper purpose — the actual purpose (reputation) differs fundamentally from the statutory purpose (planning and community benefit)
options.A: Fettering of discretion — the authority failed to consider relevant circumstances
options.B: Taking into account irrelevant considerations — the council considered the reputation of the leader
options.D: Procedural impropriety — the council failed to consult properly before deciding

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: Option C is correct. The council has acted for an improper purpose — the actual purpose (personal reputation of the leader) is inconsistent with the statutory purposes of planning law (FLK1-CAL-038, Wheeler v Leicester CC [1985]). Improper purpose is a sub-category of illegality. Option A is incorrect — fettering of discretion is about failing to exercise discretion, not about motivations. Option B is incorrect — the council considered a relevant factor (employment/community benefit in the stated reason), but the actual motivation was improper. Option D is incorrect — consultation is about procedure; the breach here is substantive (purpose).

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: A prison governor imposes a blanket ban on all visits to the prison for one month as punishment for a single violent incident by one prisoner. The ban affects hundreds of prisoners and their families, many of whom had no involvement in the incident. A prisoner's mother seeks judicial review. What is the modern approach of English courts?

options.C: The court applies proportionality review: the ban must be rational and necessary, and the disadvantage to prisoners must be proportionate to the legitimate aim
options.A: The court applies the test of Wednesbury unreasonableness — the ban is only quashed if no reasonable governor could impose it
options.B: The court cannot review the proportionality of disciplinary decisions; this is a matter of prison management discretion
options.D: The court automatically quashes any ban lasting more than one week as arbitrary

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: Option C is correct. Modern case law (R v MOD ex p Smith [1996]) shows that where fundamental rights are engaged (family life, liberty), courts now apply proportionality review rather than mere Wednesbury unreasonableness (FLK1-CAL-040, FLK1-CAL-CASE-018). The measure must be rationally connected to the aim and not excessive. This reflects HRA 1998 principles. Option A is too lenient; Wednesbury alone may not capture the disproportionality. Option B is incorrect — courts will review even executive decisions on proportionality grounds. Option D is incorrect — there is no bright-line rule about duration; it is about proportionality to the aim.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.subtopic: Procedural impropriety: natural justice and fair hearing
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: A student is expelled from university after a disciplinary hearing. The student was not informed of the allegations in advance, was not given an opportunity to hear the evidence against them, and was not allowed to present a defence. The university argues that the decision is a matter of contract law, not public law, and therefore JR does not apply. Which statement best describes the court's likely approach?

options.B: The court would apply JR principles, requiring the university to comply with natural justice (notice, hearing, fair consideration), because the university is exercising a quasi-judicial power affecting the student's rights
options.A: The court would refuse to hear the claim because the university's internal disciplinary procedures are a matter of private law only
options.C: The court would require the university to prove the student was guilty beyond reasonable doubt, as in criminal proceedings
options.D: The court would uphold the expulsion if the university acted in accordance with its own published procedures, even if those procedures are unfair

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: Option B is correct. Although the relationship may be contractual, universities are public authorities subject to JR when exercising powers affecting fundamental rights. Natural justice requires notice, an opportunity to be heard, and fair consideration (FLK1-CAL-043). Option A is incorrect — the fact that a relationship is contractual does not exclude JR entirely; quasi-judicial functions can be reviewed. Option C is incorrect — criminal standards of proof do not apply to disciplinary proceedings; the test is balance of probabilities. Option D is incorrect — even if the university followed its own procedures, if those procedures breach natural justice, the decision may be quashed.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: A local authority publishes a planning policy: 'Retirement homes for over-60s will ordinarily be approved in the Green Belt, provided they meet health and safety standards.' A developer submits a planning application for a retirement home that meets all health and safety standards. The authority's planning committee rejects the application, stating that it has changed its policy. The developer argues legitimate expectation. Which statement best reflects the modern law?

options.D: The developer may have a substantive legitimate expectation that the authority will follow its published policy, and the authority may be required to justify the change or grant approval to avoid unfairness
options.A: The developer has no claim because legitimate expectations cannot arise from published planning policies
options.B: The developer has a procedural legitimate expectation to a fair hearing, but no substantive entitlement to approval under the old policy
options.C: The developer's claim fails because the authority always retains discretion to change its policies

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: Option C is correct. Published policies can generate substantive legitimate expectations. The authority is not strictly bound, but if it changes policy mid-application, it must justify the change or treat the applicant fairly (e.g. by granting the benefit or providing a transition). The test is whether there is unfairness, not absolute entitlement (FLK1-CAL-044, Bhatt Murphy [2008]). Option A is incorrect — policies do generate expectations. Option B is incorrect — only a procedural expectation understates the position; substantive expectations are possible. Option D is incorrect — while the authority has discretion, this does not negate legitimate expectation; it requires fair exercise of discretion.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: A claimant becomes aware of an unlawful decision by a local authority to grant planning permission for a development in their neighbourhood. The claimant is very busy and does not file a claim until 4 months after the decision. The authority argues that the claim should be struck out for delay. Under CPR Part 54, what is the legal position?

options.D: The claim must be filed within three months of the date when the claimant knew or ought to have known of the decision; failure to comply means the court may refuse permission unless the claimant can show good reason for delay
options.A: The claim must be heard because the claimant has a right to review unlawful decisions regardless of time
options.B: The claim must be struck out because the three-month time limit is absolute and cannot be extended
options.C: The claim may proceed if the claimant pays compensation to the authority for the delay

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: Option C is correct. The three-month time limit (known as 'promptly') is set by CPR Part 54 (FLK1-CAL-046). The court may refuse permission for late claims unless there is good reason for the delay and no prejudice to others. The test is discretionary, not absolute. Option A is incorrect — time limits apply even to unlawful decisions. Option B is incorrect — the limit is not absolute; it can be extended in appropriate circumstances. Option D is incorrect — compensation is not a mechanism for overcoming time limits in JR.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: After successful judicial review, a planning officer has quashed an unlawful planning permission. However, the authority refuses to reconsider the application or take any further action, citing pressure of work. The claimant seeks to compel reconsideration. Which remedy would be most appropriate?

options.C: A mandatory order requiring the authority to reconsider the application
options.A: An injunction preventing the authority from issuing further planning permissions
options.B: A declaration that the original decision was unlawful
options.D: Damages for the delay in reconsideration

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: Option B is correct. A mandatory order (formerly called an order of mandamus) compels a public authority to perform a public duty (FLK1-CAL-047). Here, the authority has a duty to reconsider after quashing. Option A is incorrect — an injunction is not the appropriate remedy for compelling performance of a specific duty. Option C is incorrect — a declaration has already been made (the permission was quashed). Option D is incorrect — damages are available under HRA s.8 if Convention rights are breached, but not automatically in JR.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: Parliament passes Statute A, which expressly provides: 'This Act protects the right to a fair trial and may only be repealed by express words in a later Act.' Twenty years later, Parliament passes Statute B, which contains comprehensive new court procedures but makes no mention of the fair trial protections in Statute A. Does Statute B impliedly repeal the relevant provisions of Statute A?

options.D: No, because Statute A is a constitutional statute and cannot be impliedly repealed; if Parliament intended to repeal it, it must use express words
options.A: Yes, because all Acts of Parliament are equal and Statute B, being later, automatically repeal Statute A
options.B: No, because Statute A contains an entrenchment clause that binds Parliament
options.C: Yes, unless the court finds that Statute A is 'more important' than Statute B

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: Option B is correct. Although Statute A's own entrenchment clause is not binding (Parliament cannot bind itself), the fact that it is a constitutional statute means the common law doctrine against implied repeal applies (Thoburn v Sunderland [2002]) (FLK1-CAL-009). Express words are required to repeal it. Option A is incorrect — this ignores the distinction between constitutional and ordinary statutes. Option C is incorrect — entrenchment clauses are not binding in the UK, but the common law doctrine still applies. Option D is incorrect — the court does not rank statutes by 'importance'; it applies a rule based on their constitutional character.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: A recent Act of Parliament provides: 'Notwithstanding any other Act, the government shall not negotiate any international trade agreement without a referendum.' Six months later, Parliament passes the 'Trade Facilitation Act 2026', which authorises the government to negotiate and ratify trade agreements at its discretion, with no mention of referenda. Which principle of constitutional law best explains the courts' likely response?

options.B: The courts would apply the principle of implied repeal — later inconsistent legislation impliedly repeals earlier legislation unless the latter is a constitutional statute
options.A: The courts would strike down the 2026 Act because it violates the rule of law by removing a fundamental safeguard
options.C: The courts would refuse to interfere because both Acts are supremely valid and there is no hierarchical relationship between them
options.D: The courts would apply the principle of express repeal — the 2026 Act is only valid if it explicitly states it repeals the earlier Act

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: Option B is correct. This scenario tests the doctrine of parliamentary sovereignty and implied repeal. If the earlier Act is not a constitutional statute, a later inconsistent Act will impliedly repeal it — even without express words. The courts cannot strike down either Act (rejecting A). There is an implied repeal doctrine that applies to ordinary statutes (C is therefore incomplete/incorrect in suggesting no hierarchy). Option D is wrong because implied repeal does not require express words — that is precisely what distinguishes it from express repeal (FLK1-CAL-002, FLK1-CAL-010).

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: In R (Miller) v Secretary of State for Exiting the EU [2017], the Supreme Court held that the government could not trigger Article 50 without an Act of Parliament. Which constitutional principles did the court rely upon, and does it represent a limitation on parliamentary sovereignty?

options.D: The court relied on the principle of legality to require statutory authority for the prerogative action; this does not limit parliamentary sovereignty because Parliament could authorize the action by statute
options.A: The court held that the EU itself had authority to prevent UK withdrawal; this is a limitation on sovereignty because it subordinates Parliament to EU law
options.B: The court struck down the prerogative action as unconstitutional; this represents a fundamental change to the doctrine of parliamentary sovereignty
options.C: The court relied on common law requiring consent of the Scottish Parliament; this contradicts Dicey's theory of sovereignty

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: Option C is correct. Miller [2017] applied the principle of legality: the prerogative cannot be used to change domestic law without statutory authority (FLK1-CAL-011, FLK1-CAL-CASE-012). This does not limit parliamentary sovereignty; it shows sovereignty working through Parliament. If Parliament enacted the trigger, the prerogative would be valid. Option A is incorrect — the court did not subordinate UK law to EU law; it required UK statutory authority. Option B is incorrect — the prerogative action was not deemed unconstitutional; it was simply not authorized by law. Option D is incorrect — while the Sewel convention was discussed, the court's main holding did not turn on it, and it did not override parliamentary sovereignty.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: A statute gives a minister power to make decisions 'in the interests of public health.' The minister considers not only public health but also wider government economic policy. A campaigner challenges the decision as unlawful, arguing the minister considered irrelevant economic considerations. Under modern principles of JR, which statement best captures the court's likely approach?

options.C: The court would apply a test of proportionality: the minister is entitled to balance factors, but only if the decision is rationally connected to the statutory purpose and not disproportionate to the stated aim
options.A: The court would refuse to review the decision because the minister has discretion to consider all factors
options.B: The court would quash the decision because economic policy is never relevant to health decisions
options.D: The court would apply the test from Associated Provincial Picture Houses v Wednesbury Corp — the decision is only quashed if it is so irrational that no reasonable minister could reach it

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: Option B is correct. Modern law applies a proportionality test to discretionary decisions, especially where fundamental rights or important interests are engaged (FLK1-CAL-040). The minister can consider multiple factors, but the decision must be rationally connected to the statutory purpose and the balance must be proportionate. Option A is incorrect — discretion is not unlimited; it must be exercised for proper purposes and with relevant considerations. Option C is incorrect — economic considerations may be relevant to health, depending on context. Option D is incorrect — while Wednesbury still applies to some decisions, proportionality is now the preferred test where justified.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: A statute defines 'parent' as 'a person who has the biological capacity to bear children.' A same-sex couple, who have adopted children, argue this definition violates their right to family life (Art 8 ECHR) and equal treatment (Art 14 ECHR). Can a court cure the incompatibility through s.3 interpretation? Which factor is most important in making this choice?

options.C: The court can read the definition to include 'adoptive parent' under s.3, as this interpretation is compatible with the statutory scheme and achieves Convention compliance
options.A: The court must always prefer s.3 interpretation, even if it requires reading words into the statute that are not there
options.B: The court must make a s.4 declaration because same-sex couples are not biological parents and cannot meet the literal words of the statute
options.D: The court can proceed under s.3 only if Parliament explicitly consents to the reinterpretation

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: Option C is correct. Section 3 allows a broad interpretation where it does not fundamentally depart from the statutory purpose. If 'parent' can be read as including adoptive parents (consistent with the overall purpose of protecting family units), s.3 can be applied (FLK1-CAL-028, Ghaidan v Godin-Mendoza [2004]). This is the preferred approach to achieve Convention compliance. Option A is incorrect — s.3 has limits; it cannot be used if it departs from fundamental features. Option B is incorrect — statutory language can be read more broadly if it achieves Convention compatibility. Option D is incorrect — Parliament's consent is not required; the court has the power to interpret.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: A prison service policy states: 'Prisoners serving life sentences may apply for parole review after 25 years; all such applications will receive a full, fair hearing.' A prisoner, after 25 years, applies for parole review. The parole board holds a hearing, but pre-judges the outcome without genuinely considering the prisoner's case. The prisoner argues both procedural impropriety and breach of legitimate expectation. Which statement best describes the overlap?

options.D: Procedural impropriety (unfair hearing) and legitimate expectation (entitlement to fair consideration) overlap, and the court may apply both grounds; the breach of either is sufficient to quash and require reconsideration
options.A: Procedural impropriety and legitimate expectation are identical; the court would apply only one ground
options.B: The prisoner has only a procedural legitimate expectation, not a right to a fair hearing in administrative law
options.C: The court would dismiss the claim because the parole board has discretion over all parole decisions

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: Option C is correct. Procedural impropriety (breach of natural justice/fair hearing) and procedural legitimate expectation (entitlement to fair process) often overlap in practice (FLK1-CAL-042, FLK1-CAL-044). The court may apply both grounds; a breach of either is sufficient to quash and require a fair reconsideration. Option A is incorrect — they are distinct grounds, though they often coincide. Option B is incorrect — administrative law does protect fair hearing rights. Option D is incorrect — discretion must be exercised fairly; it is not absolute.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.subtopic: Legislative competence and the Sewel convention
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: The Scottish Parliament has exclusively devolved powers to legislate on education. The UK Parliament passes an Act that amends education law in Scotland without securing the consent of the Scottish Parliament (contrary to the Sewel convention). A Scottish education group seeks judicial review of the UK Act. Based on Miller [2017], what would be the court's likely response?

options.D: The court would recognise the Sewel convention as a constitutional principle but would not enforce it as law; the Act would remain valid, though the breach may be significant politically
options.A: The court would require the UK Parliament to obtain Scottish consent before the Act becomes law
options.B: The court would not hear the claim because devolution matters are outside the scope of JR
options.C: The court would quash the UK Act as unconstitutional because it breaches the Sewel convention

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: Option D is correct. R (Miller) v SSHD [2017] confirmed that the Sewel convention is recognized by the courts but is not legally enforceable (FLK1-CAL-049). A breach has political consequences, not legal ones. The Act would remain valid. Option A is incorrect — the court cannot impose a legal requirement to obtain consent; that is a political matter. Option B is incorrect — devolution matters are within the scope of JR, but the enforcement of conventions is not. Option C is incorrect — conventions are not enforceable in law.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.subtopic: Fettering of discretion and the balance of competing interests
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: A local authority adopts a policy that states: 'Due to budget constraints, all applications for discretionary rate relief will be refused unless the applicant can demonstrate exceptional hardship.' A small business applies for relief but is refused summary consideration of its circumstances, simply because it did not demonstrate hardship at the threshold stage. The business argues the authority has fettered its discretion. Which statement best explains the legal position?

options.D: The authority has fettered its discretion by setting a threshold that prevents individual consideration; although the policy provides a general framework, each application must receive individual assessment, and summary refusal based on the threshold alone is unlawful
options.A: The policy is lawful because budget constraints always justify refusing discretionary benefits
options.B: The authority has a blanket right to refuse relief due to budget constraints and does not need to consider individual applications
options.C: The authority has not fettered discretion because it has acknowledged the possibility of exceptions ('unless exceptional hardship')

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: Option D is correct. A policy that sets a threshold but then refuses applications summary without individual consideration has fettered discretion (FLK1-CAL-037). Although policies are lawful and budgets are relevant, the authority must genuinely consider whether the circumstances of a particular applicant warrant an exception. Automatic refusal at the threshold is unlawful. Option A is incorrect — budget constraints are relevant but do not override the duty to consider individual cases. Option B is incorrect — the authority must exercise discretion individually, not mechanically apply budget constraints. Option C is incorrect — the presence of an exception clause does not cure the fettering if the authority never genuinely considers exceptions.

Criminal Law & Practice.subject: Criminal Law & Practice
Criminal Law & Practice.stem: Which of the following best describes the 'voluntary act' requirement in criminal law?

options.A: A willed and consciously controlled act, as opposed to a reflex or involuntary movement
options.B: An act performed with the intention to commit a criminal offence
options.C: An act that the defendant willingly admits to in a police interview
options.D: An act that would not have been committed but for the defendant's criminal motive

Criminal Law & Practice.explanation: The correct answer is A. The voluntary act requirement (Hill v Baxter [1958]) means the act must be willed and consciously controlled. B is incorrect because voluntary act is about the nature of the conduct, not the defendant's intention regarding the offence itself. C is incorrect because admissions in interviews are evidence of liability, not the definition of voluntary act. D is incorrect because motive is not relevant to whether an act is voluntary — involuntary acts like reflexes remain involuntary regardless of the defendant's underlying motivation.

Criminal Law & Practice.subject: Criminal Law & Practice
Criminal Law & Practice.stem: A senior care worker at a nursing home fails to report that a colleague has been physically assaulting an elderly resident. The care worker had no other involvement. Under what circumstances could the care worker be liable for this omission?

options.A: The care worker would only be liable if there was a statutory duty to report, contractual duty to act, or a duty arising from relationship or voluntary assumption of care
options.B: The care worker would automatically be liable as an accessory for failing to report a crime
options.C: The care worker would not be liable for failing to report, as there is no general 'Good Samaritan' duty in English law
options.D: The care worker would be liable only if the resident dies as a result of the assault

Criminal Law & Practice.explanation: The correct answer is A. Criminal liability for omissions requires identification of a legal duty to act. The five recognised duty situations are statutory, contractual, relationship-based, voluntary assumption, or prior dangerous act (R v Gibbins and Proctor [1918]; R v Stone and Dobinson [1977]; R v Miller [1983]). A care worker's professional duty of care to residents may create such a duty. B is incorrect because omissions liability requires a specific duty, not automatic accessory liability for silence. C is incorrect because, while there is no general Good Samaritan duty, specific duty situations create liability for omissions. D is incorrect because omissions liability can arise for protective omissions without requiring a specific consequence.

Criminal Law & Practice.subject: Criminal Law & Practice
Criminal Law & Practice.stem: Which of the following correctly states the 'but for' test of factual causation?

options.A: But for the defendant's act, would the prohibited consequence have occurred? If 'no', factual causation is established
options.B: The defendant's act must be the sole cause of the prohibited consequence
options.C: The defendant must have intended the consequence that would 'but for' their act have occurred anyway
options.D: The defendant's act must be 'but for' the victim's actions that contributed to the harm

Criminal Law & Practice.explanation: The correct answer is A. The 'but for' test (R v White [1910]) asks whether the consequence would have occurred without the defendant's act. If the answer is 'no', factual causation is established. If the consequence would have happened anyway, factual causation fails. B is incorrect because causation does not require the defendant's act to be the sole cause — multiple causes can operate together. C is incorrect because intention regarding the 'but for' scenario is irrelevant; the test is purely factual. D is incorrect because the 'but for' test focuses on the defendant's act, not comparative causation with the victim's actions.

Criminal Law & Practice.subject: Criminal Law & Practice
Criminal Law & Practice.stem: A defendant shoots at a victim, intending to cause serious injury, and is certain that their shot will kill. According to the test for direct intention, which statement is correct?

options.A: The defendant has direct intention to cause serious injury, and the certainty of death is only relevant to oblique intention
options.B: The defendant has direct intention to kill because they foresaw death as a virtually certain consequence
options.C: The defendant has direct intention to kill because that is the natural consequence of their act
options.D: The defendant has no direct intention to kill since their aim was only to cause serious injury

Criminal Law & Practice.explanation: The correct answer is A. Direct intention (R v Mohan [1976]) exists where the consequence is the defendant's purpose or aim. Here, the defendant's aim is to cause serious injury. The fact that death is virtually certain is not relevant to direct intention to kill — it would be relevant to oblique intention (foresight as virtually certain). B is incorrect because virtually certain foresight is the test for oblique intention, not direct intention. C is incorrect because the natural consequences of an act are not relevant — direct intention requires the consequence to be the defendant's aim. D is incorrect because while there is no direct intention to kill, the defendant does have direct intention to cause GBH.

Criminal Law & Practice.subject: Criminal Law & Practice
Criminal Law & Practice.stem: A defendant stabs a victim in the abdomen. The victim is taken to hospital where medical treatment is provided, but a junior doctor negligently administers the wrong antibiotic. The victim develops a serious infection as a result of the negligent treatment and dies. The defendant argues the doctor's negligence has broken the chain of causation. Will this argument succeed?

options.A: Yes, because any medical negligence is a novus actus interveniens that automatically breaks the chain
options.B: No, because the defendant takes the victim as found, including medical treatment received
options.C: No, because the negligence would need to be 'so palpably wrong' as to break the chain, and mere negligence does not satisfy this
options.D: Yes, because the doctor's act was a free and deliberate act by a third party

Criminal Law & Practice.explanation: The correct answer is C. Medical negligence only breaks the chain of causation where it is 'so palpably wrong' (R v Smith [1959]; R v Jordan [1956] is the narrow exception where treatment was grossly negligent and the original wound was no longer an operating cause). A is incorrect because medical negligence does not automatically break the chain. B is incorrect because the thin skull rule applies to the victim's characteristics and foreseeable reactions, not to medical treatment decisions. D is incorrect because while the doctor's act is a third party act, mere negligence does not meet the threshold of being so palpably wrong.

Criminal Law & Practice.subject: Criminal Law & Practice
Criminal Law & Practice.stem: A defendant plants a bomb on an aircraft to claim insurance money. They intend to destroy the plane but hope against hope that all passengers will somehow escape. The plane crashes, killing all 200 passengers. The defendant claims they did not intend to kill anyone, only to destroy the aircraft. How would a court analyse the defendant's mens rea for murder?

options.A: The defendant's hope that people would escape negates mens rea unless they were reckless as to death
options.B: The defendant has mens rea for murder if they foresaw death as a virtually certain consequence of destroying the aircraft, even though that was not their aim
options.C: The defendant has no mens rea for murder because they did not intend the deaths and hoped people would escape
options.D: The defendant has mens rea for murder only if they subjectively wanted the passengers to die

Criminal Law & Practice.explanation: The correct answer is B. Under the test for oblique intention (R v Woollin [1999]), the defendant has mens rea for murder if they foresaw death as a virtually certain consequence, even though it was not their direct aim. The defendant's hope against hope is subjectively irrelevant once they foresaw death as virtually certain. A is incorrect because hope is irrelevant to the test; the question is what was virtually certain, not what was hoped for. C is incorrect because foresight as virtually certain (not direct intention) is sufficient for murder. D is incorrect because murder does not require the defendant to want the deaths; foresight of virtual certainty suffices.

Criminal Law & Practice.subject: Criminal Law & Practice
Criminal Law & Practice.stem: What is the key difference in mens rea between section 18 (wounding with intent) and section 20 (malicious wounding) of the Offences Against the Person Act 1861?

options.A: Section 18 requires intention to wound; section 20 requires intention to wound or recklessness
options.B: Section 18 requires intention to cause GBH; section 20 requires recklessness as to some harm
options.C: Both require the same mens rea but differ in the severity of the harm caused
options.D: Section 18 requires gross negligence; section 20 requires intention to cause injury

Criminal Law & Practice.explanation: The correct answer is B. Section 18 requires intention to cause GBH (R v Cunningham [1982]), while section 20 requires only subjective recklessness as to some harm — not necessarily GBH (R v Mowatt [1968]). A is incorrect because section 18 is not limited to intention to wound; it includes intention to cause GBH. C is incorrect because the mens rea differs fundamentally between the two offences. D is incorrect because neither offence requires gross negligence; they are crimes of intention or recklessness.

Criminal Law & Practice.subject: Criminal Law & Practice
Criminal Law & Practice.stem: A shop owner discovers that one of their employees has taken money from the till without permission. At what point does appropriation of the money occur under section 3 of the Theft Act 1968?

options.A: When the employee assumes any of the owner's rights over the money, including the right of possession (R v Gomez [1993])
options.B: When the employee forms the intention to take the money
options.C: When the employee takes physical possession of the money
options.D: When the employee removes the money from the premises

Criminal Law & Practice.explanation: The correct answer is A. Appropriation is defined as any assumption of the owner's rights, as established in R v Gomez [1993] and clarified in R v Morris [1984]. It can occur at the moment of assumption, even if that is when the defendant gains possession. B is incorrect because intention alone is not appropriation; there must be an act assuming rights. C is incorrect because while physical possession may be when appropriation occurs, the correct legal test is assumption of rights, not possession per se. D is incorrect because physical removal is not a requirement; appropriation can occur anywhere on the premises.

Criminal Law & Practice.subject: Criminal Law & Practice
Criminal Law & Practice.stem: A defendant is spotted by police moving towards a parked car with a screwdriver and a hammer, intending to break into it. The defendant is arrested before touching the car. Has the defendant attempted theft?

options.A: Yes, because the defendant was in possession of tools and had intention to steal
options.B: No, because the defendant had not yet done anything that constitutes a 'more than merely preparatory' act (R v Geddes [1996])
options.C: It depends on whether the defendant had fully formed the intention and undertaken all preparatory steps
options.D: Yes, because possession of burglary tools with intention to use them is itself an attempt

Criminal Law & Practice.explanation: The correct answer is B. Under section 1 Criminal Attempts Act 1981 and R v Geddes [1996], an attempt requires the defendant to have gone beyond merely preparatory acts. Carrying tools and approaching a location are preparatory; the defendant must have gone further — such as actually attempting to break into the car. A is incorrect because intention and tools are insufficient without going beyond preparatory conduct. C is incorrect because the test is not about fully formed intention or completion of all preparatory steps but about crossing the threshold from preparation to attempt. D is incorrect because possession of tools with intent is itself preparation, not attempt.

Criminal Law & Practice.subject: Criminal Law & Practice
Criminal Law & Practice.stem: Which of the following best describes the classification of criminal offences in England and Wales?

options.A: Crimes are classified as summary, either-way, or indictable depending on the appropriate forum for trial
options.B: All crimes are classified as either misdemeanours or felonies depending on their severity
options.C: Crimes are classified based solely on the maximum sentence available
options.D: Crimes are classified based on whether the victim is a citizen or foreigner

Criminal Law & Practice.explanation: The correct answer is A. Modern English law classifies offences into three categories: summary (tried in Magistrates' Courts), either-way (can be tried in either Magistrates' or Crown Court), and indictable (tried in Crown Court). The classification determines the appropriate forum. B is incorrect because the common law distinctions between misdemeanours and felonies have been abolished in modern law. C is incorrect because while maximum sentence is relevant, the classification system is based on the appropriate forum for trial. D is incorrect because the nature of the victim is irrelevant to the classification system.

Criminal Law & Practice.subject: Criminal Law & Practice
Criminal Law & Practice.stem: A defendant is lawfully defending their home from an intruder. Under section 76 of the Criminal Justice and Immigration Act 2008, what is the key test the court must apply?

options.A: Whether the force used was objectively proportionate to the threat posed
options.B: Whether the defendant honestly believed they were in danger and the force used was reasonable in the circumstances as the defendant believed them to be
options.C: Whether the defendant's actions were what a reasonable person would have done in identical circumstances
options.D: Whether the force used would be classified as 'grossly disproportionate' to the threat

Criminal Law & Practice.explanation: The correct answer is B. Section 76 CJIA 2008 establishes the test as (1) honest belief that force is necessary in defence, and (2) force reasonable in the circumstances as the defendant believed them to be (subjective test). A is incorrect because the test is not objective proportionality but rather reasonableness in the defendant's believed circumstances. C is incorrect because the test is what is reasonable in the defendant's believed circumstances, not what an objective reasonable person would do. D is incorrect because 'grossly disproportionate' is the threshold for householder cases (s.76(5A)) — a higher threshold — but the basic test is whether the force was reasonable.

Criminal Law & Practice.subject: Criminal Law & Practice
Criminal Law & Practice.stem: A defendant commits robbery after being threatened that they and their family will be killed unless they do so. The defendant was reasonably afraid and had no safe avenue of escape. However, the threat was made by someone the defendant had voluntarily become involved with through gang membership. Will the defence of duress succeed?

options.A: Yes, because the threat meets the test of death or serious injury and the defendant reasonably believed the threat
options.B: No, because the defendant foresaw the risk of such threats when they joined the gang — the duress was not self-induced but was a foreseeable consequence of their own choices
options.C: No, because duress is not available to any member of a gang regardless of circumstances
options.D: Yes, but only if the defendant can prove they tried to escape the gang and were threatened despite their attempts to leave

Criminal Law & Practice.explanation: The correct answer is B. In R v Hasan [2005], the Court held that duress is not available where the defendant has voluntarily become involved in a criminal group and foreseably exposed themselves to the risk of threats. While the immediate threat may meet the formal criteria, the overall defence fails. A is incorrect because meeting the threat test is not sufficient if duress is excluded on other grounds. C is incorrect because gang membership alone does not automatically exclude duress; the test is whether the defendant foresaw the risk of such threats. D is incorrect because the defence fails on foresight grounds, not on the basis of failed attempts to escape.

Criminal Law & Practice.subject: Criminal Law & Practice
Criminal Law & Practice.stem: A defendant, voluntarily intoxicated, commits assault with the intention of causing serious injury. Can the defendant rely on intoxication as a defence?

options.A: Yes, because voluntary intoxication can always negate mens rea
options.B: No, because assault is a basic intent crime where intoxication does not negate the required mens rea
options.C: Yes, but only if the defendant can prove the intoxication was involuntary
options.D: No, because intoxication is never a defence to any crime under English law

Criminal Law & Practice.explanation: The correct answer is B. Under R v Majewski [1977], voluntary intoxication is not available as a defence to basic intent crimes (crimes of recklessness where the prohibited act is itself the harm). Assault is a basic intent crime; the mens rea is intention or recklessness as to the assault, and the assault itself is the actus reus. A is incorrect because voluntary intoxication is only potentially relevant to specific intent crimes. C is incorrect because voluntary intoxication is not available to basic intent crimes regardless of whether it was voluntary or involuntary. D is incorrect because intoxication can be a defence to specific intent crimes (e.g., specific intent murder, theft) where it negates the specific intent element.

Criminal Law & Practice.subject: Criminal Law & Practice
Criminal Law & Practice.stem: A defendant suffers from diabetes and commits a serious assault during a hypoglycaemic episode (caused by low blood sugar). The defendant had no knowledge that this condition could cause such episodes. Will the defence of insanity succeed?

options.A: Yes, because the defendant suffered from a disease of the mind (diabetes) that caused the assault
options.B: Yes, but only if the defendant had previously experienced such episodes and failed to take medication
options.C: No, because hypoglycaemia is not a disease of the mind under the M'Naghten Rules; it is a temporary physiological condition
options.D: No, because the defendant did not know they could suffer such episodes, so they could not have foreseen the risk

Criminal Law & Practice.explanation: The correct answer is C. The M'Naghten Rules require a 'defect of reason from disease of the mind'. Hypoglycaemia is a temporary physiological condition, not a disease of the mind. The defendant's diabetes is a disease, but the acute hypoglycaemic episode is a temporary metabolic state. In similar cases, courts have distinguished between internal conditions (disease) and external causes. A is incorrect because diabetes alone does not trigger the defence; the acute condition causing the assault must itself be a disease of the mind. B is incorrect because prior knowledge is not the test for insanity. D is incorrect because foresight is not the test; the issue is whether the condition is a disease of the mind.

Criminal Law & Practice.subject: Criminal Law & Practice
Criminal Law & Practice.stem: What is the mens rea required for murder under English law?

options.A: Intention to cause grievous bodily harm or intention to kill
options.B: Recklessness as to whether death will be caused
options.C: Gross negligence in circumstances where death is a foreseeable consequence
options.D: Intention to cause any harm to the victim

Criminal Law & Practice.explanation: The correct answer is A. Murder requires mens rea of intention to kill or intention to cause grievous bodily harm (R v Cunningham [1982]; R v Vickers [1957]). B is incorrect because recklessness is not sufficient for murder; it may support manslaughter. C is incorrect because gross negligence manslaughter is a different offence with different mens rea. D is incorrect because the intention must be to kill or cause GBH, not merely any harm.

Criminal Law & Practice.subject: Criminal Law & Practice
Criminal Law & Practice.stem: A defendant and their spouse have had a distant, loveless marriage for 15 years. The defendant discovers that the spouse has been having an affair for the past two months. The defendant immediately confronts the spouse in anger and, in a heated argument, kills them. Can the defendant rely on the defence of loss of control under sections 54-55 of the Coroners and Justice Act 2009?

options.A: Yes, because the defendant suffered an immediate loss of control triggered by the discovery of infidelity
options.B: No, because the defence requires an abnormality of mental functioning, which the defendant does not have
options.C: No, because loss of control caused by sexual infidelity alone is explicitly excluded by the Act
options.D: Yes, provided the defendant did not incite the loss of control and was not already of unsound mind

Criminal Law & Practice.explanation: The correct answer is C. Section 55(6)(c) of the Coroners and Justice Act 2009 explicitly excludes loss of control triggered solely by sexual infidelity. A is incorrect because while there may be an immediate loss of control, the defence is barred by the statutory exclusion. B is incorrect because the issue is not absence of abnormal functioning but the specific exclusion for infidelity-based triggers. D is incorrect because even if other requirements are met, the sexual infidelity exclusion applies regardless of incitement or soundness of mind.

Criminal Law & Practice.subject: Criminal Law & Practice
Criminal Law & Practice.stem: What are the key elements of gross negligence manslaughter as established in R v Adomako [1994]?

options.A: A duty of care to the victim, breach of that duty, a risk of death created by the breach, and gross negligence
options.B: Recklessness as to death and an obvious and serious risk of death
options.C: Intention to cause serious injury that results in death
options.D: Knowledge that the defendant's act would cause harm and failure to prevent that harm

Criminal Law & Practice.explanation: The correct answer is A. The Adomako test for gross negligence manslaughter comprises: (1) duty of care owed by defendant to victim; (2) breach of that duty; (3) the breach created a risk of death (not just injury); and (4) the breach was so negligent as to be 'gross'. B is incorrect because gross negligence manslaughter does not require recklessness; it is based on negligence. C is incorrect because that describes a form of murder or serious assault, not manslaughter. D is incorrect because knowledge and failure to prevent are not the basis; the test focuses on negligent breach of duty.

Criminal Law & Practice.subject: Criminal Law & Practice
Criminal Law & Practice.stem: A defendant deliberately touches a victim's arm in a threatening manner, causing the victim to suffer an acute anxiety disorder resulting in psychiatric injury. No physical injury is sustained. Is this assault and battery, or does it constitute assault occasioning actual bodily harm under section 47 of the Offences Against the Person Act 1861?

options.A: It is assault and battery only, because ABH requires at least some minimal physical injury like bruising
options.B: It is assault and battery only, because psychiatric injury is not recognised as actual bodily harm
options.C: It constitutes section 47 ABH because psychiatric injury caused by the defendant's act counts as actual bodily harm (Chan-Fook [1994])
options.D: It constitutes ABH, and additionally the defendant is liable for the common assault element with no need to prove separate mens rea for the injury itself

Criminal Law & Practice.explanation: The correct answer is C. In R v Chan-Fook [1994], the Court held that actual bodily harm includes recognisable psychiatric injury. Section 47 requires that the assault or battery cause ABH, but the mens rea is only intention or recklessness as to the assault or battery itself, not as to the injury (R v Savage [1992]). A is incorrect because ABH is not limited to physical injury. B is incorrect because psychiatric injury is explicitly recognised as ABH. D is correct in principle (the mens rea point) but option C is the better answer because it directly identifies that psychiatric injury counts as ABH.

Criminal Law & Practice.subject: Criminal Law & Practice
Criminal Law & Practice.stem: A defendant takes a bicycle from a street after its owner has abandoned it for 18 months with no intention to return and retrieve it. The defendant honestly believes the owner has abandoned it and does not intend to return. Under the test for dishonesty established in Ivey v Genting [2017], is the defendant's conduct dishonest?

options.A: Yes, because taking another person's property without permission is inherently dishonest
options.B: No, if the defendant's conduct is not dishonest by the standards of ordinary, honest people and the defendant knew their conduct would be regarded as dishonest by those standards
options.C: Yes, because the defendant's honest belief about abandonment is irrelevant to the objective test
options.D: No, because the bicycle has been abandoned and therefore has no owner to steal from

Criminal Law & Practice.explanation: The correct answer is B. Under Ivey v Genting [2017], dishonesty is determined by: (1) what the defendant's actual state of mind was (subjective); and (2) whether that conduct is dishonest by the standards of ordinary, honest people (objective), AND the defendant knew their conduct would be so regarded. If the defendant genuinely and reasonably believed the bicycle was abandoned, the conduct may not be dishonest by the standard test. A is incorrect because taking property can be non-dishonest if there is a genuine belief in entitlement or abandonment. C is incorrect because the defendant's honest belief is highly relevant to both the subjective and objective stages of the test. D is incorrect because even abandoned property belongs to someone unless genuinely lost.

Criminal Law & Practice.subject: Criminal Law & Practice
Criminal Law & Practice.stem: A defendant watches a friend assault a stranger, knowing the assault is taking place but doing nothing to stop it. After the assault, the defendant tells the friend, 'I knew you were going to do that and I'm glad you did.' Will the defendant be liable as a secondary party (accessory) to the assault?

options.A: Yes, because the defendant's silence and subsequent approval constitutes assistance or encouragement
options.B: No, because merely watching, even with knowledge, is not sufficient; there must be evidence of intentional assistance or encouragement before or during the offence (R v Jogee [2016])
options.C: Yes, because the defendant did not instigate or plan the assault and therefore cannot be liable as a secondary party
options.D: No, because the defendant's knowledge of the assault and failure to report it makes them an accessory after the fact

Criminal Law & Practice.explanation: The correct answer is B. Following R v Jogee [2016], secondary liability requires intentional assistance or encouragement. Foresight alone is not sufficient. Mere watching and subsequent approval do not constitute intentional assistance or encouragement unless they occurred before or during the offence in a way that actually assisted or encouraged the principal offender. A is incorrect because foresight and approval are not the test; intentional assistance/encouragement is required. D is incorrect because there is no such offence as 'accessory after the fact' in English law; the approach focuses on assistance/encouragement before or during the offence. C is incorrect because a secondary party need not have instigated or planned the offence; but they must have intentionally assisted or encouraged.

Criminal Law & Practice.subject: Criminal Law & Practice
Criminal Law & Practice.stem: A defendant is charged with burglary (an indictable either-way offence). At the first hearing, the prosecution applies for the defendant to be remanded in custody. What grounds could the court rely on under the Bail Act 1976 to refuse bail?

options.A: The court can refuse bail if there are substantial grounds for believing the defendant will fail to surrender, commit further offences, interfere with witnesses, or obstruct justice
options.B: The court can refuse bail if it has reasonable suspicion that the defendant committed the offence
options.C: The court can refuse bail only if it believes the defendant is a risk to the public's safety
options.D: The court can refuse bail at any time for any reason, provided the defendant is brought before a judge within 72 hours

Criminal Law & Practice.explanation: The correct answer is A. Under the Bail Act 1976, the court can refuse bail if satisfied there are substantial grounds for believing the defendant will (1) fail to surrender; (2) commit further offences; (3) interfere with witnesses or obstruct justice; or (4) fail to comply with conditions. B is incorrect because reasonable suspicion of guilt does not satisfy the bail test. C is incorrect because risk to the public is one ground among several; the test is broader. D is incorrect because bail cannot be refused arbitrarily; there must be substantial grounds for belief in one of the specific categories.

Criminal Law & Practice.subject: Criminal Law & Practice
Criminal Law & Practice.stem: Under the Criminal Procedure and Investigations Act 1996 (CPIA), what is the primary duty of the prosecution regarding disclosure?

options.A: The prosecution must disclose all material that might reasonably be considered capable of undermining the case for the prosecution or assisting the case for the accused (primary disclosure duty)
options.B: The prosecution must disclose everything in its possession, including every witness statement and piece of evidence
options.C: The prosecution must disclose only the evidence it intends to use at trial
options.D: The prosecution has no obligation to disclose anything until the defendant makes a defence statement

Criminal Law & Practice.explanation: The correct answer is A. Under section 3 CPIA 1996, the prosecution has a continuing duty of primary disclosure: to disclose all material that might reasonably be considered capable of undermining the prosecution's case or assisting the defence. B is incorrect because not everything need be disclosed, only material that meets the disclosure test. C is incorrect because the disclosure duty extends beyond evidence the prosecution will use at trial. D is incorrect because primary disclosure is a duty of the prosecution independent of the defence making a statement.

Criminal Law & Practice.subject: Criminal Law & Practice
Criminal Law & Practice.stem: A defendant is sentenced to five years' imprisonment for armed robbery. Which of the following best describes the sentencing court's multiple purposes in imposing such a sentence?

options.A: Punishment, rehabilitation, deterrence (specific and general), public protection, and reparation — a pluralistic approach to sentencing with multiple purposes
options.B: Punishment only, as the primary purpose of sentencing is to inflict deserved harm on the offender
options.C: Rehabilitation only, as modern sentencing aims to reform offenders and reintegrate them into society
options.D: Deterrence only, as the primary function of sentencing is to discourage others from committing similar offences

Criminal Law & Practice.explanation: The correct answer is A. The sentencing framework encompasses multiple purposes: punishment/retribution (deserved consequence), rehabilitation (reform of the offender), deterrence (both specific — to the individual offender — and general — to deter others), public protection (incapacitation), and reparation (compensation/amends). B is incorrect because punishment is only one purpose among several in modern sentencing. C is incorrect because while rehabilitation is important, it is not the sole purpose. D is incorrect because deterrence is one purpose among several, not the only one.

Criminal Law & Practice.subject: Criminal Law & Practice
Criminal Law & Practice.stem: A defendant with diagnosed borderline personality disorder (BPD) kills their estranged partner after months of unhealthy obsession and emotional dysregulation, which are characteristic of their condition. A psychiatrist testifies that the defendant's BPD substantially impaired their ability to form rational judgements and exercise self-control, and that this condition provides a substantial explanation for the killing. The defendant had normal cognitive capacity and knew the act was unlawful. Will the defence of diminished responsibility succeed?

options.A: Yes, because the defendant's mental condition substantially impaired their ability to exercise self-control and provides substantial explanation for the killing
options.B: No, because the defendant knew their action was unlawful and had normal cognitive capacity
options.C: No, because borderline personality disorder is a personality disorder, not a recognised medical condition sufficient for the defence
options.D: Yes, but only if the defendant was unable to understand the nature of their actions

Criminal Law & Practice.explanation: The correct answer is A. Under section 2 of the Homicide Act 1957 as amended by the Coroners and Justice Act 2009, diminished responsibility requires: (1) abnormality of mental functioning from a recognised medical condition; (2) substantially impaired ability to understand, form rational judgements, or exercise self-control; and (3) substantial explanation for the killing. BPD is a recognised medical condition, and the evidence shows substantial impairment of self-control and judgment, providing substantial explanation. B is incorrect because the test does not require cognitive inability to understand unlawfulness; it focuses on the ability to exercise self-control and form rational judgements. C is incorrect because personality disorders arising from recognised medical conditions can constitute abnormality for this defence. D is incorrect because the test does not require inability to understand the nature of the act, only substantial impairment of judgment or self-control.

Criminal Law & Practice.subject: Criminal Law & Practice
Criminal Law & Practice.stem: A defendant has sexual intercourse with another person. The defendant honestly but unreasonably believes the other person has consented. Under the Sexual Offences Act 2003, will the defendant be convicted of rape?

options.A: No, because the defendant's honest belief in consent, regardless of reasonableness, is a defence
options.B: Yes, because consent is a matter for the complainant to decide, and honesty without reasonableness is insufficient
options.C: No, if the defendant's belief is based on the other person's behaviour or words at the time
options.D: Yes, because rape requires intention to have intercourse, which is satisfied regardless of consent issues

Criminal Law & Practice.explanation: The correct answer is B. Under section 1 of the Sexual Offences Act 2003, the defendant must have a reasonable belief in consent. An honest but unreasonable belief is not a defence — the test is both subjective (honest) and objective (reasonable). A is incorrect because reasonableness is required as well as honesty. C is incorrect because behaviour or words are circumstantial; the test is whether the belief in consent is reasonable in all the circumstances. D is incorrect because the issue is not intention to have intercourse but reasonable belief in consent.

Criminal Law & Practice.subject: Criminal Law & Practice
Criminal Law & Practice.stem: A defendant, a financial advisor, advises clients to invest in a purported hedge fund. The defendant knows the fund is fictitious and no genuine investment exists, yet continues to accept payments from clients over two years. Is the defendant guilty of fraud by false representation under section 2 of the Fraud Act 2006?

options.A: No, because fraud by false representation requires a single representation, not a course of conduct
options.B: Yes, because the defendant has made an express representation (the investment exists) that is false, knowing it to be false or being reckless as to its falsity, with intent to make a gain
options.C: Yes, but only if the clients actually relied on the false representation
options.D: No, because the representation is not explicit but rather implied, and implied representations are covered only under fraud by abuse of position

Criminal Law & Practice.explanation: The correct answer is B. Section 2 Fraud Act 2006 requires: (1) a false representation (express or implied); (2) made dishonestly; (3) knowing it to be false or being reckless as to falsity; and (4) with intent to make a gain or cause loss. The defendant clearly satisfies all elements. A is incorrect because fraud requires a representation but a course of conduct involving multiple representations can constitute the crime. C is incorrect because reliance is not required — the crime is committed upon making the false representation with the necessary mens rea. D is incorrect because explicit statements that the fund exists constitute express representations.

Criminal Law & Practice.subject: Criminal Law & Practice
Criminal Law & Practice.stem: Two defendants agree to import cannabis. One defendant is a police officer acting under cover, who never intends to actually go through with the importation but is gathering evidence against the other defendant. Can both defendants be convicted of conspiracy to import cannabis?

options.A: No, because the police officer never intended to actually carry out the offence and therefore lacked the necessary mens rea for conspiracy
options.B: Yes, because a conspiracy exists once two people agree with the requisite mens rea to commit the offence
options.C: Yes, but the police officer can rely on a statutory defence as an undercover agent
options.D: No, because one of the parties to the conspiracy is an officer of the law and therefore cannot form the mens rea for conspiracy

Criminal Law & Practice.explanation: The correct answer is A. Conspiracy requires that the conspirators agree with the intention to pursue the agreed course of conduct. An undercover officer who never intends the course of conduct to be carried out lacks the necessary mens rea. The absence of shared intention to pursue the unlawful course of conduct between at least two parties means there is no conspiracy. B is incorrect because both parties must intend the course of conduct; a police officer pretending to agree does not satisfy this. D is incorrect because officers of the law can form the mens rea for conspiracy; the issue here is the officer's genuine lack of intent. C is incorrect because while statutory defences may exist for undercover agents, the immediate issue is the absence of mens rea.

Criminal Law & Practice.subject: Criminal Law & Practice
Criminal Law & Practice.stem: A defendant is charged with actual bodily harm (ABH) under section 47 OFFPAP 1861, an either-way offence. At the allocation hearing in the Magistrates' Court, the defendant desires trial in the Crown Court, but the magistrates consider the case suitable for trial in the Magistrates' Court. Can the defendant insist on Crown Court trial?

options.A: No, the magistrates' view on suitability is determinative and the defendant must accept trial in the Magistrates' Court
options.B: Yes, the defendant has an automatic right to elect Crown Court trial for all either-way offences under the Magistrates' Courts Act 1980 s.19
options.C: Yes, but only if the defendant can persuade the magistrates that the facts are exceptionally complex or serious
options.D: No, unless the defendant is charged with a second or subsequent either-way offence within the past year

Criminal Law & Practice.explanation: The correct answer is B. Under the Magistrates' Courts Act 1980 section 19, for either-way offences, if after the allocation hearing the defendant declines summary trial (or it is declined by the prosecution), the defendant has the right to elect trial in the Crown Court. The defendant's election cannot be overridden by the magistrates' view on suitability. A is incorrect because the defendant's right to elect is not dependent on the magistrates' view. C is incorrect because the right is not conditional on complexity or seriousness; it is a statutory right. D is incorrect because the right is not limited to repeat offenders.

Criminal Law & Practice.subject: Criminal Law & Practice
Criminal Law & Practice.stem: A defendant pleads guilty to grievous bodily harm contrary to section 20 OFFPAP 1861 and has relevant previous convictions. The court is considering whether to impose a custodial sentence or a community order with a rehabilitation requirement. Under the Sentencing Code, what is the test the court must apply?

options.A: The court must assess whether the offence is serious enough to require custody in the interests of justice; a community order is permissible only if the court is satisfied that it is commensurate with the seriousness of the offence
options.B: The court must impose a custodial sentence if the defendant has any previous convictions, as they evidence a pattern of offending
options.C: The court must impose the shortest custodial sentence possible to balance punishment with the hope of rehabilitation
options.D: The court has discretion to impose either a custodial or community sentence without applying a specific legal test

Criminal Law & Practice.explanation: The correct answer is A. The Sentencing Code (building on the Criminal Justice Act 2003) establishes that a court must not impose a custodial sentence unless satisfied that the offence is serious enough to require it in the interests of justice. Conversely, a community order is available if commensurate with seriousness. B is incorrect because previous convictions are relevant to culpability and risk assessment but do not mandate custody. C is incorrect because the test is not about imposing the shortest custodial sentence but about whether custody is required at all. D is incorrect because the court must apply the statutory test of seriousness.

Criminal Law & Practice.subject: Criminal Law & Practice
Criminal Law & Practice.stem: An advocate is instructed to represent one defendant in a fraud trial. Prior to trial, the advocate is approached by a co-defendant asking for representation in the same trial. Both defendants face similar charges but it is foreseeable that their defences could diverge significantly — the first defendant may seek to shift responsibility to the co-defendant. What professional duty does the advocate owe?

options.A: The advocate must decline instructions to represent the co-defendant because a conflict of interest is foreseeable, and continuing to represent only the first defendant creates an irreconcilable conflict
options.B: The advocate can represent both defendants provided they each give informed consent and the case remains straightforward
options.C: The advocate can represent both defendants without difficulty, as the duty of confidentiality prevents them from using information from one client against the other
options.D: The advocate must represent both defendants if asked, as refusing would violate the cab rank principle

Criminal Law & Practice.explanation: The correct answer is A. Professional conduct rules require advocates to avoid conflicts of interest. Where one defendant's defence may involve implicating a co-defendant, a conflict is foreseeable and irreconcilable. The advocate cannot properly serve both clients' interests, and informed consent is insufficient to cure such structural conflicts. B is incorrect because consent cannot overcome an irreconcilable conflict of interest. C is incorrect because the duty of confidentiality does not resolve the underlying conflict of divergent interests at trial. D is incorrect because the cab rank principle does not require acceptance of instructions where a conflict exists.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.stem: Under LP(MP)A 1989 s.2, what is the essential requirement for a valid contract for sale of land?

options.A: The contract must be in writing and signed by both parties, incorporating all material terms.
options.B: The contract must be in writing and signed by the seller only.
options.C: The contract must be in writing but need not identify all material terms if agreed orally.
options.D: The contract must be signed by both parties but can be in electronic format only.

Property Practice.explanation: LP(MP)A 1989 s.2 requires that a contract for sale of land must be in writing and signed by or on behalf of both parties. All material terms must be incorporated into the written document. Part-performance is no longer available to avoid this requirement post-s.2.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.stem: Under LRA 2002, what is the basic position regarding the priority of registered dispositions?

options.A: Registered dispositions take priority in accordance with the order of registration, subject to overriding interests.
options.B: Later registered dispositions always take priority over earlier unregistered interests regardless of notice.
options.C: Priority is determined by the order in which parties became aware of earlier interests.
options.D: Unregistered interests always take priority if the owner had actual occupation.

Property Practice.explanation: LRA 2002 s.29 provides that a registrable disposition for valuable consideration takes priority according to the order of registration. However, overriding interests under Sch.3 (such as actual occupation under para 2) can take priority despite not being registered.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.stem: Which search is most likely to reveal whether a commercial lease is subject to forfeiture proceedings?

options.A: Local Authority Search.
options.B: Water and Drainage Search.
options.C: Bankruptcy Search against the landlord.
options.D: None of the standard searches — this requires enquiry of the landlord and review of lease terms.

Property Practice.explanation: Standard searches (Local Authority, Water, Drainage, Chancel Repair) do not reveal forfeiture proceedings. This information must be obtained by: (1) reviewing the lease terms and conditions; (2) enquiring of the current occupier about breaches; (3) reviewing notices served; and (4) conducting company searches if the landlord is a company in financial distress.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.stem: What is the SDLT rate payable on the purchase of a residential property for £250,000 by a first-time buyer with no previous property interest?

options.A: 1% on the amount over £125,000.
options.B: 2% on the whole amount.
options.C: 5% on the whole amount.
options.D: 0% — fully relieved as first-time buyer relief.

Property Practice.explanation: First-time buyer relief under FA 2003 (as amended) provides full relief on residential property up to £425,000 for first-time buyers. For purchases at £250,000, a first-time buyer is fully relieved of SDLT. Higher rates (+3%) apply only to additional residential properties for non-first-time buyers.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.stem: Under the Standard Conditions of Sale (6th edition), what is the default completion time frame after exchange of contracts?

options.A: 10 working days.
options.B: 14 calendar days.
options.C: 5 working days.
options.D: The contract specifies the precise date; the standard conditions do not set a default period.

Property Practice.explanation: Standard Condition 6.1.1 provides that completion takes place on the date specified in the contract. Absent express agreement, the default position is 5 working days after exchange. The completion date is critical and must be agreed in writing before exchange.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.stem: Under LRA 2002 Sch.3 para 2, what TWO conditions must be satisfied for an interest based on actual occupation to override a registered disposition?

options.A: The owner must be in actual occupation and their interest must be known to the buyer OR would have been obvious on reasonable inspection.
options.B: The owner must be in occupation and have paid council tax.
options.C: The owner must be in occupation for at least 12 months and have paid rent.
options.D: The owner must be in occupation and must have a written notice of the interest.

Property Practice.explanation: LRA 2002 Sch.3 para 2 requires: (1) the person claiming the interest is in actual occupation at the time of the disposition; and (2) the interest is known to the buyer, or would have been obvious on reasonable inspection. Both conditions must be satisfied. Actual occupation alone is insufficient if the interest would not have been apparent.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.stem: Under LTA 1954 s.24, what is the legal effect when a business tenancy ends?
