options.A: The tenancy ends but continues to subsist unless the landlord serves a s.25 notice opposing renewal.
options.B: The tenancy immediately terminates and the tenant must vacate without further rights.
options.C: The tenancy automatically continues indefinitely unless either party seeks termination.
options.D: The tenancy ends but the tenant is entitled to remain in occupation pending agreement of a new lease.

Property Practice.explanation: LTA 1954 s.24 provides that a business tenancy continues beyond its contractual termination date unless proper notice procedures are followed. The landlord must serve a s.25 notice (or tenant must serve s.26 request) to initiate the renewal process. Without such notice, the tenancy continues at the existing terms.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.stem: When does the mortgagee's power of sale under LPA 1925 s.101 become exercisable?

options.A: Immediately upon execution of the mortgage deed.
options.B: When three months' rent is in arrears or the mortgagor breaches any term.
options.C: When capital is 3 months overdue OR interest is 2 months arrears OR any covenant is breached.
options.D: Only after the mortgagee obtains a court order.

Property Practice.explanation: LPA 1925 s.103 specifies when the power of sale becomes exercisable: (1) when capital money is due and unpaid for at least 3 months after becoming due; (2) when interest is 2 months in arrears; or (3) when a covenant of the mortgage is breached. The power arises under s.101 but does not become exercisable until one of these conditions is met.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.stem: Under the UK Finance Mortgage Lenders' Handbook, when must a solicitor disclose a conflict of interest arising from acting for both lender and borrower?

options.A: Immediately upon discovery of any matter that could affect the lender's position or create a conflict.
options.B: Only if the lender specifically requests disclosure.
options.C: Only if the borrower is purchasing at a significant undervalue compared to the valuation.
options.D: Never — the Handbook permits joint representation without disclosure if the borrower consents.

Property Practice.explanation: The UK Finance Mortgage Lenders' Handbook requires solicitors acting for both lender and borrower to disclose conflicts immediately. Key conflict situations: (1) undervalue purchases; (2) non-disclosure to lender; (3) undisclosed incentives or gifts. Immediate disclosure is required; in serious conflicts, the solicitor should decline to act for one party.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.stem: Under POCA 2002 s.330, when must a solicitor submit a Suspicious Activity Report (SAR) to the National Crime Agency?

options.A: As soon as reasonably practicable after knowing or suspecting money laundering, before proceeding with the transaction.
options.B: Only if the solicitor is certain that money laundering has occurred.
options.C: Within 7 days of forming a suspicion of money laundering.
options.D: Only if the client refuses to provide satisfactory explanation for the source of funds.

Property Practice.explanation: POCA 2002 s.330 requires disclosure of any knowledge or suspicion of money laundering to the National Crime Agency. The disclosure must be made as soon as reasonably practicable and must be made before proceeding with the transaction. Failure to disclose (without appropriate consent) constitutes a criminal offence. The threshold is suspicion, not certainty.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.stem: A property investor purchases a second residential property (not their main residence) for £350,000. There are no reliefs available. What SDLT is payable?

options.A: £14,250 (graduated rates plus 3% higher rate surcharge).
options.C: £13,250 (3% higher rate on the whole amount).
options.D: £15,000 (standard graduated rates at standard residential rates only).

Property Practice.explanation: For a second residential property, the higher rate of 3% applies (in addition to standard rates). Calculation: £0-£125k at 0% = £0; £125k-£250k at 5% = £6,250; £250k-£350k at 5% + 3% higher rate = £7,500 at higher rate = £14,250 total SDLT.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.stem: A buyer takes a registrable disposition (legal estate) for valuable consideration. An unregistered equitable interest in the same property was created before the disposition but was not overridden. Which statement best explains the priority position?

options.A: The registered disposition always takes priority because it is registered.
options.B: The unregistered equitable interest takes priority because it was created first.
options.C: Both interests have equal status and must be dealt with by contribution agreement.
options.D: The registered disposition takes priority unless the equitable interest is a Sch.3 overriding interest (e.g. actual occupation).

Property Practice.explanation: LRA 2002 s.29 provides that a registrable disposition for valuable consideration takes free of all unregistered interests EXCEPT those listed in Sch.3 (overriding interests). Sch.3 para 2 (actual occupation) is the most common exception. If the unregistered equitable owner is in actual occupation and the interest was known to the buyer or obvious on inspection, it overrides the registered disposition.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.stem: Two dispositions of the same registered land are presented for registration on the same day: a first mortgage and a second charge. Both are correct in form. In what order should they be registered, and what is the priority outcome?

options.A: They can be registered in any order; priority is determined by date of execution.
options.B: The first to be presented for registration is registered first and takes priority.
options.C: The first mortgage should be registered first (as it provides security); the second charge has no priority rights.
options.D: Both must be registered in the order of presentation; priority is determined by this order regardless of the charge structure.

Property Practice.explanation: Dispositions are registered in the order in which they are presented for registration. Both charges would be registered in order of presentation, with the first registered charge taking priority over the second. The character of the charge (mortgage vs second charge) does not alter the registration order rule — it is simply order of presentation that determines priority under LRA 2002.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.stem: A property is held on trust by A and B (trustees) for C (beneficiary with a beneficial interest in the equitable estate). The property is sold to a buyer. For C's interest to be overreached, which condition must be met?

options.A: The buyer must conduct a search showing the trust on the register.
options.B: The buyer must have actual notice of the trust.
options.C: C's interest must be disclosed to the buyer in writing.
options.D: Capital money from the sale must be paid to BOTH trustees (or a trust corporation).

Property Practice.explanation: Overreaching under LPA 1925 ss.2 and 27 requires: (1) sale by at least two trustees (or a trust corporation); and (2) capital money paid to the trustees. If these conditions are met, even a buyer with notice of the trust takes the legal estate free of the beneficiary's equitable interest — the interest is shifted to the capital money. Failure to pay two trustees means the interest is NOT overreached.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.stem: A tenant under a commercial lease with a forfeiture clause is three months in arrears with rent. The landlord wishes to forfeit the lease. What procedural step must the landlord take BEFORE peaceable re-entry?

options.A: Serve a s.146 LPA 1925 notice specifying the breach and giving a reasonable time to remedy.
options.B: For non-payment of rent only, no notice is required — peaceable re-entry is permitted immediately.
options.C: Obtain a court order for forfeiture.
options.D: Serve a forfeiture notice via registered post and wait 14 days.

Property Practice.explanation: For non-payment of rent specifically, s.146 LPA 1925 does NOT require notice before forfeiture (this is the exception to the general s.146 notice requirement). However, for other breaches, s.146 notice is mandatory. If the breach is remediable, the tenant must be given a reasonable opportunity to remedy. For straightforward arrears of rent, a landlord can proceed to peaceable re-entry or court forfeiture without prior s.146 notice.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.stem: A landlord wishes to oppose the grant of a new business tenancy under LTA 1954 on the ground that the landlord intends to demolish and rebuild the property. What must the landlord establish?

options.A: The landlord must have a firm intention to demolish; it need not be certain or immediate.
options.B: The landlord must prove that demolition is reasonably likely within a reasonable time after the tenancy ends.
options.C: The landlord must demonstrate that demolition cannot be carried out without possession of the demised property.
options.D: The landlord must obtain planning permission for redevelopment before opposing renewal.

Property Practice.explanation: LTA 1954 s.30(1)(f) permits opposition to renewal where the landlord intends to demolish or reconstruct AND that demolition/reconstruction cannot be carried out without possession. The landlord must establish: (1) firm intention to demolish/reconstruct; (2) reasonable expectation this will occur within a reasonable time; and (3) that the works cannot be undertaken without obtaining possession of the property. The leading case is Espresso Coffee Machine Co v Aintree plc.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.stem: Under LTA 1954 s.30(1)(e), a landlord opposes renewal on the ground of persistent substantial breaches of covenant. The tenant admits one late payment of service charges (subsequently remedied). Can the landlord succeed on this ground?

options.A: Yes — any breach is sufficient.
options.B: No — the ground requires persistent and substantial breaches; a single remedied breach is insufficient.
options.C: Yes — service charge breaches are automatically considered substantial.
options.D: No — service charge breaches are never grounds for opposition.

Property Practice.explanation: LTA 1954 s.30(1)(e) requires 'persistent substantial breaches' of covenant. A single isolated breach, even if later remedied, is unlikely to satisfy this test. The court has held that the breaches must be repeated, substantial in character, and show a pattern of non-compliance. One late payment of service charges would not constitute 'persistent' breaches sufficient to ground opposition.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.subtopic: Conflict of Interest — Buyer and Lender
Property Practice.stem: A solicitor is instructed to act for both buyer and lender in a purchase transaction. The buyer discloses that the property is being purchased at £100,000 below the lender's valuation. The lender has not been informed. What must the solicitor do?

options.A: Continue with the transaction and do not disclose the undervalue to the lender.
options.B: Immediately disclose the undervalue to the lender and obtain consent to proceed.
options.C: Advise the buyer to disclose the undervalue to the lender; if the buyer refuses, cease acting for the buyer.
options.D: Decline to act for the lender immediately and proceed only for the buyer.

Property Practice.explanation: The UK Finance Mortgage Lenders' Handbook specifically identifies undervalue purchases as creating a conflict of interest. The solicitor must: (1) immediately disclose the undervalue to the lender; (2) obtain the lender's express consent to proceed; and (3) if the lender does not consent or withdraws the mortgage offer, cease acting. Non-disclosure is a breach of the Handbook and could expose the solicitor to liability.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.stem: In a chain of three property transactions (A→B, B→C, C→D), what is the critical requirement for simultaneous exchange of contracts?

options.A: All three contracts must be signed by the parties on the same calendar day.
options.B: Contracts must be exchanged in a coordinated manner so that each party becomes bound only when all parties in the chain are bound.
options.C: The middle party (B) must hold the contracts of all parties until release of all funds.
options.D: Exchange can occur individually; simultaneous exchange is not essential in a chain.

Property Practice.explanation: In a chain, simultaneous exchange is essential to protect each party's position. Contracts must be exchanged in a coordinated manner — typically via telephone with the cooperation of all solicitors — so that each party is bound only when all parties in the chain are committed. Failure to achieve simultaneous exchange exposes parties to risk (e.g., if the first transaction falls through, others may be unable to proceed). This is achieved through careful sequencing and telephone conversations releasing contracts together.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.stem: During the requisitions on title phase, the buyer's solicitor discovers that a covenant affecting the property (not disclosed in the contract) permits only residential use, yet the property has been used for a small commercial office for five years. The seller produces no evidence of discharge or waiver. What is the position?

options.A: The buyer must complete; the covenant is presumed obsolete after five years of use.
options.B: The buyer is not entitled to rescind; the buyer can only claim damages.
options.C: This is a title defect; the buyer may rescind if the seller cannot remove the defect by producing a discharge or indemnity policy.
options.D: The seller is in breach of contract and must refund the purchase price.

Property Practice.explanation: A restrictive covenant is a defect in title if it remains binding and enforceable. Five years' non-enforcement does not discharge the covenant. The seller must either: (1) produce evidence of discharge (formal deed); (2) obtain a waiver from those with the benefit of the covenant; or (3) provide a restrictive covenant indemnity insurance policy. If the seller cannot remove the defect, the buyer is entitled to rescind unless agreeing to proceed subject to the defect and a price reduction.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.stem: A solicitor has submitted a SAR to the NCA regarding a client's property transaction. Before the NCA has responded, the client asks the solicitor why the transaction has not proceeded. Can the solicitor disclose to the client that an SAR has been submitted?

options.A: No — s.333A POCA 2002 prohibits 'tipping off' by disclosing that an SAR has been submitted, subject to statutory exceptions.
options.B: Yes — the client has a right to know why the transaction is delayed.
options.C: Yes — but only after receiving consent from the NCA.
options.D: No — but the solicitor can give a vague explanation of 'compliance issues' without naming the NCA.

Property Practice.explanation: POCA 2002 s.333A prohibits 'tipping off' — disclosure of the fact that an SAR has been submitted to the NCA if this would prejudice the NCA's investigation. This is a strict liability offence. The solicitor cannot disclose to the client that an SAR has been made, except where: (1) the NCA consents; (2) the disclosure is to a professional adviser for legal advice; or (3) the disclosure is to another financial institution for AML compliance purposes.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.stem: The seller fails to complete on the agreed completion date. Five days later, the buyer serves notice requiring completion within 5 working days. The seller still fails to complete. What is the buyer's primary remedy?

options.A: The buyer can only claim damages for breach of contract.
options.B: The buyer can rescind the contract and claim damages.
options.C: The buyer must complete the purchase even if the seller has failed to do so.
options.D: The buyer is entitled to specific performance of the contract.

Property Practice.explanation: If the seller fails to complete, the buyer's primary remedy is specific performance — an order requiring the seller to complete the sale. Damages are an inadequate remedy for breach of a unique asset (the property). The buyer serves notice to complete (giving 5+ working days); if the seller still fails, the buyer can pursue specific performance. Rescission is available but is a remedy of last resort; specific performance is the preferred remedy in property contracts.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.stem: A solicitor is acting for a trust (the purchaser). The trustee is known to the solicitor, but one beneficial owner's identity is unknown. What action must the solicitor take to comply with MLR 2017?

options.A: Proceed with the transaction; the MLR 2017 does not require identification of beneficial owners.
options.B: Obtain identity verification for the trustee and proceed; beneficial owners need not be verified.
options.C: Identify and verify the identity of all beneficial owners (those with 25%+ interest) before proceeding.
options.D: Obtain a trustee indemnity insurance policy instead of identifying beneficial owners.

Property Practice.explanation: MLR 2017 Regulation 5 requires Customer Due Diligence (CDD) for beneficial owners — persons who ultimately own or control 25% or more of the entity. If a trust is the purchaser, the solicitor must identify and verify the identity of all beneficial owners (trust beneficiaries with 25%+ interest). Failure to do so is a breach of AML obligations and may constitute failure to disclose money laundering.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.stem: A developer purchases Plot A for £2m and Plot B for £1.2m in separate but related transactions (linked transactions under FA 2003 s.108). The buyer intends to develop the two plots together. Are the purchases linked, and how should SDLT be calculated?

options.A: The transactions are not linked; SDLT is calculated separately on each property.
options.B: The transactions are linked; consideration is aggregated (£3.2m) for SDLT purposes, but Multiple Dwellings Relief (MDR) does not apply.
options.C: The transactions are linked; consideration is aggregated (£3.2m), and if the buyer is developing multiple dwellings, MDR may apply to reduce the rate.
options.D: Linked transactions always result in surcharge; SDLT is calculated at the highest rate on the aggregated consideration.

Property Practice.explanation: FA 2003 s.108 treats acquisitions of land within a certain period for the same or connected purposes as 'linked transactions.' The consideration is aggregated for SDLT purposes. If the acquisition is of land for development as multiple dwellings, Multiple Dwellings Relief (MDR) under FA 2003 Sch.6B may apply, reducing the SDLT rate from 5% to lower graduated rates. The solicitor must identify linkage and claim MDR if applicable.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.stem: A fraudster impersonates the registered owner and sells the property to an innocent buyer for value. The buyer registers the disposition. The true owner later discovers the fraud and seeks to recover the property. What is the priority position?

options.A: The true owner can recover; the buyer's registration is void because it is based on fraud.
options.B: The true owner can recover; an unregistered interest based on fraud cannot be overreached or overridden.
options.C: The buyer takes free of the true owner's interest under LRA 2002 s.29 because the buyer is a registered proprietor for value.
options.D: The buyer takes the legal estate but the true owner retains an overriding interest of actual occupation if in occupation.

Property Practice.explanation: This is the controversial position under LRA 2002 s.29: even if the registered proprietor obtained the property through fraud, a disposition by that proprietor to a buyer for value takes free of interests of any prior owner (subject to Sch.3 overriding interests). The true owner cannot recover the property from an innocent registered buyer, though they may have a claim against the fraudster for damages. This reflects the policy of LRA 2002 to protect purchasers of registered land.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.stem: A tenant and landlord enter into a commercial lease with a clause purporting to exclude LTA 1954 protection. The clause merely states 'This lease is excluded from the protection of the Landlord and Tenant Act 1954.' Is this effective to contract out?

options.A: Yes — the clause clearly shows intention to contract out.
options.B: No — s.38A requires that the tenant be given statutory prescribed notice and must make a separate statutory declaration before or at the same time as entering into the lease.
options.C: Yes — but only if the lease is for a term of more than 14 years.
options.D: No — s.38A contracting out is now prohibited by recent statutory reform.

Property Practice.explanation: LTA 1954 s.38A(1)-(3) requires a strict procedural compliance for contracting out: (1) the landlord must serve a statutory prescribed notice on the tenant at least 14 days before the lease is entered; (2) the notice must comply with specific requirements (explain the effect of contracting out); and (3) the tenant must make a statutory declaration confirming they have received notice and understand the consequences. A mere clause in the lease is insufficient — the prescribed procedure is mandatory. Non-compliance means the tenant retains LTA 1954 protection.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.subtopic: Sch.3 Para 2 Overriding Interest vs Registered Charge
Property Practice.stem: A property is registered at HM Land Registry. The registered proprietor mortgages the property to Bank A and registers the charge. An unregistered interest based on actual occupation (Sch.3 para 2) arises before Bank A's charge is registered. What is the priority position?

options.A: The Sch.3 para 2 interest takes priority because it existed before the charge.
options.B: Bank A's registered charge takes priority because registration under LRA 2002 creates registered proprietor's interest.
options.C: Bank A's charge takes priority unless the holder of the Sch.3 interest was in occupation and visible to Bank A's surveyor.
options.D: The Sch.3 para 2 interest overrides the registered charge; priority is determined by order of acquisition, not registration.

Property Practice.explanation: A Sch.3 para 2 overriding interest (based on actual occupation) overrides ALL registered interests, including registered charges, regardless of when the charge is registered. If the person is in actual occupation and their interest is known to Bank A or would have been obvious on reasonable inspection, the interest overrides the charge registration. This reflects the principle that overriding interests 'trump' registered interests in priority. Bank A's remedy is against the valuer for negligence.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.subtopic: Property Fraud — Identity and Lender Indemnity
Property Practice.stem: A fraudster impersonates the true owner and instructs a solicitor to sell the property. The solicitor carries out identity checks but the fraudster provides forged identity documents. The property is sold to a lender as security. The lender later discovers the fraud and seeks an indemnity from the solicitor's professional indemnity insurer. What is likely the outcome?

options.A: The indemnity will be paid because the solicitor relied on documents provided by the fraudster.
options.B: The indemnity will be refused because the solicitor negligently failed to detect the fraud.
options.C: The indemnity will be paid only if the lender has a separate indemnity policy against fraud.
options.D: The indemnity will be paid in full because property fraud is a recognised risk covered by all policies.

Property Practice.explanation: Professional indemnity insurance typically excludes or limits cover for losses arising from fraudulent impersonation if the solicitor has failed to conduct proper identity verification. Modern PI policies include explicit fraud exclusions or require compliance with AML CDD procedures. If the solicitor accepted forged documents without scrutiny, the insurer may refuse the claim as a breach of policy conditions. The lender's remedy depends on whether they have a separate lender indemnity policy (which may also have exclusions for fraud by the borrower).

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.subtopic: Beneficial Ownership — Trust and Overreaching Failure
Property Practice.stem: A property is registered in the names of two registered proprietors (A and B) but the beneficial interest is held entirely by a third party, C, under a trust (only A and B are trustees). C is not in actual occupation. A and B sell the property to a buyer. Capital money is paid to A only. What is C's position?

options.A: C's beneficial interest is overreached because A and B are trustees, regardless of payment to one trustee only.
options.B: C's beneficial interest is NOT overreached because capital money must be paid to BOTH trustees (or a trust corporation).
options.C: C has an overriding interest under Sch.3 para 2 because C has a beneficial interest.
options.D: C's beneficial interest is overreached if A and B acted in good faith.

Property Practice.explanation: Overreaching under LPA 1925 ss.2 and 27 requires that capital money be paid to AT LEAST two trustees (or a trust corporation). Payment to A alone is insufficient — the capital must be paid to both A and B, or to one trustee and a trust corporation. Because overreaching has not been completed (capital paid to one trustee only), C's beneficial interest is NOT overreached and remains binding on the buyer. The buyer takes subject to C's interest and the buyer may be able to claim a charge against the purchase money in C's hands.

Property Practice.subject: Property Practice
Property Practice.subtopic: Alienation Covenant — s.19 LTA 1927 and 1995 Act
Property Practice.stem: A commercial lease contains an absolute covenant against assignment. The tenant wishes to assign to a proposed assignee with excellent covenant strength. Under LTA 1927 s.19, the landlord refuses consent arbitrarily (for reasons unrelated to the business use of the premises). Subsequently, the Landlord and Tenant (Covenants) Act 1995 applies to the lease on later assignments. What is the legal position?

options.A: The covenant remains absolute; the landlord is not required to give consent under either Act.
options.B: LTA 1927 s.19(1) implies a term that landlord consent cannot be unreasonably withheld; the 1995 Act overrides this for later assignments.
options.C: LTA 1927 s.19(1) implies a term that consent cannot be unreasonably withheld; the 1995 Act applies similar protections but allows landlords to impose reasonable conditions.
options.D: The 1995 Act automatically discharges the absolute covenant, permitting all assignments.

Property Practice.explanation: LTA 1927 s.19(1) converts an absolute covenant against assignment into a qualified covenant — the landlord's consent cannot be unreasonably withheld (but conditions can be imposed). The Landlord and Tenant (Covenants) Act 1995 (applying to post-1996 leases or where the parties elect) provides similar protection: the landlord must give consent unless a reasonable ground exists. Grounds include: (1) unsuitability of the assignee; (2) potential deterioration of the premises; (3) security concerns. The landlord cannot refuse on arbitrary grounds unrelated to the lease.

Land Law.subject: Land Law
Land Law.stem: What is the effect of registration at HM Land Registry?

options.A: Registration creates guaranteed title that is conclusive evidence of ownership, except for matters on the register or overriding interests
options.B: Registration merely records ownership without providing protection
options.C: Registration prevents all claims by others
options.D: Registration requires annual renewal

Land Law.explanation: Under LRA 2002, registration is guaranteed by the Crown. The registered proprietor's title is conclusive evidence, subject only to: (1) entries on the register, (2) overriding interests (Sch.3), (3) fraud or forgery. This is more protective than unregistered land.

Land Law.subject: Land Law
Land Law.stem: Under s.1 LPA 1925, which is NOT a legal estate?

options.A: A life estate
options.B: A fee simple absolute in possession
options.C: A term of years absolute
options.D: A fee simple absolute held in remainder

Land Law.explanation: Section 1(1) LPA 1925 creates only two legal estates: fee simple absolute in possession and term of years absolute. Life estates were abolished as legal estates and exist only in equity. Remainder interests are also equitable only.

Land Law.subject: Land Law
Land Law.stem: How does the benefit of a freehold covenant pass to successors?

options.A: The benefit passes at law if it accommodates the dominant land, or in equity through assignment, building scheme, or annexation
options.B: The benefit always passes automatically without formality
options.C: The benefit only passes if expressly assigned by deed
options.D: The benefit does not pass to successors

Land Law.explanation: The benefit of a freehold covenant passes through multiple routes: (1) at law — if it accommodates/benefits the dominant land; (2) in equity — through express assignment, building scheme, or annexation. Accommodation is essential — the covenant must benefit the land, not just the person.

Land Law.subject: Land Law
Land Law.stem: Which is an overriding interest under Schedule 3 LRA 2002?

options.A: A beneficial interest under a trust (unless protected by restriction)
options.B: A person in actual occupation of the property
options.C: An estate contract
options.D: A restrictive covenant (unless protected by notice)

Land Law.explanation: Schedule 3 LRA 2002 lists overriding interests that bind a disponee for value despite not being on the register. 'A person in actual occupation' is a key overriding interest. Actual occupation is determined objectively.

Land Law.subject: Land Law
Land Law.stem: Which is NOT a requirement for a valid easement per Re Ellenborough Park [1956]?

options.A: The dominant owner must be a natural person, not a company
options.B: There must be a dominant and servient tenement
options.C: The right must accommodate the dominant tenement
options.D: The right must not be substantially the same as proprietorship

Land Law.explanation: The four Re Ellenborough Park [1956] requirements are: (1) dominant and servient tenements; (2) accommodation of dominant tenement; (3) not owned by the same person; (4) not substantially equivalent to proprietorship. A company can be a dominant owner — there is no requirement that they be a natural person. This is a common misconception.

Land Law.subject: Land Law
Land Law.subtopic: Joint Tenancy vs Tenancy in Common
Land Law.stem: Which of the following is NOT one of the four unities required for a joint tenancy?

options.A: Unity of title — the same document
options.B: Unity of time — simultaneous vesting
options.C: Unity of interest — the same share
options.D: Unity of intention — each must intend to live together on the property

Land Law.explanation: The four unities are: (1) unity of title; (2) unity of time; (3) unity of interest; (4) unity of possession. There is no requirement of 'unity of intention to live together.' Joint tenants have survivorship rights; tenants in common do not.

Land Law.subject: Land Law
Land Law.stem: A tenant is 3 months in arrears. The landlord serves notice requiring payment within 14 days. The tenant pays on day 15. The landlord seeks to forfeit. What is the tenant's position?

options.A: The landlord can forfeit because the deadline was missed
options.B: The tenant must wait 6 months before seeking relief
options.C: Forfeiture is automatic; no relief is available
options.D: The tenant is entitled to relief because payment before actual forfeiture normally prevents it; courts routinely grant relief in equity

Land Law.explanation: Forfeiture for non-payment is governed by statute. If the tenant pays arrears before the landlord actually forfeits, the breach is waived and forfeiture cannot occur. Even if formal proceedings begin, courts routinely grant relief from forfeiture to a tenant who pays arrears plus costs (Dighton v Stead [2002]). Courts are reluctant to deprive a tenant of property for non-payment if the tenant remedies the breach.

Land Law.subject: Land Law
Land Law.stem: What is the key distinction between a licence and a lease?

options.A: A licence is created orally; a lease must always be by deed
options.B: A licence grants limited permission to enter land, whereas a lease grants exclusive possession for a term
options.C: A licence requires express written permission; a lease does not
options.D: A licence is revocable at will; a lease grants exclusive possession for a term, and is not revocable at will

Land Law.explanation: The key distinctions (from Street v Mountford [1985]): (1) Exclusive possession: a lease grants exclusive possession; a licence grants permission only and is revocable. (2) Certainty of term: a lease is for a definite term; a bare licence is revocable at will. Exclusive possession is the clearest indicator of a lease.

Land Law.subject: Land Law
Land Law.stem: An easement of right of way (granted 1950) has not been used for 45 years. The servient owner blocked it with a locked gate. Does the easement remain enforceable?

options.A: The easement is automatically extinguished by disuse
options.B: The easement is extinguished by prescription after 20 years of non-use
options.C: The easement remains unless formally released by deed
options.D: Non-use alone does not extinguish; but combined with the locked gate and clear intention to block, abandonment may be proved (Gale v Gale [1974])

Land Law.explanation: Mere non-use does not extinguish an easement. Abandonment requires both: (1) long non-use suggesting no intention to resume, and (2) an act showing intention to abandon (such as obstruction). Gale v Gale [1974] confirms this two-part test. Here, 45 years plus the locked gate may support abandonment, but the burden is high.

Land Law.subject: Land Law
Land Law.stem: A 1920 estate has a covenant restricting use to 'private dwellings only.' An owner seeks to convert to a small hotel. The original beneficiary cannot be traced. Under s.84 LPA 1925, what is likely?

options.A: The covenant must be discharged because the beneficiary cannot be traced
options.B: The court will refuse because the restriction reflects original estate intention
options.C: The court can only discharge if the owner pays compensation to unknown beneficiaries
options.D: The court may modify under s.84(1)(aa) if the character of the neighbourhood has changed and the restriction provides no practical benefit

Land Law.explanation: Section 84 LPA 1925 empowers the Upper Tribunal to modify or discharge restrictive covenants. Section 84(1)(aa) applies where: the restriction provides no practical benefit and neighbourhood character has changed. In Chorley v Boldy [2002], the court granted modification where the 1920s neighbourhood had changed materially.

Land Law.subject: Land Law
Land Law.stem: Under Wheeldon v Burrows, when is an easement implied by law?

options.A: When the right is continuous, apparent, used at time of transfer, and reasonably necessary for enjoyment
options.B: When parties expressly state they intend an easement to exist
options.C: When the right has been used for 10 years
options.D: When the parties have failed to register the easement

Land Law.explanation: Wheeldon v Burrows [1879] implies easements where: (1) continuous and apparent; (2) in actual use at transfer; (3) reasonably necessary for enjoyment. This applies to transfers and leases. The test is objective.

Land Law.subject: Land Law
Land Law.stem: What formality is required for a 7-year legal lease?

options.A: A deed is required under s.52 LPA 1925; s.54(2) only excuses formality for leases of 3 years or less
options.B: No formality is required if there is exclusive possession
options.C: Only a written agreement signed by both parties
options.D: Registration at HM Land Registry

Land Law.explanation: Section 52 LPA 1925 requires a deed for all legal estate transfers, including leases. Section 54(2) provides an exception: leases for 3 years or less at best consideration without taking possession can be oral. A 7-year lease requires a deed.

Land Law.subject: Land Law
Land Law.stem: What fundamental protection does equity grant to mortgagors?

options.A: The equity of redemption — the right to redeem by paying principal, interest, and costs, protected from clogs and fetters
options.B: The right to extend the loan period indefinitely
options.C: The right to pay only the principal amount without interest
options.D: The right to sell the property whenever desired

Land Law.explanation: The equity of redemption is the mortgagor's equitable right to recover mortgaged property by discharging the debt. It is protected from clogs and fetters (Knightsbridge Estates v Byrne [1939]). Courts will not permit terms that prevent, obstruct, or penalize redemption.

Land Law.subject: Land Law
Land Law.stem: A and B are joint tenants. A serves written notice on B: 'I sever the joint tenancy.' Two months later A dies. Where does A's interest pass?

options.A: To A's estate, because written notice under s.36(2) LPA 1925 effects severance immediately
options.B: To B by survivorship because A did not have time to complete the severance
options.C: The interest is forfeited because severance was not completed before death
options.D: To the Crown as there is no severance or surviving joint tenant

Land Law.explanation: Section 36(2) LPA 1925 (amended by TLATA 1996 s.36(3)) provides that written notice on the other joint tenant(s) effects severance immediately upon service. Once severed, the interest becomes tenancy in common and passes under A's will or intestacy, not by survivorship (Harris v Harris [1981]).

Land Law.subject: Land Law
Land Law.stem: A property owner has openly used a right of way across a neighbour's land without permission for 23 years. The neighbour now objects. What is the outcome?

options.A: The owner has no right because use was without permission
options.B: The owner has acquired a legal easement by prescription under the Prescription Act 1832 because 20 years of use is sufficient
options.C: The owner has only an equitable easement
options.D: The owner cannot acquire any right without express written grant

Land Law.explanation: The Prescription Act 1832 provides that use 'as of right' (openly, without secrecy, without force, without permission) for 20 years grants a legal easement. Twenty-three years exceeds this threshold. Use 'as of right' means the use appears authorized by right (not permission, not against law).

Land Law.subject: Land Law
Land Law.stem: What distinguishes a contractual licence from a bare licence?

options.A: A contractual licence is created by deed; a bare licence is oral
options.B: A contractual licence is revocable only in accordance with the contract; a bare licence is revocable at will
options.C: A contractual licence gives exclusive possession; a bare licence does not
options.D: A contractual licence binds successors; a bare licence is personal

Land Law.explanation: A bare licence is revocable at will. A contractual licence arises from an agreement and can only be revoked in accordance with the contract. If revoked in breach of contract, the licensee can claim damages. However, both remain personal and do not bind successors in title (Wood v Leadbitter [1845]).

Land Law.subject: Land Law
Land Law.stem: A restrictive covenant 'not to use for business' was created in 1950 and is on the register. The current owner wishes to use the property for retail. What is the owner's position?

options.A: The owner can ignore the covenant because it is outdated
options.B: The burden does not run with freehold land at law (Austerberry v Oldham), so the owner's liability depends on equitable enforcement and notice
options.C: The owner must obtain the beneficiary's permission
options.D: The covenant is void because it is too restrictive

Land Law.explanation: Austerberry v Oldham [1885] establishes: the burden of a freehold covenant does NOT run with freehold land at law. The burden only runs in equity where certain conditions are met — and only if the covenant is restrictive. This is a critical distinction from leases, where covenants run through privity of estate.

Land Law.subject: Land Law
Land Law.stem: A mortgagee seeks to exercise the power of sale. Must the mortgagee first obtain a court order?

options.A: Yes, a court order is always required before the mortgagee can exercise the power of sale
options.B: No, the mortgagee can exercise express or implied power of sale out of court, provided the power exists in the deed and the mortgage is in default
options.C: Only if the mortgagor objects to the sale
options.D: Only after the mortgagor has been in default for 12 months

Land Law.explanation: The mortgagee has a power of sale either express (in the mortgage deed) or implied under s.101 LPA 1925. Importantly, this power can be exercised OUT OF COURT — no court order is required. The mortgagee must be either in possession or the mortgage in default. This is more efficient than requiring a court order every time.

Land Law.subject: Land Law
Land Law.stem: An owner promises their nephew 'You can live in the cottage rent-free for life.' The nephew relies on this, spending substantial money improving it. The owner dies with no mention of the cottage in the will. Can the nephew claim an equitable interest?

options.A: No, because the promise was gratuitous and unenforceable
options.B: No, because proprietary estoppel only applies to formal written agreements
options.C: Yes, because proprietary estoppel operates where there is: (1) an assurance about a right to the property; (2) reliance on the assurance; (3) detrimental act; and (4) unconscionability (Thorner v Major [2009])
options.D: Yes, but only if the nephew was in actual occupation at the owner's death

Land Law.explanation: Proprietary estoppel (Thorner v Major [2009]) requires: (1) Assurance; (2) Reliance; (3) Detriment (money, labour); (4) Unconscionability. If established, the court has discretion to grant a remedy — full transfer, life interest, licence, or compensation. The promise need not be written or for consideration.

Land Law.subject: Land Law
Land Law.stem: A house is registered in A's sole name. A and B cohabited with a common agreement that the property would be jointly owned. B paid half the mortgage (capital). Has a constructive trust arisen?

options.A: No, because the property is registered in A's sole name
options.B: No, because B is not married to A
options.C: Yes, because Lloyds Bank v Rosset [1991] establishes two routes: express common intention plus detrimental reliance, or direct financial contribution; B satisfies both
options.D: Yes, but only if B can show intent to become a legal owner

Land Law.explanation: Lloyds Bank v Rosset [1991] establishes two routes for constructive trusts: (1) Express agreement/common intention plus detrimental reliance; (2) Direct financial contribution to purchase price. Here, B has: (a) express common intention, AND (b) direct financial contributions. B satisfies both and acquires an equitable interest. Sole registration in A's name does not defeat this.

Land Law.subject: Land Law
Land Law.stem: A wife charges the matrimonial home to secure her husband's business loan without independent advice. The husband defaults. Under RBS v Etridge [2001], what must the bank establish?

options.A: The bank must prove the wife agreed freely; if so, the charge is enforceable
options.B: The bank is not responsible for ensuring independent legal advice
options.C: The bank must: (1) give notice of the risk; (2) recommend independent legal advice; and (3) obtain confirmation that advice was given; if the bank fails these steps, the charge may be unenforceable
options.D: The wife cannot claim undue influence as a spouse is presumed to understand such transactions

Land Law.explanation: RBS v Etridge [2001] requires that where a lender lends to a guarantor, the bank must follow procedures: (1) inform the guarantor of maximum liability and recommend independent legal advice; (2) obtain a report from the guarantor's solicitor confirming advice was given; (3) if the bank fails these steps and undue influence is established, the charge may be unenforceable.

Land Law.subject: Land Law
Land Law.stem: A leaseholder assigns a 40-year lease to a purchaser. The lease contains a covenant to maintain the building. The new leaseholder does not maintain it. Can the landlord sue the new leaseholder?

options.A: No, because the landlord has no privity of contract with the new leaseholder
options.B: No, because the landlord must sue the original leaseholder
options.C: Yes, because privity of estate allows the landlord to sue the current tenant for covenant breaches that touch and concern the land
options.D: Only if the new leaseholder expressly assumed the covenants in the assignment deed

Land Law.explanation: For leases, leasehold covenants run with the land through privity of estate. When a lease is assigned, the new tenant becomes bound by all covenants (restrictive and positive) that touch and concern the land. A 'maintain the structure' covenant clearly touches and concerns the land. The new tenant is bound even without express assumption.

Land Law.subject: Land Law
Land Law.stem: Following establishment of proprietary estoppel, what is the court's approach to granting a remedy?

options.A: The court must always transfer full legal ownership
options.B: The court can only grant a licence
options.C: The court must require the claimant to pay fair market value
options.D: The court has discretion to grant whatever remedy satisfies the equity — full transfer, a lesser interest, or monetary compensation — depending on circumstances

Land Law.explanation: In proprietary estoppel, the court's role is to satisfy the equity created by the owner's assurance and the claimant's reliant detriment (Thorner v Major [2009], Jennings v Rice [2002]). The remedy varies with the facts. The court may declare a life interest, grant a licence, transfer the property, order payment representing reliance loss, or require purchase at a fixed price. The court asks: what is the minimum remedy to satisfy the equity?

Land Law.subject: Land Law
Land Law.stem: After 10 years of adverse possession of registered land, an applicant applies to be registered. The registered proprietor objects. When can the applicant be registered?

options.A: Immediately, because 10 years of occupation is sufficient
options.B: Only in exceptional circumstances under s.98 LRA 2002 — such as spent convictions or proprietary estoppel
options.C: After 20 years of occupation
options.D: Only if the proprietor consents

Land Law.explanation: Schedule 6 LRA 2002 creates a notification process. After 10 years, the applicant applies to the Land Registry. The Registry notifies the registered proprietor who has 65 days to object. If no objection, the applicant is registered. If an objection is filed, registration occurs only in 'exceptional circumstances' (s.98 LRA 2002).

Land Law.subject: Land Law
Land Law.stem: X and Y are joint tenants of a house. Y ceases paying the mortgage and occupies exclusively. X seeks to force a sale; Y objects. Under TLATA 1996 s.14-15, what is the likely outcome?

options.A: X can force sale because a joint tenant can unilaterally require sale
options.B: The court must order sale because the relationship has broken down
options.C: The court has discretion under s.15 to refuse sale if the purpose of acquisition has not wholly failed and considers factors like welfare of dependent children and the intentions of both parties
options.D: Y's default automatically grants X the right to force sale

Land Law.explanation: Under TLATA 1996 s.14-15, the court has discretion in applications for sale. Section 15 factors include: (1) intentions of the parties; (2) purpose for which land was held; (3) welfare of dependent children; (4) interests of secured creditors. Even though the relationship has failed, courts may decline sale or impose conditions (Bainbridge v Bainbridge [2011]).

Land Law.subject: Land Law
Land Law.stem: A landlord regularly accesses the demised premises without notice, disrupting the tenant's business. The lease has no express quiet enjoyment clause. Can the tenant claim breach?

options.A: No, because quiet enjoyment is only protected if expressly stated
options.B: Yes, an implied covenant of quiet enjoyment is read into every lease; the landlord must not substantially interfere with the tenant's possession (Markham v Paget [1908])
options.C: Only if the landlord causes physical damage
options.D: No, because the landlord owns the fee simple and retains rights

Land Law.explanation: An implied covenant of quiet enjoyment is read into every lease. The landlord covenants not to substantially interfere with the tenant's possession and enjoyment. This covers both the landlord's own actions and failure to prevent third-party interference. Repeated unannounced access disrupting business breaches this covenant (Lavender v Betts [1942]). The covenant is implied by law.

Land Law.subject: Land Law
Land Law.stem: X and Y purchased a property for £300,000 (X: £200,000; Y: £100,000). They are joint proprietors but never discussed beneficial ownership. Property is now worth £500,000. How will the court quantify beneficial interests?

options.A: Strictly proportional to financial contributions — X: 2/3, Y: 1/3
options.B: Each holds 1/2 (joint tenancy) regardless of contributions
options.C: The court applies a holistic approach (Stack v Dowden [2007]): examining intentions, conduct, contributions; if intentions cannot be deduced, it may quantify based on fairness (Jones v Kernott [2011])
options.D: Y's contribution is a gift, so X holds the entire beneficial interest

Land Law.explanation: Stack v Dowden [2007] and Jones v Kernott [2011] set out the modern approach. The starting position is that joint legal title implies joint beneficial title. This is rebuttable. The court applies a holistic approach, examining: (1) parties' intentions; (2) basis of purchase; (3) contributions; (4) conduct; (5) purpose of acquisition. Once intentions cannot be deduced, the court may make a quantitative determination reflecting fairness.

Land Law.subject: Land Law
Land Law.stem: A property is held on trust by trustees A and B for beneficiary X. A and B sell to C, who pays the purchase price to both trustees. The price is invested in another property in A and B's names. Has C acquired free of X's interests?

options.A: No, because beneficiary interests cannot be overreached
options.B: No, if C has notice of the trust from the register
options.C: Yes, because the trustees sold and the purchase price is invested in replacement property; X's interests are overreached to the replacement property (s.2 LPA 1925)
options.D: Yes, but only if C is a bona fide purchaser for value without notice

Land Law.explanation: Overreaching (s.2 LPA 1925): where trustees sell trust property, beneficiaries' equitable interests are overreached — they shift from the land to the purchase money and are reattached to any property purchased with the proceeds. A purchaser who pays the trustees takes free of beneficiaries' interests — those interests are overreached to the replacement property.

Land Law.subject: Land Law
Land Law.stem: A property is held by two trustees on trust for a beneficiary. The trustees are registered as proprietors. The register shows no restriction. One trustee acts alone and sells to C (who has no knowledge of the trust). Has overreaching occurred?

options.A: No, because a sole trustee cannot overreach — two signatures are always required
options.B: The sale is void; the beneficiary retains their interest
options.C: Overreaching may fail if C is aware that two trustees are registered; but if C is genuinely unaware and the sale appears legitimate, overreaching may still operate
options.D: C is automatically protected because the Land Registry will not accept the transfer without all trustees signing

Land Law.explanation: The requirement for overreaching is that the purchase price be paid to 'the trustees.' In registered land, the requirement is less strictly enforced. Caunce v Caunce [1969] established that a purchaser cannot be misled by the register — if two trustees are registered, a prudent purchaser will require both to sign. However, if only one trustee appears on the register and the purchaser genuinely relies on the register, overreaching may occur if the price is paid to 'the trustees.'

Land Law.subject: Land Law
Land Law.stem: After 10 years of adverse possession of registered land, an applicant applies to be registered. The registered proprietor objects. Under s.98 LRA 2002, when can the applicant be registered despite the objection?

options.A: The applicant cannot be registered if the proprietor objects
options.B: The applicant is registered automatically after 20 years of occupation
options.C: The applicant can be registered if they pay fair compensation to the proprietor
options.D: Only in exceptional circumstances — such as spent convictions, proprietary estoppel, or where the applicant has the benefit of a legal easement or interest

Land Law.explanation: Schedule 6 LRA 2002 creates a notification process. If the registered proprietor objects within 65 days, the applicant is only registered in 'exceptional circumstances' under s.98 LRA 2002. Examples include: spent convictions, proprietary estoppel, where the applicant has the benefit of a legal easement or other registered interest. This high threshold makes acquiring registered land by adverse possession much harder than under old law.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills & Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: Under s.9 of the Wills Act 1837, a valid will must satisfy which of the following requirements?

options.A: The will must be in writing, signed by the testator or at their direction, and witnessed by two persons who must be present together throughout the execution.
options.B: The will must be in writing, signed by the testator only (never by direction), and witnessed by at least one person in the testator's presence.
options.C: The will must be in writing, signed by the testator, and witnessed by two persons who must each sign in the presence of the testator but need not be present together.
options.D: The will must be in writing and signed by the testator, but no witnesses are required if the testator is over 70 years old.

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: Section 9 of the Wills Act 1837 requires: (1) the will to be in writing; (2) signed by the testator (or at their direction, with their knowledge); (3) the signature must be made or acknowledged in the presence of two witnesses; and (4) each witness must attest and sign in the presence of the testator. Crucially, both witnesses must be present simultaneously when the testator acknowledges the will and when each signs. This is a stringent requirement — 'simultaneous presence' has been strictly interpreted by the courts. Failure to comply renders the will invalid unless the court exercises dispensing power under s.21 of the Administration of Justice Act 1982.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills & Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: A testator signs a will with a mark (X) instead of their full signature. Two witnesses are present simultaneously and sign afterwards. Is the will valid?

options.A: No, because the mark is not a formal signature and does not satisfy s.9 of the Wills Act 1837.
options.B: No, because s.9 requires a signature in the testator's usual handwriting.
options.C: Yes, provided the mark was made with the intention of authenticating the will and the court is satisfied it was the act of the testator.
options.D: Yes, but only if the testator declared to the witnesses that the mark was their signature.

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: A mark (such as an X, thumbprint, or initials) can constitute a valid signature for the purposes of s.9 of the Wills Act 1837 provided: (1) it was made by the testator with the intention of authenticating the will; and (2) the court is satisfied that the mark was indeed the deliberate act of the testator — see Re Davey [1900]. The signature does not have to be in any particular form, nor need it be in the testator's usual handwriting. A mark, if authenticated as the testator's own act done with testamentary intent, suffices. The formal requirements are functional, not prescriptive.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills & Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: Which of the following correctly identifies the test for testamentary capacity established in Banks v Goodfellow (1870)?

options.A: The testator must understand the nature of making a will, the extent of their property, and the claims of those who might benefit, and must not suffer from any mental disorder.
options.B: The testator must be free from any medical condition, able to list all assets, and able to recall the names of all blood relatives.
options.C: The testator must have capacity for 28 days before and after the will's execution, and must be certified by a medical practitioner.
options.D: The testator need only understand the nature of a will and the extent of their property; understanding family claims is optional.

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: Banks v Goodfellow (1870) established the foundational test for testamentary capacity, which requires the testator to: (1) understand the nature of making a will and its legal effects; (2) understand the extent of the property being disposed of; (3) understand the claims of those who might reasonably expect to benefit; and (4) not be suffering from any disorder of the mind that 'poisons the affections, perverts the sense of right, or prevents the exercise of natural faculties' in disposing of property. All four limbs must be satisfied at the moment of execution. This is a test of functional understanding, not perfection — the testator need not have perfect memory or intelligence, only a reasonable grasp of these essential matters.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills & Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: A testator with advancing dementia gave clear, detailed instructions for her will when mentally competent. By the time of execution, she had declined significantly but still understood that she was signing a will prepared in accordance with instructions she had previously given. Is the will valid under the principle in Parker v Felgate (1883)?

options.A: No, because Parker v Felgate requires full capacity at execution, which she did not have.
options.B: Yes, but only if a medical practitioner confirmed her capacity at the time of giving instructions.
options.C: No, because testamentary capacity must be assessed at the time of signature only; prior capacity is irrelevant.
options.D: Yes, provided the will was actually prepared in accordance with the instructions given when she had capacity and she understood at execution that it reflected her earlier wishes.

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: Parker v Felgate (1883) allows a will to be valid where: (1) the testator had capacity when giving instructions to the solicitor; (2) the will was prepared in accordance with those instructions; and (3) at execution, the testator understood that they were signing a will prepared pursuant to instructions given when they had capacity — even if they could no longer fully recall those details. This is a practical exception to the strict rule in Banks v Goodfellow. The testator in this scenario satisfies all three conditions: she had capacity when instructing, the will reflects those instructions, and she retained awareness at execution that the will embodied her earlier wishes. The will is therefore valid.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills & Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: A testator signs her will in the presence of two witnesses. Witness A sees the testator sign but leaves before Witness B signs. Witness B never sees the testator sign, only hears from Witness A that she has done so. Both witnesses then sign. Is this valid under s.9 of the Wills Act 1837?

options.A: Yes, because both witnesses have signed and the testator was present when the first witness signed.
options.B: No, because both witnesses must be present simultaneously when the testator acknowledges or signs the will and when each witness signs.
options.C: Yes, provided Witness B heard the testator acknowledge the signature, even though they did not see it.
options.D: No, because Witness A left before the attestation was complete, breaking the chain of evidence.

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: Section 9 of the Wills Act 1837 requires that the testator's signature be 'made or acknowledged in the presence of two witnesses present at the same time' and that each witness must attest and sign 'in the presence of the testator'. This is interpreted to require that both witnesses be present simultaneously at both the testator's signing/acknowledgment and at each witness's signing. The principle in Cavendish (1889) and later cases confirms that simultaneous presence is essential — not sequential presence. In this scenario, Witness B was not present when the testator signed, and Witness A left before Witness B signed. Neither witness was present with the other at the critical moment. The will therefore fails to comply with s.9 and is invalid, unless the court exercises its dispensing power under s.21 of the Administration of Justice Act 1982.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills & Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: A testator executes a valid will in 2020. In 2022, the testator marries. The testator makes no new will and does not execute any new codicil. What is the status of the 2020 will?

options.A: The will remains valid because subsequent marriage does not affect wills executed before the marriage.
options.B: The will is revoked by the marriage unless it was expressed to be made 'in contemplation of marriage'.
options.C: The will is revoked by the marriage, but the testator may apply to court to restore it.
options.D: The will is suspended until the testator makes a new will after marriage.

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: Section 18 of the Wills Act 1837 provides that subsequent marriage revokes a prior will unless the will was expressed to be made in contemplation of marriage. To avoid revocation, the testator would need to execute a new will after marriage or execute a codicil. In this scenario, the marriage in 2022 revokes the 2020 will.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills & Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: A testator's will provides: 'I leave my house to my son, and my residuary estate to my daughter.' The son predeceases the testator, leaving two children. What happens to the house?

options.A: The house lapses and falls into residue; the daughter takes the house as part of the residuary estate.
options.B: The house reverts to the testator's estate and is distributed under the intestacy rules.
options.C: The house passes to the son's two children by virtue of s.33 of the Wills Act 1837.
options.D: The house is held in trust for the son's widow.

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: Under s.33 of the Wills Act 1837, if a beneficiary under a will predeceases the testator, the gift normally lapses (fails). However, s.33 provides an important exception: if the beneficiary was the testator's child or remoter issue (descendant), and the deceased beneficiary left issue (children, grandchildren, etc.) who survive the testator, then the gift passes to the deceased beneficiary's issue in equal shares, as if the beneficiary had survived. This rule applies to both specific gifts (like the house) and residuary gifts. In this scenario, the son predeceased the testator but left children (the testator's grandchildren). Section 33 applies, and the house passes to those grandchildren in equal shares. The daughter does not inherit the house.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills & Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: A person dies intestate, leaving a spouse and two adult children. Under the Administration of Estates Act 1925 (as amended by the Intestates' Estates Act 1952 and subsequent amendments), what is the spouse's entitlement?

options.A: The spouse takes the entire estate absolutely.
options.B: The spouse takes personal chattels only; the children take the rest of the estate in equal shares.
options.C: The spouse takes half the estate; the children take the other half in equal shares.
options.D: The spouse takes personal chattels, plus a statutory legacy of approximately £322,000 (as at Feb 2020, subject to indexation), plus half of any residue after payment of debts and expenses; the children take the other half of the residue.

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: Under s.46 of the Administration of Estates Act 1925 (as amended), where the deceased leaves a spouse and issue (children), the spouse takes: (1) the personal chattels (defined as tangible movable property excluding money and securities) absolutely; (2) a statutory legacy (a fixed sum, currently £322,000 as at February 2020, subject to indexation); and (3) a life interest in half of the residue. However, the 'life interest' has been abolished by modern amendments; instead, the spouse takes an absolute interest in that half of the residue. The children take the other half of the residue on statutory trusts (meaning they take in equal shares, subject to the rights of remoter issue if a child predeceases). If the estate is insufficient to pay the statutory legacy in full, the spouse takes what is available. This rule applies to both spouses and civil partners, and it reflects a balance between protecting the surviving spouse and preserving rights for the deceased's children.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills & Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: A person dies leaving a net estate of £500,000 (after debts). The estate includes their principal private residence worth £250,000, which is left to their adult daughter. What is the Inheritance Tax treatment?

options.A: The entire estate is charged at 40% IHT because it exceeds the nil rate band.
options.B: The nil rate band of £325,000 applies, plus the residential nil rate band (RNRB) of £175,000 (total £500,000). No IHT is payable.
options.C: Only the excess over £325,000 is charged at 40% IHT (i.e. £175,000 × 40% = £70,000), because the RNRB cannot be used here.
options.D: The house is exempt from IHT because it is being left to a direct descendant.

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: The nil rate band (NRB) under the Inheritance Tax Act 1984 is frozen at £325,000. The residential nil rate band (RNRB), introduced by the Finance Act 2015, provides an additional exemption of up to £175,000 where the deceased's home is left to a direct descendant (a 'qualifying beneficiary'), such as an adult child or grandchild. The RNRB is available in addition to the standard NRB. In this scenario, the deceased's estate of £500,000 falls within the combined NRB and RNRB allowances (£325,000 + £175,000 = £500,000). Therefore, no IHT is payable. Note that the RNRB is not available if the home is left to a non-qualifying beneficiary (e.g. a spouse or a trust), and it is subject to various conditions and phasing rules. However, the question states the home is left to the daughter, who qualifies.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills & Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: A person makes a gift of £10,000 to their spouse during life. After the testator's death, the testator's estate is £300,000. What is the Inheritance Tax treatment of these gifts and the estate?

options.A: The gift to the spouse is a potentially exempt transfer (PET) and is charged at 40% if the testator dies within 7 years.
options.B: The gift to the spouse is taxable at 20% (half rate) because it is to a spouse.
options.C: The gift to the spouse is exempt from Inheritance Tax because spouses are exempt (provided they are UK domiciled), so the gift is not charged and does not affect the nil rate band.
options.D: The gift to the spouse uses the annual exemption of £3,000.

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: Transfers between spouses and civil partners are exempt from Inheritance Tax under the IHTA 1984, provided both parties are UK domiciled (or the transferee is UK domiciled). This exemption applies to both lifetime gifts and gifts on death. There is no limit on the amount of the exemption — a spouse can inherit the entire estate without any IHT. The annual exemption of £3,000 and small gifts exemption of £250 per person apply to other gifts, not to gifts between spouses. In this scenario, the gift of £10,000 to the spouse is entirely exempt, and the spouse receives it free of IHT. The spouse's entitlement to the £10,000 does not count toward the testator's nil rate band, and the testator's nil rate band of £325,000 remains available for gifts to non-exempt beneficiaries.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills & Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: A testator's daughter is one of two witnesses to the will. The testator intends his daughter to inherit a substantial legacy under the will. What is the legal consequence under s.15 of the Wills Act 1837?

options.A: The will is invalid in its entirety because a beneficiary cannot be a witness.
options.B: The will is valid, but the gift to the daughter is void and falls back into the estate.
options.C: The will is valid, and the gift to the daughter is valid because she was a proper witness.
options.D: The gift is void only if the daughter's signature was obtained improperly.

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: Section 15 of the Wills Act 1837 provides that a gift made to a witness (or to the spouse of a witness) is void, but the will itself remains valid. This rule applies even if the witness was unaware they were a beneficiary at the time of witnessing. The policy is to prevent the appearance of undue influence and to ensure independent attestation. However, the invalidity applies only to the gift to the beneficiary-witness — the rest of the will remains valid and is given effect. In this scenario, the will is valid, but the legacy to the testator's daughter is void and will fall back into the residuary estate or intestacy as appropriate.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills & Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: A testator's will bequeaths 'my shareholding in ABC Ltd' to the testator's nephew. At the time the will was made, the testator held 100 ordinary shares in ABC Ltd. Before the testator's death, ABC Ltd is acquired by another company and the testator's shares are converted into shares in the acquiring company. What happens to the bequest?

options.A: The bequest is adeemed (fails) because the original property no longer exists in the form specified.
options.B: The bequest is satisfied by the shares in the acquiring company, because they are the natural substitute for the original shares.
options.C: The bequest fails, and the nephew receives nothing, because specific gifts cannot be modified after the will is made.
options.D: The nephew receives the value of the original shares in cash from the estate.

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: Ademption is the common law doctrine that if a specific bequest (a gift of a particular item) is no longer owned by the testator in the form described at death, the gift fails. However, the courts have developed an exception where there has been a 'continuity of identity' — if the property has been converted into a substitute of substantially the same nature in the ordinary course of events, the gift is not adeemed. The classic example is shares that are converted through a merger or reorganisation. In this scenario, the testator's shares were converted into shares in the acquiring company — this is a continuity of identity as regards the testator's investment. The nephew takes the shares in the acquiring company. If, by contrast, the testator had sold the shares and the proceeds had been used for something else, the gift would be adeemed.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills & Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: A person dies intestate leaving a spouse and three children: A (deceased, but who left two children), B (survived by one child), and C (no children). How does the intestacy law distribute the children's share of the residue under the statutory trusts?

options.A: Equal division into three shares: one for A's children (shared between them), one for B's child, one for C.
options.B: Equal division into five shares: one each for A's two children, one each for B's child and C, and one reserve share.
options.C: Equal division into two shares: one for the children of the deceased's children, one for C.
options.D: Equal division into three shares (A, B, C), regardless of their children's status.

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: The statutory trusts for issue under s.46 of the Administration of Estates Act 1925 operate 'per stirpes', meaning 'by the stocks' or 'by branches'. The estate is divided into shares based on the number of the deceased's direct children (the 'stirpes'), not based on the total number of grandchildren. Each child's share is then divided among their own surviving issue. In this scenario, the deceased had three children; therefore, the children's portion of the residue is divided into three equal shares — one for A (which A's two children divide equally between them), one for B (which B's one child takes entirely), and one for C (who takes the whole share). This prevents the estates of deceased children from being diluted if they had multiple offspring.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills & Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: Who is NOT eligible to apply under the Inheritance (Provision for Family and Dependants) Act 1975 for a court order for financial provision from an estate?

options.A: A person who was married to the deceased at the date of death.
options.B: An adult child of the deceased who is in full-time employment and has a good salary.
options.C: A person who cohabited with the deceased as spouse or civil partner for at least two years immediately before death.
options.D: A person who was the deceased's legal dependent (relying on the deceased for financial support) at death.

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: Section 1 of the Inheritance (Provision for Family and Dependants) Act 1975 defines 'any person' who may apply for an order. The statutory applicants are: (1) the spouse or civil partner of the deceased; (2) a former spouse or civil partner (if not remarried); (3) a cohabitant (two years immediately before death); (4) a child of the deceased; (5) a child of the family (i.e. someone the deceased treated as a child of the family); and (6) any other person who immediately before death was maintained wholly or partly by the deceased. An adult child with good employment income and financial independence is not eligible — the applicant must be a child (in the family law sense) and typically needs to be a minor or dependent. The Act protects dependants and children, not all children as of right. Note that eligibility differs from the strength of claim; an adult child with independent means simply does not fall within the Act's categories.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills & Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: A 25-year-old adult child of the deceased applies under the Inheritance (Provision for Family and Dependants) Act 1975. The deceased's will left the entire estate to an unrelated charity. The child was not dependent on the deceased and has a good job. What standard applies, and is the application likely to succeed?

options.A: The spouse standard applies (reasonable financial provision). The child is likely to succeed because they are a child of the deceased.
options.B: The child must show they were dependent on the deceased or were a child of the family. If neither is true, they have no claim.
options.C: The maintenance standard applies: provision only for 'maintenance' (i.e. income to meet reasonable requirements). Even if eligible, the child is unlikely to succeed given their independent financial position.
options.D: The child must prove the deceased acted unreasonably by not providing for them. The burden is on the child to show the will is unjust.

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: For most applicants other than spouses, the standard under s.2 of the Inheritance (Provision for Family and Dependants) Act 1975 is 'such financial provision as it would be reasonable for the deceased to make for [the applicant's] maintenance'. 'Maintenance' does not mean the child's full financial needs or standard of living, but rather income sufficient to meet their reasonable requirements — typically housing, food, and essential expenses. The 'reasonable requirement' test is much lower than the spouse standard. In this scenario, the child is a direct child and is therefore eligible to apply. However, they must satisfy the court that they need maintenance (i.e. have a shortfall between their needs and available resources). An adult child with a good job and financial independence is unlikely to meet that threshold. The fact that the deceased left the estate to charity is not, by itself, evidence of unreasonableness or grounds for overriding the will — the court only intervenes if the deceased failed to make 'reasonable' provision for maintenance.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills & Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.subtopic: Payment of debts — order of application
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: An estate is insolvent (liabilities exceed assets). The deceased's debts consist of: (1) funeral expenses, (2) a mortgage on the house (secured debt), (3) unsecured personal loans, and (4) income tax owed. Under the rules for payment of debts in an insolvent estate, what is the correct order of priority?
