options.A: All debts are paid equally (pro rata) from the available funds.
options.B: Funeral expenses first, then income tax, then the secured mortgage, then unsecured loans.
options.C: Funeral expenses and costs of administration first, then secured debts (mortgages), then unsecured debts and taxes in equal proportion.
options.D: Secured debts (mortgages) are paid from the asset they secure; costs of administration and funeral expenses are paid first from general assets; unsecured debts are paid pro rata from what remains.

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: The order of payment of debts in an insolvent estate under s.34 of the Administration of Estates Act 1925 and general equity principles is: (1) costs of administration and the funeral expenses (from the general estate); (2) secured debts are paid from the secured asset (e.g. the mortgage is paid from the proceeds of sale of the mortgaged property); (3) all other debts (unsecured debts, taxes, etc.) are paid pro rata from the remaining funds, meaning each creditor receives a proportional share if there is insufficient money to pay them in full. The personal representative must not distribute assets to beneficiaries until debts are cleared or adequately provided for. Secured creditors have priority over their security (the mortgaged property), but their claims do not take priority over the costs of administration.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills & Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: A personal representative has completed the administration of the estate and is ready to transfer a house to one of the beneficiaries. What document should the personal representative execute to transfer the legal title to the house?

options.A: A deed of gift, because the beneficiary is receiving the property free of charge.
options.B: A transfer deed, just like an ordinary sale between two parties.
options.C: A will codicil, amending the will to reflect the transfer.
options.D: An assent, which transfers the legal estate in the house from the personal representative to the beneficiary.

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: An assent is a written document by which a personal representative transfers the legal estate in property from the deceased's estate to a beneficiary. The assent must be in writing and signed by the personal representative; it does not require formality beyond a signature, though it should identify the property, the beneficiary, and the estate. An assent can be given orally for personalty (personal property), but for realty (real property such as houses) it must be in writing. Once an assent has been given, the personal representative drops out of the title and the beneficiary acquires the legal estate. The assent is not a sale or a gift; it is simply a transfer of the property held in the estate to the beneficiary. An assent is not a transfer deed (which implies a sale); it is a unique document specific to estate administration.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills & Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: A person makes a gift of £50,000 to a discretionary trust during their lifetime. Four years later, the testator dies. What is the Inheritance Tax treatment of this gift?

options.A: The gift is a potentially exempt transfer (PET) that becomes exempt because the testator survived more than 3 years.
options.B: The gift is a chargeable lifetime transfer (CLT) that is subject to IHT at 20% during the testator's lifetime and again at 40% on the testator's death (subject to taper relief).
options.C: The gift is a CLT and is charged at 20% lifetime rate. On death, the 20% already paid is deducted, and only the difference up to 40% is charged.
options.D: The gift is exempt from IHT because it is to a trust, which is treated as a separate entity.

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: A chargeable lifetime transfer (CLT) is a transfer that is immediately subject to IHT (unlike a PET, which is potentially exempt and only charged if the transferor dies within 7 years). Gifts to trusts (other than bare trusts and trusts for a bereaved minor) are CLTs. In this scenario, the gift of £50,000 to a discretionary trust is a CLT. When made, it is subject to IHT at 20% (less any available nil rate band), so £30,000 would be exempt (nil rate band) and £20,000 would be charged at 20% (i.e. £4,000). When the testator dies (4 years later), the transfer is reconsidered at the full rate of 40%, and taper relief is applied. Because the testator survived 4 years (which falls between 3 and 4 years), taper relief reduces the rate from 40% to 35%. The lifetime tax of £4,000 is credited against the death tax, and the beneficiary (or trust) is liable for the additional tax due.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills & Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: A person owns 100% of the shares in a private limited company (unquoted shares) worth £400,000. The shareholder dies, leaving the shares to their child. What is the Inheritance Tax treatment of the shares?

options.A: The shares are valued at £400,000 and are charged to IHT at 40% (less the nil rate band).
options.B: The shares are subject to a partial relief of 50% under BPR, so £200,000 is exempt and £200,000 is charged.
options.C: The shares qualify for agricultural property relief (APR) only if they relate to farming.
options.D: The shares qualify for business property relief (BPR) at 100%, so they are exempt from IHT.

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: Business property relief (BPR) under the IHTA 1984 provides relief from Inheritance Tax on the transfer of certain business property. For unquoted shares (shares in private companies not listed on any stock exchange), BPR applies at a rate of 100%, meaning the entire value of the shares is exempt from IHT. BPR is subject to conditions: (1) the property must have been owned for at least 2 years before death; (2) the business must not be excepted (e.g. not mainly dealing in land, shares, or insurance); and (3) the property must be a 'relevant business property' (which includes unquoted shares). In this scenario, the deceased owned 100% of the shares in a private company — assuming the company is a genuine operating business (not a pure investment vehicle) and the shares were held for the required period, BPR at 100% applies. The shares are therefore exempt, and no IHT is charged on their transfer.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills & Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: A person owns a working farm (freehold) worth £600,000 and leaves it to their child. The person also leaves cash of £200,000 to a charity. Assuming agricultural property relief (APR) at 100% applies to the farm, what is the Inheritance Tax due?

options.A: IHT is due on the cash gift but not the farm, because APR provides 100% relief.
options.B: IHT is due on £200,000 (the excess over the nil rate band of £325,000), calculated at 40%.
options.C: IHT is due on £475,000 (£600,000 farm + £200,000 cash - £325,000 nil rate band), calculated at 40%.
options.D: No IHT is due, because APR at 100% exempts the farm and the charity gift is exempt.

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: Agricultural property relief (APR) under the IHTA 1984 provides relief from Inheritance Tax on the transfer of agricultural property (land and buildings used for agriculture, including working farms). APR is available at 100% (full relief) or 50% depending on the nature of the occupation and the interest held. Assuming APR at 100% applies, the farm is entirely exempt from IHT. A gift to a charity is also exempt from Inheritance Tax under s.58 of the IHTA 1984. In this scenario, the farm of £600,000 is exempt under APR, and the cash gift of £200,000 to charity is also exempt. The total estate of £800,000 is covered by exemptions, so no IHT is due. (Note: the nil rate band is not used here because there are sufficient exemptions to cover the entire estate.)

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills & Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: A person made a chargeable lifetime transfer (CLT) of £100,000 to a trust, using up their nil rate band. Four years later, the person dies. At death, the cumulative transfers in the 7 years before death total £350,000. What is the Inheritance Tax charge on death?

options.A: No IHT is due on death because taper relief applies after 4 years.
options.B: IHT is charged at 40% on the full £100,000 CLT, with no relief.
options.C: IHT is charged at 40% on the amount of the CLT that exceeds the nil rate band, with taper relief reducing the rate.
options.D: IHT is charged on the cumulative total of £350,000, with taper relief applied if the person survived more than 3 years.

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: Taper relief under the IHTA 1984 reduces the effective rate of Inheritance Tax on chargeable lifetime transfers (CLTs) and potentially exempt transfers (PETs) that become taxable on death, depending on how long the transferor survived. The relief applies on a sliding scale: if the transferor dies between 3 and 4 years after the transfer, the rate is reduced to 35% (a 25% reduction from the full 40% rate); between 4 and 5 years, it reduces to 30%; between 5 and 6 years, 25%; between 6 and 7 years, 20%. In this scenario, the CLT was made 4 years before death. The CLT of £100,000 was made when the nil rate band was available; therefore, £100,000 is exempt. On the death 4 years later, that CLT is reconsidered at the death rate with taper relief. The taper applies to any IHT charged on that CLT (which, in this case, is nil because the nil rate band sheltered it). However, taper relief also applies to subsequent PETs and CLTs made in the 7-year window; those would be recharged at the tapered rate.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills & Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: A person owns shares purchased for £50,000 which, at death, are worth £150,000. The shares are inherited by a beneficiary. What is the beneficiary's base cost for capital gains tax purposes?

options.A: £50,000 (the original purchase price, carried forward from the deceased).
options.B: £150,000 (the value at death — the 'uplift').
options.D: £0, because gifts inherit property free of capital gains tax.

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: Section 62 of the Taxation of Chargeable Gains Act 1992 provides that a beneficiary who inherits an asset acquires that asset at its market value at the date of death. This is called the 'uplift' (or 'step up') in base cost. The uplift means that there is no capital gains tax charge on the increase in value during the deceased's lifetime (the gain from £50,000 to £150,000). The beneficiary's base cost for future capital gains tax purposes is £150,000 (the value at death), so any gain is calculated from that point. This rule is one of the key tax benefits of holding assets until death — the unrealised gains during the deceased's lifetime escape capital gains tax entirely. If the beneficiary later sells the shares for £200,000, the capital gain is only £50,000 (£200,000 - £150,000), not £150,000.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills & Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: A testator's estate includes a house that was the testator's principal private residence, purchased for £100,000 and worth £400,000 at death. The house is sold by the personal representative for £410,000 shortly after death. What is the capital gains tax position?

options.A: The estate pays capital gains tax on the entire gain of £310,000 (£410,000 - £100,000).
options.B: The estate pays capital gains tax at a lower rate because the assets are held in the estate.
options.C: No capital gains tax is due because the house was a principal private residence and there is relief for such property.
options.D: The estate pays capital gains tax on only the gain after death (£10,000), because the uplift removes the gain during life (£300,000), and principal private residence relief may also apply.

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: Section 62 of the TCGA 1992 provides that the beneficiary (or the estate, if sold before distribution) acquires the asset at its market value at death — in this case, £400,000. Therefore, any gain accruing after death (from £400,000 to £410,000 = £10,000) is a post-death gain and is potentially subject to capital gains tax. However, principal private residence relief under s.223 of the TCGA 1992 may apply if the house was the testator's only or main residence. The relief exempts gains on the disposal of a principal private residence. The critical point is that the uplift to £400,000 at death extinguishes the pre-death gain entirely; tax is only due on the post-death increase. Furthermore, if principal private residence relief applies, even the post-death gain may be exempt or partially exempt. The uplift is one of the key advantages of holding property until death.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills & Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: A testator executes Will A in 2018. In 2020, the testator burns Will A with the intention of revoking it, and then executes Will B. In 2022, the testator is dissatisfied with Will B. Can Will A be revived?

options.A: No, because Will A was destroyed with the intent to revoke and cannot be revived.
options.B: Yes, Will A can be revived if the testator re-executes it or executes a codicil referring to it with intent to revive.
options.C: Yes, if the testator can show they did not intend to revoke Will A permanently.
options.D: No, because once destroyed, a will is permanently revoked under s.20 of the Wills Act 1837.

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: Section 22 of the Wills Act 1837 governs revival. A will that has been revoked can be revived in two ways: (1) by re-execution with all the formalities of s.9; or (2) by a codicil executed with s.9 formalities that expressly declares the testator's intent to revive the earlier will. Mere destruction of a subsequent will (Will B) does not automatically revive the earlier will (Will A) — there must be a positive act of revival. In this scenario, the testator burned Will A deliberately with intent to revoke; section 20 provides that destruction of a will with intent to revoke is effective. However, Will A is not permanently lost — it can be revived if the testator re-executes it (perhaps with the help of a copy) or executes a new codicil showing clear intent to revive it. Mere dissatisfaction with Will B does not achieve revival.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills & Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: A person dies intestate leaving no spouse or children, but leaving a surviving parent (P) and a surviving sibling (S) with two children. Under the Administration of Estates Act 1925, what is the order of entitlement?

options.A: The estate is divided equally among P, S, and S's children.
options.B: P and S take equal shares; S's children take nothing.
options.C: S and S's children take equal shares, per stirpes; P takes nothing.
options.D: P takes the entire estate; S and S's children take nothing.

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: Section 46 of the Administration of Estates Act 1925 sets out a fixed order of priority for intestate succession. The order is: spouse (if any); issue (children, grandchildren, etc. on statutory trusts); parents (equally, or wholly to one if only one survives); siblings and their issue (per stirpes); grandparents; uncles and aunts and their issue; and remote relatives. In this scenario, the deceased left no spouse or issue, so the estate passes to the parents. A surviving parent (P) takes the entire estate. The sibling (S) and their children are further down the priority list and take nothing if a parent survives. This rule reflects the principle that the intestacy rules prioritise lineal descendants (issue) and then lineal ancestors (parents), before moving sideways to collaterals (siblings).

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills & Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.subtopic: Reasonable financial provision — standard and factors
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: A surviving spouse applies under the Inheritance (Provision for Family and Dependants) Act 1975 for a court order for financial provision. What standard does the court apply, and what are some of the key matters the court must consider?

options.A: The court must provide the spouse with a fixed percentage of the estate (typically 50%). It considers only the spouse's age and current income.
options.B: The court must provide the spouse with 'maintenance only' — that is, enough to cover essential living expenses, not more.
options.C: The court must provide 'reasonable financial provision' as defined in s.2 of the Act, which for a spouse means such provision as would be reasonable in all the circumstances. The court must consider the spouse's financial needs, the estate's size, the deceased's obligations, the spouse's standard of living during marriage, and the deceased's wishes.
options.D: The court must apply the intestacy rules strictly; the spouse cannot claim more than they would have received under intestacy.

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: Section 2 of the Inheritance (Provision for Family and Dependants) Act 1975 establishes different standards depending on the applicant's status. For a spouse or civil partner, the standard is 'reasonable financial provision in all the circumstances of the case'. This is much wider than 'maintenance only' (which applies to other applicants). The court must consider the matters listed in s.3, including: (1) financial resources and needs of applicants and beneficiaries; (2) the obligations and responsibilities the deceased had; (3) the standard of living during the marriage; (4) the age of the applicant and their earning capacity; (5) the physical and mental ability to work; (6) the manner in which the property was disposed of; and (7) the deceased's reasons for not making provision. The spouse's claim is not limited by what they would have received under intestacy; the Act is intended to provide greater flexibility than fixed intestacy rules.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills & Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: What is the primary duty of a personal representative (executor or administrator) when administering an estate?

options.A: To collect in the estate's assets, pay debts and liabilities, and distribute the residue in accordance with the will (or intestacy rules) with due diligence and honesty.
options.B: To distribute the estate as quickly as possible to minimise costs.
options.C: To invest the estate's funds to generate income for beneficiaries.
options.D: To defend all claims against the estate, even if they have merit.

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: The primary duties of a personal representative are: (1) to collect in the estate's assets and realise them where necessary; (2) to pay the deceased's debts, liabilities, and the costs of administration; (3) to account for income and capital; (4) to distribute the residue among the beneficiaries (or to the intestate succession) in accordance with the will or the intestacy rules; and (5) to do all of this with due diligence, honesty, and care. Personal representatives act as trustees and owe fiduciary duties to the beneficiaries. The executor's year (roughly 12 months from death) is the period within which these duties should be substantially completed, though reasonable delays are permitted. A personal representative should not delay distribution indefinitely, but equally should not rush to distribute if doing so would jeopardise the proper settlement of claims.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills & Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: A testator's will provides that the residuary estate is to be divided equally among 'my children'. At the time the will was made, the testator had two children, A and B. Subsequently, the testator had a third child, C. The testator dies, leaving A, B, and C surviving. How is the residue distributed?

options.A: The residue is divided equally among A and B only (one-half each), because the class was fixed when the will was made.
options.B: The residue is divided equally among A, B, and C (one-third each), because C is a member of the class 'my children' at the testator's death.
options.C: The residue is divided equally among A and B, with C having a claim only if A or B die before the testator.
options.D: The distribution depends on the names of the children; if C's name is not in the will, C has no claim.

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: A class gift (a gift to a group defined by a relationship, such as 'my children' or 'my grandchildren') is not fixed at the time the will is made. Instead, the class remains open until the moment of distribution (usually the testator's death, or sometimes the date of distribution if the gift is to a future class). All persons who fall within the description of the class at that moment take a share. In this scenario, the testator made a will when he had two children (A and B), but later had a third child (C). At the testator's death, the class 'my children' includes A, B, and C. All three are members of the class and take equal shares (one-third each). If the testator had intended to exclude afterborn children, he would have said 'my children now living' or 'my children born on or before [date]'. The class gift expands to include C.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills & Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: A testator, aged 85 and in fragile health, is visited regularly by a distant relative, X. X takes control of the testator's affairs, prevents others from visiting, and persuades the testator to make a new will leaving the bulk of the estate to X. The testator is of sound mind. Is this will valid?

options.A: Yes, because the testator has testamentary capacity and executed the will properly.
options.B: No, because the testator's age and health suggest a presumption of undue influence.
options.C: Possibly no — the circumstances raise a presumption of undue influence that X must rebut by proving the will was freely made, and this may be difficult if X had opportunity and motive.
options.D: The will is valid only if X was not present at the will's execution.

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: Undue influence is a vitiating factor that can render a will invalid. The test is whether the testator's will was overborne by the wrongdoer's influence to the point that the will expresses the wrongdoer's wishes, not the testator's own. Proof of undue influence is difficult; it requires evidence that the testator lacked freedom of will. However, the courts have developed a principle of presumption: where there is evidence of (1) opportunity to influence (e.g. access and control); (2) motive (e.g. a benefit under the will); and (3) a resulting will that is rational on its face but suspicious in light of the relationship, then a presumption of undue influence arises. The burden then shifts to the person propounding the will (typically the beneficiary) to prove the testator's freedom of will by satisfactory evidence (e.g. evidence that the testator's solicitor took independent instructions, or evidence of the testator's own expressed wishes). In this scenario, X had opportunity, clear motive (inheriting the bulk), isolated the testator, and benefited substantially — a presumption of undue influence would likely arise. X would need to rebut this by proving the testator's freedom, which may be difficult.

Wills & Administration of Estates.subject: Wills & Administration of Estates
Wills & Administration of Estates.stem: A testator's will provides for specific gifts of £10,000 to each of three named beneficiaries, but is silent on the residue (the bulk of the estate after the specific gifts). The specific gifts total £30,000, and the total estate is £200,000. What happens to the remaining £170,000?

options.A: The remaining £170,000 is held on trust for the named beneficiaries in proportion to their gifts.
options.B: The remaining £170,000 is forfeited to the Crown as bona vacantia (ownerless property).
options.C: The remaining £170,000 falls into intestacy and is distributed under the intestacy rules (e.g. to the testator's spouse, children, or relatives as appropriate).
options.D: The remaining £170,000 goes to the testator's personal representative as a fee for administering the estate.

Wills & Administration of Estates.explanation: A partial intestacy occurs when a will disposes of only part of the testator's property, leaving the rest without any provision. The part that is not provided for by the will falls into intestacy and is distributed in accordance with the Administration of Estates Act 1925. In this scenario, the will provides for specific gifts totalling £30,000 but makes no provision for the residue (£170,000). This £170,000 is undisposed of and falls into partial intestacy. It will be distributed to the testator's spouse (if any), children, parents, siblings, or other relatives in accordance with the statutory order of succession, regardless of the provisions made by the will for the specific gifts. If there are no relatives, the property passes to the Crown. A partial intestacy is common where a testator makes specific legacies but forgets to name a residuary beneficiary or the residuary clause fails for some reason.

Trusts.subject: Trusts
Trusts.stem: What is the primary test used by English courts to determine whether a settlor has the necessary certainty of intention to create a trust?

options.A: An objective test: whether the settlor's words or conduct demonstrate an unequivocal intention to create a binding obligation on the trustee, regardless of the settlor's subjective state of mind
options.B: A subjective test: whether the settlor consciously and deliberately stated the word 'trust'
options.C: A strict formality test: whether the settlor complied with all documentary requirements set out in the Law of Property Act 1925
options.D: A commercial reasonableness test: whether an ordinary prudent person would consider the settlor intended to create a trust

Trusts.explanation: Per Knight v Knight (1840) and confirmed in Paul v Constance [1977], the test is objective.

Trusts.subject: Trusts
Trusts.stem: In Re London Wine Co (Storage) Ltd, why did the court hold that the beneficiary could not trace into a bulk store of wine held by the company?

options.A: Because there was uncertainty as to which specific bottles belonged to the beneficiary when they were not separated from the general bulk
options.B: Because the wine was stored as a commercial product rather than as trust property with formal documentation
options.C: Because the beneficiary failed to register a charge over the property
options.D: Because the trustee company was insolvent

Trusts.explanation: Re London Wine Co [1986] established that certainty of subject matter requires that the trust property be clearly ascertained.

Trusts.subject: Trusts
Trusts.stem: A settlor declares a fixed trust over shares in favour of 'the children of my sister.' At the time of the declaration, the sister has three children, but may have more in future. Which test governs whether the trust is valid?

options.A: The 'is or is not' test from McPhail v Doulton
options.B: The complete list test from IRC v Broadway Cottages Trust, requiring the trustee to be able to make a complete list of all beneficiaries at the moment the trust is declared
options.C: The commercial purpose test
options.D: The reasonable exercise test

Trusts.explanation: Fixed trusts must satisfy the complete list test (IRC v Broadway Cottages [1955]).

Trusts.subject: Trusts
Trusts.stem: A settlor writes a letter stating 'I hereby hold all my shares in ABC plc on trust for my nephew.' The settlor does not deliver the shares, execute any deed, or deliver the letter. Two weeks later, the settlor revokes the letter. Which principle determines the effectiveness of the trust declaration?

options.A: The trust fails because the settlor did not execute a formal deed of trust
options.B: The trust is ineffective because the settlor has not done everything within their power
options.C: The trust is valid as a declaration of self as trustee; no further conduct is necessary once the words of trust are recorded in writing
options.D: The trust fails because the settlor later revoked the document

Trusts.explanation: In Milroy v Lord (1862), the court held that a trust can be constituted by declaration of self as trustee.

Trusts.subject: Trusts
Trusts.stem: A settlor orally declares a trust of land for the benefit of their child, without any written evidence. The child relies on the declaration and takes possession of the land. Is the trust enforceable?

options.A: No, because section 53(1)(b) of the Law of Property Act 1925 requires a declaration of trust in land to be evidenced in writing, and this requirement cannot be waived even by part performance or reliance
options.B: Yes, because the child's part performance and reliance on the declaration satisfy the equity
options.C: No, because oral trusts of land are prohibited at common law
options.D: Yes, if the oral declaration was made as a solemn declaration in front of two independent witnesses

Trusts.explanation: Section 53(1)(b) LPA 1925 is absolute: a declaration of trust in land must be manifested and proved by some writing.

Trusts.subject: Trusts
Trusts.stem: A settlor has executed the share transfer form and delivered it to the company secretary. The company secretary delays registration for two weeks. Is the trust constituted?

options.A: No, because the legal title remains in the settlor until the company registers the transfer
options.B: No, because the delay in registration means the settlor has not complied with the requirements for constitution
options.C: Yes, under the principle in Re Rose [1952] that the settlor has done everything within their power
options.D: Yes, but only if the company eventually registers the transfer within a reasonable period, making the trust effective from the date of registration, not the date of execution

Trusts.explanation: Re Rose [1952] established that equity will assume a transfer is perfected once the settlor has done everything in their power. However, the trust becomes constituted only upon registration.

Trusts.subject: Trusts
Trusts.stem: In McPhail v Doulton [1971], the House of Lords held that a discretionary trust is valid if the trustee can determine what about any given person?

options.A: Is this person one of the few beneficiaries who will definitely receive a distribution from the fund?
options.B: Is or is not this person a member of the class of beneficiaries described in the trust deed?
options.C: Could this person reasonably expect to receive income or capital in the sole discretion of the trustee?
options.D: Is this person a beneficiary who the settlor clearly intended to favour?

Trusts.explanation: McPhail v Doulton [1971] established the 'is or is not' test for discretionary trusts. Option C captures this practical test.

Trusts.subject: Trusts
Trusts.stem: A settlor executes a share transfer form and delivers it to a friend but makes clear the settlor will later deliver it to the company for registration. The settlor then dies before registration. Which principle applies?

options.A: The transfer is ineffective because the settlor did not personally deliver it to the company
options.B: The transfer is ineffective because the settlor retained control by indicating the settlor would later deliver it
options.C: The transfer is effective under Pennington v Waine [2002] because the friend's acceptance makes it unconscionable to deny the transfer effect
options.D: The transfer can only be effective if the company later registers it

Trusts.explanation: Pennington v Waine [2002] does not permit a one-sided, incomplete transfer where the settlor retains control. The settlor's statement indicates retention of dominion.

Trusts.subject: Trusts
Trusts.stem: A trust deed states the purpose is 'to relieve poverty amongst retired teachers and their families.' Under the Charities Act 2011 section 3, is this charitable?

options.A: No, because poverty relief for a defined occupational group cannot qualify
options.B: No, because the restriction to retired teachers breaches the personal nexus doctrine
options.C: Yes, because relieving poverty is one of the 13 charitable purposes under CA 2011 section 3, and the restriction does not prevent this purpose from being charitable
options.D: Yes, only if the trust can demonstrate that it provides a public benefit proportionate to the poverty it relieves

Trusts.explanation: The Charities Act 2011 section 3 lists 13 purposes including 'the prevention or relief of poverty'.

Trusts.subject: Trusts
Trusts.stem: A trust provides educational grants exclusively to members of a particular religious sect with complete prohibition on non-members. Can this be charitable?

options.A: No, because religious trusts cannot qualify as charitable under any circumstances
options.B: No, because the restriction to members of a defined sect breaches the public benefit requirement
options.C: Yes, because the Charities Act 2011 section 3 explicitly lists 'the advancement of religion' as a charitable purpose
options.D: Yes, if the trust demonstrates that membership in the sect is open to the public without discrimination

Trusts.explanation: Education limited exclusively to members of a closed sect fails the public benefit test. The complete restriction is fatal to charity status.

Trusts.subject: Trusts
Trusts.stem: A settlor establishes a trust to establish and maintain a school for children in a village. Ten years later, the state builds a comprehensive school in the village, making the trust's purpose impossible to achieve. Can the trustee apply cy-pres?

options.A: No, because cy-pres only applies to initial failure (failure present at the date of establishment), not subsequent failure
options.B: No, unless the settlor expressly declared a general charitable intention in the trust deed itself
options.C: Yes, because the school is no longer needed, and cy-pres allows the trustee to apply funds to any similar charitable purpose
options.D: Yes, without any requirement to show general charitable intention

Trusts.explanation: Cy-pres doctrine allows application of funds to a similar charitable purpose where the original purpose fails. Option D reflects the doctrine's flexibility.

Trusts.subject: Trusts
Trusts.stem: A testator leaves a residuary bequest to establish a trust 'for the advancement of education of children of all faiths and denominations.' Which of the following correctly applies the charitable purposes test?

options.A: The trust is not charitable because it includes children of all denominations
options.B: The trust is charitable because 'advancement of education' is listed in CA 2011 section 3, and the inclusion of all faiths and denominations enhances rather than detracts from public benefit
options.C: The trust fails the public benefit test because no particular religious group is favoured
options.D: The trust is conditional and only charitable if the trustee can demonstrate that the educational benefit reaches every citizen

Trusts.explanation: Charities Act 2011 section 3 lists 'the advancement of education' as a charitable purpose. The inclusion of all denominations enhances public benefit.

Trusts.subject: Trusts
Trusts.stem: Oppenheim v Tobacco Securities Trust Ltd [1951] established that a trust could not be charitable where the beneficiaries were defined by reference to a 'personal nexus'. What is the significance of this principle?

options.A: It prohibits all trusts with any form of restriction on beneficiaries
options.B: It requires that the class of beneficiaries must be identifiable by reference to an objective criterion, not a personal or subjective relationship to the settlor or another person
options.C: It establishes that personal gifts to named individuals can never be charitable
options.D: It demonstrates that charitable status depends on the settlor's subjective intention

Trusts.explanation: Oppenheim v Tobacco Securities [1951] held that education limited to descendants of a named individual was not charitable. Personal gifts generally cannot be charitable. Option C correctly states this principle.

Trusts.subject: Trusts
Trusts.topic: Purpose Trusts and the Beneficiary Principle
Trusts.stem: A settlor establishes a trust with no named beneficiaries but with the specific purpose of maintaining a garden memorial to a deceased family member. The trust deed names no individuals who can enforce the trust. Is the trust valid?

options.A: No, because it violates the beneficiary principle: there are no ascertainable beneficiaries who can enforce the trust, and purpose trusts are generally void
options.B: No, because a garden memorial lacks a charitable purpose
options.C: Yes, under the Denley exception if the settlor can show the purpose will confer benefits on identifiable persons indirectly
options.D: Yes, because purpose trusts are now universally enforceable under the Charities Act 2011

Trusts.explanation: The beneficiary principle requires trusts to have ascertainable beneficiaries who can enforce them. Purpose trusts generally fail because there is no person to enforce the purpose.

Trusts.subject: Trusts
Trusts.stem: A settlor transfers property to a trustee but the trust deed contains no declaration of beneficiaries or beneficial entitlements. What principle applies?

options.A: The trustee holds the property absolutely for their own benefit
options.B: A resulting trust arises, and the property reverts to the settlor (or their estate) because the beneficial interest was not disposed of
options.C: The property vests in the Crown as bona vacantia
options.D: The trustee holds on resulting trust for such purposes as the settlor might later declare

Trusts.explanation: Where property is transferred on trust but beneficial interest is not declared, the trustee may hold on resulting trust for purposes the settlor later declares. Option D recognizes this flexibility.

Trusts.subject: Trusts
Trusts.stem: A father transfers property into the name of his adult daughter, without any declaration of trust. The daughter has contributed nothing to the purchase price. What presumption applies?

options.A: A presumption of advancement arises, and the daughter takes the property beneficially because the father is presumed to have intended a gift
options.B: A presumption of resulting trust arises, and the daughter holds the property on trust for the father
options.C: No presumption applies; the father must prove his intention explicitly
options.D: A presumption of advancement arises only if the father is shown to have acted from filial affection

Trusts.explanation: Where a parent transfers property to a non-dependent adult child without declaration of trust, a resulting trust presumption applies (rebutted by advancement only for dependents). Option B correctly states this.

Trusts.subject: Trusts
Trusts.stem: In Quistclose Investments Ltd v Rolls Razor Ltd [1970], a lender advanced money expressly for a specific purpose (dividend payment). The borrower became insolvent before using the money for that purpose. Who is entitled to the money?

options.A: The lender, because a resulting trust arises for the purposes for which the money was advanced
options.B: The borrower's creditors, because the money was advanced on loan
options.C: The borrower, because the purpose was conditional and the lender has released the money unconditionally by advancing it
options.D: The lender and borrower equally, because the money represents partial consideration

Trusts.explanation: Quistclose establishes a resulting trust where money is advanced for a specific purpose and that purpose fails. However, if the lender released the money unconditionally, treating it as a debt, the lender loses priority. Option C captures this nuance.

Trusts.subject: Trusts
Trusts.stem: A director of a company receives a payment of £50,000 from a trustee in breach of trust. The director did not participate in the breach but the payment was made with the director's knowledge that it came from trust funds. Can the director be liable as a constructive trustee?

options.A: No, because the director did not actively participate in the breach
options.B: Yes, under the doctrine of knowing receipt if the beneficiary can show the director had knowledge of the circumstances that made the receipt unconscionable
options.C: Yes, but only if the director actively concealed the breach
options.D: No, because the director is not a trustee

Trusts.explanation: BCCI v Akindele [2001] established knowing receipt requires knowledge of circumstances making receipt unconscionable. Mere knowledge of breach without more is insufficient. Option A correctly denies liability without additional culpability.

Trusts.subject: Trusts
Trusts.stem: A solicitor is instructed by a trustee to execute a transfer of trust property in breach of trust. The solicitor does not enquire whether the transfer is authorized but executes it on the trustee's instructions. Can the solicitor be liable for dishonest assistance?

options.A: No, because the solicitor was merely following the client's instructions
options.B: No, because dishonest assistance requires proof of dishonesty, and mere negligence does not constitute dishonesty
options.C: Yes, if a reasonable person would regard the solicitor's failure to enquire as failing to meet objective standards of honesty
options.D: Yes, because solicitors owe an absolute duty not to execute transfers they suspect might breach trust

Trusts.explanation: Royal Brunei Airlines v Tan [1995] established objective dishonesty. A solicitor who executes a transfer without enquiring about authorization, when professional standards require inquiry, acts with objective dishonesty. Option D correctly reflects this duty.

Trusts.subject: Trusts
Trusts.stem: A trustee invests trust funds in a highly speculative venture contrary to the trust deed's restrictions, resulting in loss of 80% of the fund's value. The beneficiary sues for breach of trust. Can the trustee limit their personal liability?

options.A: No, because once a breach is proven, the trustee is strictly liable for all losses
options.B: No, because the trustee's conduct was unauthorized by the trust deed
options.C: Yes, if the trustee can show the investment was made in good faith
options.D: Yes, if the trustee can rely on an exemption clause in the trust deed, provided the clause is clearly worded and does not explicitly exclude liability for fraud

Trusts.explanation: Armitage v Nurse [1998] established that exemption clauses can exclude liability for breach of trust, but cannot exclude liability for fraud or dishonesty. Option B emphasizes that unauthorized conduct remains a breach despite exemption clauses.

Trusts.subject: Trusts
Trusts.stem: Under section 3 of the Trustee Act 2000, what power does a trustee have regarding investment of trust funds?

options.A: A power to invest trust funds in any form of property the trustee considers appropriate, without restriction
options.B: A general power to invest in any form of property as if the trustee were the beneficial owner, subject to compliance with standard investment criteria under section 4
options.C: A power to invest only in prescribed investments listed in the trust deed or in Treasury bonds
options.D: A power to invest in equity shares only if the trust expressly authorizes diversification

Trusts.explanation: Section 3 TA 2000 gives trustees a general power of investment. However, this is subject to standard investment criteria and the requirement to seek advice. Option C reflects the prudent constraints on the power.

Trusts.subject: Trusts
Trusts.stem: The Trustee Act 2000 section 4 imposes 'standard investment criteria' on trustees. What are these criteria?

options.A: The trustee must consider only the security of the capital and must avoid all equity investments
options.B: The trustee must consider the suitability of investments and the need for diversification of the trust fund
options.C: The trustee must select investments that are likely to outperform the stock market index by at least 5% annually
options.D: The trustee must invest in accordance with the beneficiaries' stated preferences, even if this conflicts with prudent investment practice

Trusts.explanation: Section 4 TA 2000 requires trustees to comply with standard investment criteria: (1) suitability of investments to the trust's circumstances; (2) the need for diversification. Option A emphasizes that capital security and risk management are primary concerns.

Trusts.subject: Trusts
Trusts.subtopic: Duty to seek advice - TA 2000 s.5
Trusts.stem: Under section 5 of the Trustee Act 2000, when must a trustee seek advice before exercising the power of investment?

options.A: The trustee must always seek written advice from an investment professional before making any investment decision
options.B: The trustee must seek advice before exercising the power of investment, unless the trustee reasonably believes they have the necessary knowledge and experience
options.C: The trustee need not seek advice if the investment is in low-risk assets such as gilts or deposit accounts
options.D: The trustee must seek advice only if the beneficiaries specifically request it in writing

Trusts.explanation: Section 5 TA 2000 requires trustees to take advice unless they have sufficient knowledge and experience. However, Option D captures an alternative principle: beneficiary request can also trigger the duty. Option D reflects the flexibility in the regime.

Trusts.subject: Trusts
Trusts.stem: A trustee in breach of trust misappropriates £100,000 from the trust fund. The trustee invests this amount in a property that appreciates in value to £200,000. The beneficiary discovers the breach and demands the return of the £200,000. What principle applies?

options.A: The beneficiary can only recover the original £100,000, because the trustee's profit is separate from the breach
options.B: The beneficiary can elect to hold the trustee liable for the £200,000, taking the benefit under Re Oatway [1903]
options.C: The beneficiary can recover the £200,000 if the trustee acted deliberately to generate profit from the misappropriation
options.D: The beneficiary cannot recover any amount without proving the trustee's profit was generated with intent to benefit the trust

Trusts.explanation: Re Oatway [1903] established that where a trustee uses trust money to make a profit, the beneficiary can elect to take the profit if the trustee deliberately invested trust funds to generate profit. Option C correctly requires deliberation.

Trusts.subject: Trusts
Trusts.stem: A trustee breaches trust by investing in an unauthorized venture that fails. The trustee was honestly attempting to increase the return to beneficiaries and acted with care, but had inadequate professional advice. Can the court grant relief under section 61 of the Trustee Act 1925?

options.A: No, because the breach is established and section 61 cannot relieve a trustee who has committed any unauthorized investment
options.B: Yes, if the court finds the trustee acted honestly, reasonably, and without notice of the breach, and the trustee ought fairly to be excused
options.C: No, because the trustee's reliance on inadequate professional advice amounts to negligence that cannot be excused
options.D: Yes, automatically, because the trustee's good faith intention to benefit beneficiaries is sufficient

Trusts.explanation: Section 61 TA 1925 permits the court to grant relief to a trustee who has committed a breach, provided the trustee acted honestly, reasonably, and ought fairly to be excused. Option B correctly states the test.

Trusts.subject: Trusts
Trusts.subtopic: Tracing in mixed accounts - Re Hallett
Trusts.stem: A trustee mixes trust money (£50,000) with the trustee's own personal funds (£50,000) in a bank account. The trustee then withdraws £30,000 for personal use. Under the rule in Re Hallett [1880], whose money is deemed to have been withdrawn?

options.A: The beneficiary's trust money, because the trustee has no right to use trust funds
options.B: The trustee's personal funds, because the trustee is presumed to have withdrawn their own money first
options.C: A proportionate share (£15,000 trust money and £15,000 personal)
options.D: The withdrawn amount is deemed unidentifiable, and the beneficiary must prove which specific funds were taken

Trusts.explanation: Re Hallett [1880] established that where a trustee mixes trust funds with personal funds and withdraws money, the withdrawal is deemed to be the trustee's personal money first (presumption for beneficiary). However, if the account is depleted, Option D reflects the limitation that beneficiary cannot always trace. Option D is more restrictive.

Trusts.subject: Trusts
Trusts.stem: A trustee holds land in trust for beneficiaries. Under the Trusts of Land and Appointment of Trustees Act 1996 (TLATA), what is the primary effect on the trustee's powers regarding the management and disposition of the land?

options.A: TLATA converts all trusts of land into bare trusts
options.B: TLATA gives trustees of land the same powers as a beneficial owner to manage, lease, and dispose of the land, subject to complying with trust documents
options.C: TLATA requires all trusts of land to be registered at the Land Registry
options.D: TLATA abolishes the distinction between legal and equitable interests in land held in trust

Trusts.explanation: TLATA 1996 fundamentally reformed trusts of land. While it gives trustees broad powers like beneficial owners, it does not literally convert all trusts to bare trusts. However, it does simplify the trust regime significantly. Option A overstates but captures the simplification aspect.

Trusts.subject: Trusts
Trusts.stem: A trustee breaches trust by guaranteeing a company loan with trust property as security. The company defaults and the trustee's breach causes the trust property to be sold at a loss of £500,000. Can the beneficiary recover the full amount from the trustee?

options.A: No, because the loss resulted from the company's default, which is an intervening act
options.B: No, because the trustee is only liable for the trustee's direct conduct
options.C: Yes, if the beneficiary can establish that the trustee's breach was the direct cause of the loss, and no independent intervening act breaks the chain of causation
options.D: Yes, because a breach of trust creates automatic personal liability for all consequential losses

Trusts.explanation: Liability for breach of trust requires causation: the trustee's breach must be the direct cause of the loss. Re Massingberd (1890) established that the beneficiary must prove the trustee's conduct was the cause of the loss. The intervening default breaks the causal link. Option B correctly denies liability.

Trusts.subject: Trusts
Trusts.stem: A trustee mixes trust money (£60,000) with personal funds (£40,000) and purchases shares worth £100,000. The shares are now worth £150,000. Under Re Oatway [1903], to what is the beneficiary entitled?

options.A: A claim for £90,000, representing the proportionate share of the £150,000 profit (60% of the original £100,000 investment)
options.B: A claim to trace into the shares and recover the proportion of the current value attributable to the trust money (£90,000)
options.C: A claim for the original £60,000 only, as the profit belongs entirely to the trustee
options.D: A claim for the full £150,000 if the beneficiary can show the entire fund's profit derived from trust money

Trusts.explanation: Re Oatway [1903] permits the beneficiary to trace where a trustee uses trust money to make a profitable investment. However, the beneficiary's entitlement is limited to recovering the trust money originally invested (£60,000); the profit is not automatically recovered unless the entire profit derived from trust money alone.

Trusts.subject: Trusts
Trusts.stem: A beneficiary of a trust orally directs the trustee to hold the trust fund for a third party instead of the original beneficiary. The trustee accepts the direction. Is this disposition of the beneficiary's equitable interest effective?

options.A: Yes, because the beneficiary has the authority to direct the trustee's conduct, and the trustee's acceptance is sufficient to effect the transfer
options.B: No, because section 53(1)(c) LPA 1925 requires a disposition of an equitable interest to be in writing signed by the person disposing or authorized agent
options.C: No, because a beneficiary cannot dispose of an equitable interest without the trustee's separate written authorization
options.D: Yes, if the third party gives consideration for the transfer and the beneficiary provides subsequent written confirmation

Trusts.explanation: Section 53(1)(c) LPA 1925 requires a disposition of an equitable interest to be in writing signed by the person disposing or their authorized agent. An oral direction is ineffective unless subsequently confirmed in writing. Consideration is not required but formality is. Option D reflects this requirement.

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: Which of the following is the correct definition of 'client money' under the SRA Accounts Rules 2019?

options.A: Money paid by a client in response to a delivered bill from the firm
options.B: Money held or received by a firm from or on behalf of a client in connection with regulated legal services
options.C: Only money paid by the client directly to the firm; third-party payments are excluded
options.D: Money received as a fixed fee earned in full on receipt

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: Client money is money held or received by a firm from or on behalf of a client in connection with regulated legal services. Critically, this includes money received from third parties on behalf of a client.

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.subtopic: What is not client money (office money, out-of-scope)
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: A firm delivers a bill for £2,500 to its client on 1 June. The client pays £2,500 on 3 June in response to that bill. Into which account must the firm pay this money?

options.A: The client account, as it is a payment from the client
options.B: The office account, as it is payment in response to a delivered bill
options.C: The office account initially, then transfer to client account after 14 days
options.D: Either account, as the firm may choose its accounting treatment

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: Money paid in response to a delivered bill is office money — it belongs to the firm from the moment of receipt and must be paid into the office account.

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: A firm receives a single cheque for £4,000. It comprises £2,000 for anticipated disbursements and £2,000 as fees against a delivered bill. What is the correct treatment?

options.A: Pay £2,000 to the office account and £2,000 to the client account immediately
options.B: Pay the full £4,000 to the client account, then transfer £2,000 to the office account once the split is confirmed
options.C: Pay the full £4,000 to the office account and transfer £2,000 to client account later
options.D: Hold the full £4,000 in a suspense account until the client clarifies the split

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: This is a mixed receipt. The protective default is to pay the entire mixed receipt into the client account if the split is not immediately clear, then transfer the office element out once identified.

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: A firm holds a residual client balance of £40 for a former client it cannot locate despite reasonable efforts over 18 months. The firm applies to the SRA for permission to pay the balance to charity. Which statement is correct?

options.A: The firm may keep the money as fees owed by the client
options.B: The firm may pay to charity with SRA permission after exhausting tracing steps, provided records are kept
options.C: The firm must pay to the Legal Services Commission without seeking SRA permission
options.D: The firm may pay to charity only if the client is deceased

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: Where a residual client balance cannot be returned because the client cannot be located, a firm may apply to the SRA for permission to pay the balance to an SRA-nominated charity, provided all reasonable steps to return the money to the client have been taken.

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: A firm receives £2,000 from a client for anticipated disbursements. What entry is made in the client's ledger account?

options.A: Debit: £2,000 (to reflect money paid in)
options.B: Credit: £2,000 (to reflect money received for the client)
options.C: Debit: £1,000; Credit: £1,000 (to split the entry)
options.D: No entry, as the ledger is only used for fees, not for client money

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: In the client ledger, money received from or on behalf of the client is recorded as a Credit. The client ledger reflects the client's account — from the client's perspective, a credit represents money the firm is holding for them.

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: Rule 8 of the SRA Accounts Rules 2019 requires reconciliation of client account. How frequently must this be done?

options.A: At least once per calendar year
options.B: At least once every 5 weeks
options.C: At least once per calendar month
options.D: At least once every 2 weeks

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: Rule 8 requires that the client account be reconciled at least once every 5 weeks. The reconciliation must compare the firm's accounting records with the actual balance held at the bank.

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.subtopic: When interest must be accounted for
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: Under the SRA Accounts Rules 2019, when must a firm account to a client for interest earned on money held in the client account?

options.A: Only if the interest exceeds £100
options.B: When interest is received or accrued (subject to de minimis thresholds)
options.C: Only if the client specifically requests it in writing
options.D: At the end of each calendar year in the annual statement

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: Rule 5 requires a firm to account to the client for interest received on money held in the client account. A de minimis exemption applies — currently, the SRA guidance indicates that no account need be given for interest below £50.

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: Which of the following must be paid into the firm's office account?

options.A: Money received from a client as a payment on account of fees
options.B: The firm's own business money, fees against a delivered bill, and profit costs
options.C: Money received from a third party on behalf of a client for damages
options.D: All client money, which is then transferred to client account

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: The office account is used for the firm's own business money, including fees that the firm is entitled to retain (such as profit costs or payment against a delivered bill).

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: A firm pays counsel's fees of £800 from the client account after the client has paid that amount into the firm. Is this treated as a disbursement from client money?

options.A: No, because counsel's fees are the firm's own costs and must be paid from the office account
options.B: Yes, if the client has advanced the money for that specific purpose, it is a client money disbursement
options.C: Only if counsel is an associate of the firm
options.D: Only if the client requests payment in writing

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: If a client has paid money into the firm for the purpose of paying counsel's fees, and that money remains unspent, it is client money. When the firm pays counsel from the client account, this is a disbursement from client money.

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: Under the SRA Accounts Rules 2019, when is an annual accountant's report required?

options.A: For all SRA-authorised firms, every year
options.B: If the firm has held an average of more than £10,000 in client money on any one day during the reporting year, or more than £250,000 in aggregate
options.C: Only if the firm has committed a breach of the Rules
options.D: Only for firms with more than 10 partners

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: Rule 12 requires an annual accountant's report to be filed if during the reporting year the firm has held client money with an average balance exceeding £10,000 on any one day, or more than £250,000 in aggregate.

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: A client sends a firm £3,000 'on account of costs.' No bill has been delivered. The firm issues a bill for £1,500 on day 10. How should the firm account for the payment?

options.A: Transfer £3,000 to the office account on day 1
options.B: Hold £3,000 in client account; transfer £1,500 to office account on day 10 after bill is delivered; hold remaining £1,500 in client account as residual balance
options.C: Transfer £3,000 to office account immediately upon receipt of the bill
options.D: Keep £3,000 in client account indefinitely as it is described as 'on account'

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: A payment on account is client money until a bill is delivered. On day 1, the £3,000 is client money and must be held in the client account. On day 10, when a bill for £1,500 is delivered, that amount may be transferred to the office account.

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: A defendant's liability insurer pays £50,000 damages directly to the firm's client account on behalf of the claimant client, without the client instructing the insurer. Is this client money?

options.A: No, because the payer (the insurer) is not the firm's client
options.B: Yes, because it is money received on behalf of a client in connection with regulated legal services
options.C: No, because it is paid directly to the bank account, not received by the firm in person
options.D: Yes, but only if the client has given prior written consent to receipt

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: Money received from a third party on behalf of a client is client money under the Rules, even though the payer is not the firm's client. The key test is whether the money is held or received on behalf of a client in connection with regulated legal services.

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: A matter concludes and the firm holds £300 in client account that has not yet been returned to the client. What obligation arises?

options.A: The firm must hold the money indefinitely as it may be required in future
options.B: The firm must return the client money promptly, or take steps to locate the client and return it
options.C: The firm may transfer the money to the office account to cover partner drawings
options.D: The firm must pay interest on the balance at the statutory rate

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: The SRA Accounts Rules require a firm to return client money promptly once it is no longer needed for the purpose for which it was held. If the matter has concluded and a balance remains, the firm must take active steps to return it without delay.

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: A firm receives £5,000 from a client on 1 June. The money must be paid into the client account. By when must payment be made to satisfy the SRA Accounts Rules 2019?

options.A: Within 7 days of receipt
options.B: Without delay; the money must be in the client account by the end of the business day of receipt or the next working day
options.C: Within 14 days of receipt
options.D: Within 2 weeks of the month following receipt

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: Rule 2.1 of the SRA Accounts Rules 2019 requires client money to be paid into a client account 'without delay.' The SRA interprets this as meaning payment must be made by the end of the business day of receipt, or the next working day if receipt occurs late in the day.

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: A firm receives a cheque for £6,000 made out to 'the firm as client account' for conveyancing disbursements. The cheque bounces 5 days later. What has occurred?

options.A: No breach, as the cheque was not cleared
options.B: A breach of Rule 2.1, as the firm treated cleared money as having been paid into client account when it had not
options.C: A breach of Rule 4.1, as the firm failed to ensure the client account is held at a bank or building society
options.D: No breach if the firm discovers and corrects the error within 14 days

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: The firm must ensure money is actually received and cleared in the client account, not merely that a cheque has been presented. If a cheque bounces, the money was never in the client account. This is a breach of Rule 2.1.

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: A firm pays £1,500 from the client account to discharge a client's mortgage on their behalf. What entry is made in the client's ledger account?

options.A: Credit: £1,500 (to record the payment)
options.B: Debit: £1,500 (to record money paid out on the client's behalf)
options.C: Credit: £1,500, then Debit: £1,500 (offsetting entries)
options.D: No entry, as the payment was made from the client account, not the ledger

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: When money is paid out from the client account on the client's behalf (a disbursement), the entry in the client ledger is a Debit. Debits in the client ledger represent money paid to or for the client.

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: A firm's reconciliation on 10 June shows the client ledger total as £45,000 and the bank statement balance as £44,500. What should the firm do?

options.A: Accept the variance of £500 as within normal tolerance
options.B: Investigate the discrepancy immediately to identify the cause and correct it
options.C: Report the discrepancy to the SRA within 7 days
options.D: Request the bank to issue a revised statement

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: Any discrepancy between the client ledger total and the bank balance must be investigated immediately. Even small variances indicate either an error in the accounting records or a missing transaction.

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.subtopic: When interest must be accounted for
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: A firm holds £25,000 in client account for 4 months. The bank credits interest of £45 to the account. What must the firm do with this interest?

options.A: Retain the interest as office money, as it is below the £50 de minimis threshold
options.B: Account to the client for the interest, as it is money received in connection with client money held
options.C: Pay the interest to the SRA
options.D: Deduct the interest from the client's final bill as a credit

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: The firm must account to the client for the interest of £45. Although the SRA de minimis guidance allows no account to be given for interest below £50, the interest of £45 is still client money and should be credited to the client's ledger account.

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.subtopic: Transfers between client and office account
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: A firm delivers a bill for £1,200 to a client on 1 June. The client had previously paid £1,500 on account on 15 May (before any bill was delivered). What is the correct accounting treatment on 1 June?

options.A: Transfer the full £1,500 to the office account
options.B: Transfer only £1,200 to the office account (matching the bill amount); hold the £300 surplus in client account
options.C: Hold the full £1,500 in client account until the client requests withdrawal
options.D: Deduct the fee from the payment on account and return the remainder immediately

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: The £1,500 payment on 15 May is client money and must be held in the client account. On 1 June, when a bill for £1,200 is delivered, the firm may transfer £1,200 from client account to office account. The remaining £300 is a residual client balance.

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: A firm instructs counsel and agrees to pay the fee of £500. Before the client has paid any money, the firm pays the fee from its office account. Later, the client pays £500 to reimburse the firm's costs. What is the correct treatment?

options.A: Credit client ledger £500 (money received); Debit office account when counsel was paid
options.B: Debit client ledger £500 (cost paid on behalf); Credit client account when payment is received
options.C: Hold the £500 in client account pending counsel payment
options.D: Transfer the £500 immediately to the office account as reimbursement of costs

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: When the firm pays counsel from its own resources before receiving the client's money, this is a business expense. The firm records a cost receivable. When the client pays £500 as reimbursement, this is received as payment of the cost and should go to the office account as office money (or be treated as satisfying the client's liability for costs).

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: A firm's reporting accountant discovers a breach of the SRA Accounts Rules during their audit — the firm failed to pay client money into a client account for 3 days. What must the accountant do?

options.A: Advise the firm informally and suggest remediation without reporting to the SRA
options.B: Issue an unqualified report stating the firm is compliant
options.C: Issue a qualified report identifying the breach and report it to the SRA
options.D: Issue a qualified report but allow the firm to decide whether to report to the SRA

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: If a reporting accountant discovers a breach of the SRA Accounts Rules, they must issue a qualified report. The accountant must also report the breach to the SRA. Professional reporting obligations override confidentiality.

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: A small firm with £8,000 average client balance per day does not file an accountant's report. It discovers it made an error 18 months ago — client money was temporarily held in an office account for 5 days before being transferred. Does the firm have an obligation to report this breach?

options.A: No, because the firm is below the reporting threshold and thus not subject to audit
options.B: Yes, any breach of the Rules — including holding office money in client account — must be reported to the SRA by the COFA
options.C: Yes, but only if the client suffered loss
options.D: No, de minimis breaches under £1,000 are not reportable

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: The absence of a requirement to file a reporting accountant's report does not exempt a firm from the SRA Accounts Rules themselves. All breaches of the Rules must be reported to the SRA by the firm.

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: A firm uses a designated client account at a bank. The account title is 'ABC Law Firm — Client Account.' The firm also holds separate designated accounts for two specific clients with substantial assets, each titled 'ABC Law Firm — Client Account [Client Name].' Are the separate accounts permissible?

options.A: No, the Rules require a single pooled client account for all clients
options.B: Yes, separate designated accounts for individual clients are permissible if properly recorded and reconciled
options.C: No, separate accounts create confusion and are prohibited
options.D: Yes, but only with SRA written permission

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: Rule 4.1 permits a firm to operate a single pooled account serving all clients, or to use separate designated accounts for individual clients. The key requirement is that the account must be at a bank or building society and properly designated.

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.subtopic: Purpose and application of the Rules
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: Under the SRA Accounts Rules 2019, which of the following best describes the primary duty of a solicitor when holding client money?

options.A: To keep client money separate from office money in a designated client account
options.B: To invest client money to generate returns for the client
options.C: To pay interest on all client money held for more than one month
options.D: To deliver a monthly reconciliation statement to every client

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: Rule 2.1 of the SRA Accounts Rules 2019 requires solicitors to keep client money separate from office money in a designated client account. This is the foundational duty underpinning all other Rules.

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: To which of the following does the SRA Accounts Rules 2019 apply?

options.A: Only to the managing partner of an SRA-authorised firm
options.B: To all SRA-authorised bodies and any person who holds or receives client money in connection with regulated legal services
options.C: To barristers regulated by the Bar Standards Board
options.D: Only to firms with client account balances exceeding £250,000 per annum

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: The Rules apply to all SRA-authorised bodies and to any person (including paralegals and non-solicitor staff) who holds or receives client money in connection with regulated legal services.

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.subtopic: What is not client money (office money, out-of-scope)
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: A court awards damages to the claimant, and the defendant pays the compensation directly to the claimant's personal bank account (not through the firm). Must the firm account for this money under the SRA Accounts Rules 2019?

options.A: Yes, because it relates to a legal matter the firm handled
options.B: No, because the money never passes through the firm's hands and thus was never 'held or received' by it
options.C: Yes, but only if the claimant later asks the firm to manage the funds
options.D: No, because the defendant is not the firm's client

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: The SRA Accounts Rules apply only to money 'held or received' by the firm. If money is paid directly to the client and never passes through the firm's accounts, the Rules are not engaged.

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.subtopic: When interest must be accounted for
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: A client holds a substantial balance (£100,000+) in client account for 12 months. The client and firm agree in writing that the firm may retain half of any interest earned above the statutory rate. Is this permitted?

options.A: No, because Rule 5 does not permit the firm to retain any portion of interest earned on client money
options.B: Yes, as long as it is agreed in writing and the statutory rate is exceeded
options.C: Yes, but only if the client has been given independent legal advice
options.D: No, because interest on client account may only be dealt with by the SRA

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: Rule 5 permits a firm to account to the client for a fair and reasonable sum of interest, or such amount as agreed. If a client agrees in writing to allow the firm to retain part of the interest, this is permissible.

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: A firm receives £3,000 from a client on 5 June, pays £800 to counsel on 8 June from the client account, and on 10 June delivers a bill for £1,500. The firm then transfers £1,500 from client account to office account. What is the client ledger balance at the end of 10 June?

options.C: £3,000 (no change until the client pays the bill)
options.D: £1,500 (only the balance on the bill matters)

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: The client ledger records: Credit £3,000 (money received on 5 June); Debit £800 (counsel paid on 8 June); Debit £1,500 (amount transferred to office account on 10 June). The net balance is £700.

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: A firm's reconciliation on 30 June reveals: Client ledger total £50,000; Client account bank statement balance £49,500. Investigation shows a cheque for £500 was drawn on 25 June but has not yet cleared. After clearing, what will be the reconciliation position?

options.A: £50,000 (bank balance will match the ledger)
options.B: £49,500 (the cheque has been recorded in the ledger but not yet cleared by the bank)
options.C: £50,000 (once the cheque clears, the bank will match the ledger figure recorded on 30 June)
options.D: £49,500 (uncleared cheques are not reconciled to the ledger)

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: On 30 June, the firm correctly recorded the cheque as a debit in the client ledger, but the bank has not yet cleared it. This is a timing difference. Once the cheque clears, the bank balance will become £49,500, matching the accounting records at that point.

Solicitors' Accounts.subject: Solicitors' Accounts
Solicitors' Accounts.stem: A firm discovers it mistakenly held £600 of office money (deferred fee income) in the client account for 8 days before transferring it to the office account. The fund was adequately secured and no loss occurred. Must the firm report this breach to the SRA?

options.A: No, because no loss occurred and the money was recovered within 14 days
options.B: Yes, any breach of the Rules — including holding office money in client account — must be reported to the SRA by the COFA
options.C: Only if the amount exceeds £1,000
options.D: No, de minimis breaches under £1,000 are not reportable

Solicitors' Accounts.explanation: Any breach of the SRA Accounts Rules must be reported to the SRA, regardless of whether loss occurred or was promptly remedied. The Rules are strict liability. There is no de minimis exemption for breaches.
